topics.title: Synthesis of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate by the acetoacetic ester condensation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Synthesis of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate by the acetoacetic ester condensation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Acetoacetic Ester Condensation** is the classical **Claisen Condensation** of two molecules of ethyl acetate in the presence of a strong base (sodium ethoxide) to yield **ethyl acetoacetate** (ethyl 3-oxobutanoate). The α-carbon of the resulting β-ketoester is sufficiently acidic (pKₐ ≈ **11**) to be deprotonated by the same base, forming a resonance-stabilized **carbanion (enolate)**. This enolate undergoes a **nucleophilic substitution (S_N2)** reaction with an alkyl halide — in this case, **n-butyl bromide** — to furnish the alkylated product, **ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate** (ethyl 2-n-butyl-3-oxobutanoate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board-Examiner Definition (verbatim KTU phrasing):**
> "The self-condensation of ethyl acetate catalysed by sodium ethoxide yields ethyl acetoacetate. Subsequent C-alkylation at the active methylene carbon using n-butyl bromide produces ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Molecular LEGO" Analogy

Imagine you have a **train of two connected carriages** (the ethyl acetoacetate molecule). The **coupling between them** is the *active methylene carbon* (–CH₂–). This coupling is "loose" because the base (sodium ethoxide) acts like a **magnetic key** that pulls the coupling apart, leaving a hook (the enolate carbanion) ready to attach a **new carriage** (the n-butyl group, –C₄H₉).

- **Ethyl acetate** → the basic building block
- **Sodium ethoxide** → the catalyst (the "magnetic key")
- **Active methylene group** → the "coupling hook"
- **n-Butyl bromide** → the "new carriage" being attached
- **Ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate** → the final extended train

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU Board 2024):**
> This experiment tests **CO₂ — Application of synthetic organic principles** and **CO₃ — Quantitative estimation and yield analysis**, mapping to Bloom's cognitive levels **Apply (L3)** and **Analyse (L4)**.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Unit |
|-----------|-------|------|
| Molecular weight of ethyl acetoacetate | **130.14** | g/mol |
| Molecular weight of n-butyl bromide | **137.03** | g/mol |
| Molecular weight of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate | **186.25** | g/mol |
| pKₐ of ethyl acetoacetate | **10.7** | – |
| Boiling point of ethyl acetoacetate | **180.4** | °C |
| Boiling point of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate | **228 – 230** | °C (atm. pressure) |
| Density of n-butyl bromide | **1.276** | g/cm³ |
| Density of ethyl acetate | **0.902** | g/cm³ |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Background — The "Why" Behind Each Step

The acetoacetic ester condensation is a two-stage synthetic strategy:

### **Stage 1: Claisen Condensation (Formation of the β-Ketoester)**
Two equivalents of ethyl acetate react via **nucleophilic acyl substitution**. The α-hydrogen of one ethyl acetate (pKₐ ≈ **25**) is removed by the strongly basic ethoxide ion (conjugate acid pKₐ ≈ **16**), generating a small equilibrium concentration of the enolate. This enolate attacks the carbonyl carbon of a second ethyl acetate molecule, displacing ethoxide. The driving force is the **deprotonation of the acidic α-hydrogen of the product** (pKₐ ≈ 11) by the base, which makes the overall reaction thermodynamically favourable.

### **Stage 2: C-Alkylation at the Active Methylene**
The strongly acidic **active methylene carbon** (–CH₂– flanked by C=O and COOC₂H₅) is deprotonated quantitatively by sodium ethoxide. The resulting **delocalised enolate** (stabilised by both carbonyl and ester groups) is a soft nucleophile that attacks n-butyl bromide in an **S_N2** pathway. The new C–C bond is formed at the α-carbon, giving the alkylated β-ketoester.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why n-Butyl Bromide and not n-Butyl Chloride?**
> Bromide is the preferred leaving group because it is a **better leaving group (larger, more polarisable, weaker C–Br bond of 68 kcal/mol)** than chloride (C–Cl bond of 84 kcal/mol). This accelerates the S_N2 step and improves yield.

## 2.2 Reaction Scheme (Overall)

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1 (Claisen):} \quad 2\,\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \xrightarrow{\text{NaOC}_2\text{H}_5} \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 2 (Alkylation):} \quad \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \xrightarrow{\text{(i) NaOC}_2\text{H}_5 \\ \text{(ii) n-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{Br}} \text{CH}_3\text{COCH(n-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{)COOC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula & Reagent Cheat Sheet

| Component | Formula / Role | Molecular Weight | Key Property | Engineering/Industrial Utility |
|-----------|----------------|------------------|--------------|---------------------------------|
| Ethyl acetate | CH₃COOC₂H₅ — substrate | **88.11** g/mol | bp 77 °C | Solvent in paints, inks, nail polish |
| Sodium metal | Na — base precursor | **22.99** g/mol | stored in kerosene | Strong base generation in situ |
| Absolute ethanol | C₂H₅OH — solvent | **46.07** g/mol | bp 78 °C | Anhydrous reaction medium |
| Sodium ethoxide | C₂H₅ONa — strong base | **68.05** g/mol | hygroscopic | Generated in situ for Claisen |
| n-Butyl bromide | n-C₄H₉Br — alkylating agent | **137.03** g/mol | bp 101 °C | S_N2 electrophile |
| Glacial acetic acid | CH₃COOH — for acidification | **60.05** g/mol | bp 118 °C | Quenches the enolate |
| Ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate | Final product | **186.25** g/mol | bp 228 – 230 °C | Intermediate in pharma synthesis |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Alkylated β-ketoesters are versatile **C-C bond forming intermediates** in the pharmaceutical industry. They are precursors to:
- **Anti-inflammatory drugs** (e.g., ibuprofen-type structures)
- **Heterocyclic compounds** (pyrazoles, pyrimidines, isoxazoles)
- **Fragrance chemistry** (jasmonate analogues)
- **Agrochemicals** (herbicides and insecticides)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Significance:** The active methylene alkylation strategy is foundational to **multi-step organic synthesis (MSOS)** — every synthetic organic chemist uses this chemistry when building complex carbon skeletons.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Reaction Mechanism (with Curved-Arrow Logic)

### **Stage 1: Claisen Condensation Mechanism**

**Step 1.1 — Enolate formation of ethyl acetate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + {}^{-}\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \left[\text{CH}_2{}^{(-)}\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longleftrightarrow \text{CH}_2{=}\text{C(O}^{-}\text{)OC}_2\text{H}_5\right] + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The ethoxide ion abstracts an α-hydrogen (pKₐ ≈ 25). Although this equilibrium lies far to the left, the small concentration of enolate is sufficient to drive the overall reaction.

**Step 1.2 — Nucleophilic addition to a second ethyl acetate molecule:**

The enolate carbanion attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of a second molecule of ethyl acetate.

$$
\begin{aligned}
{}^{-}\text{CH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O}^{-})(\text{CH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5)\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1.3 — Expulsion of ethoxide (collapse of tetrahedral intermediate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{C(O}^{-})(\text{CH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5)\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + {}^{-}\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1.4 — Irreversible deprotonation of the product (driving force):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + {}^{-}\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}^{(-)}\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Explanation:** The product is a **β-ketoester**, whose α-hydrogen has pKₐ ≈ 11 — much more acidic than ethanol (pKₐ ≈ 16). Hence, the deprotonation is **irreversible**, pulling the entire equilibrium toward the product. This is the **thermodynamic driving force** of the Claisen condensation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why one equivalent of base is sufficient:** Each mole of base consumed in Step 1.1 is regenerated in Step 1.4. Therefore, **sodium ethoxide acts as a true catalyst** (in a stoichiometric sense only during the enolate phase).

### **Stage 2: C-Alkylation Mechanism**

**Step 2.1 — Generation of the stabilised enolate of ethyl acetoacetate:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{NaOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}^{(-)}\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5\,\text{Na}^{+} + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.2 — Resonance stabilisation of the enolate (delocalisation over both carbonyls):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{C(O)–CH}^{(-)}\text{–COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longleftrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O}^{-})\text{=CH–COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longleftrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{CO–CH= C(O}^{-})\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.3 — S_N2 attack on n-butyl bromide:**

The carbanionic α-carbon attacks the electrophilic carbon of n-butyl bromide, displacing bromide.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}^{(-)}\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{CH}_3\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{CH}_2\text{Br} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH(n-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{)COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{Br}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2.4 — Acidic workup to liberate the neutral product:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Na}^{+}\text{ enolate} + \text{CH}_3\text{COOH} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH(n-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{)COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{CH}_3\text{COO}^{-}\text{Na}^{+}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.2 Stoichiometric Calculation Walk-Through (Analytical Matrix)

### **3.2.1 Reagent Quantities (Typical KTU Lab Scale)**

Assume a target of **0.10 mol** of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate (target M = 186.25 g/mol → theoretical mass ≈ 18.6 g).

| Reagent | Moles Required | Calculation | Mass / Volume |
|---------|----------------|-------------|----------------|
| Ethyl acetate | 0.20 mol (2× for Claisen) | 0.20 × 88.11 g/mol | **17.6 g** (≈ 19.5 mL) |
| Sodium metal | 0.10 mol (catalytic) | 0.10 × 22.99 g/mol | **2.30 g** |
| Absolute ethanol | ~25 mL | – | **25 mL** |
| n-Butyl bromide | 0.105 mol (5% excess) | 0.105 × 137.03 g/mol | **14.4 g** (≈ 11.3 mL) |
| Glacial acetic acid | 0.15 mol | 0.15 × 60.05 g/mol | **9.0 g** (≈ 8.6 mL) |

### **3.2.2 Theoretical Yield Calculation**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical Yield} &= n_{\text{limiting}} \times M_{\text{product}} \\
&= 0.10\ \text{mol} \times 186.25\ \text{g/mol} \\
&= 18.625\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

### **3.2.3 Percentage Yield Formula**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Yield} = \frac{\text{Actual mass of pure product obtained}}{\text{Theoretical mass}} \times 100
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked Example:**
If the student actually isolates **13.5 g** of pure ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\% \text{ Yield} = \frac{13.5\ \text{g}}{18.625\ \text{g}} \times 100 = 72.48\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected Range:** A good lab yield for this experiment is **60 – 75%**. Below 50% suggests incomplete alkylation or losses during distillation; above 80% is excellent.

## 3.3 Diagnostic / Purity Check — The Iron(III) Chloride Test

A small portion of the product is treated with neutral **FeCl₃ solution**. A **deep wine-red / violet colour** confirms the presence of the **enol form** of the β-ketoester (the enol tautomer chelates Fe³⁺). Absence of colour indicates the product is fully ketonised / pure keto form.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COCH(n-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{)COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \rightleftharpoons \text{CH}_3\text{C(OH)=C(n-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{)COOC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Mechanism of colour:** Six-membered chelate ring formed between the enol –OH and the ester carbonyl oxygen, coordinated to Fe³⁺.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define acetoacetic ester condensation. Write the structure of the product formed when ethyl acetoacetate is alkylated with n-butyl bromide.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Acetoacetic ester condensation is the **Claisen condensation of two molecules of ethyl acetate** in the presence of sodium ethoxide to give **ethyl acetoacetate** (ethyl 3-oxobutanoate, CH₃COCH₂COOC₂H₅). The product possesses a highly acidic **active methylene group** (pKₐ ≈ 11) flanked by the keto and ester carbonyls, which can be deprotonated and alkylated.

When the sodium enolate of ethyl acetoacetate is treated with **n-butyl bromide**, an S_N2 alkylation occurs at the active methylene carbon, yielding:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COCH(n-C}_4\text{H}_9\text{)COOC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

This is **ethyl 2-n-butyl-3-oxobutanoate** (commonly called ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate).

**Mark distribution:** Definition (1) + Reaction context (1) + Structure (1).

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Why is sodium ethoxide preferred over sodium hydroxide in the Claisen condensation of ethyl acetate?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Compatibility of conjugate acid:** Ethoxide is the conjugate base of ethanol (pKₐ = 16), which is also the alcohol formed in the Claisen step. This avoids **trans-esterification** or hydrolysis side-reactions that would occur with hydroxide (pKₐ of water = 15.7, but hydroxide attacks the ester carbonyl of ethyl acetate, causing hydrolysis to acetic acid + ethanol).
2. **Reversibility avoidance:** If NaOH is used, the ester bond of the product would be **hydrolysed**, regenerating the carboxylate and reducing yield.
3. **Solubility in the organic medium:** Sodium ethoxide is soluble in the ethanol/ethyl acetate medium, providing a **homogeneous reaction**; NaOH forms a separate aqueous phase, reducing contact with the organic substrate.

**Mark distribution:** Point 1 (1.5) + Point 2 (1) + Point 3 (0.5).

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe the **mechanism of the Claisen condensation** of ethyl acetate catalysed by sodium ethoxide. Discuss the role of the thermodynamic driving force. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** Outline the **laboratory procedure** for the synthesis of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate from ethyl acetoacetate. Mention the reagents, conditions, and the expected yield. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

The Claisen condensation proceeds in four mechanistic stages:

**Stage 1: Enolate Generation**
Ethoxide ion (a strong base) abstracts an α-hydrogen of ethyl acetate to generate a small equilibrium concentration of the **enolate carbanion**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O}^{-} \rightleftharpoons {}^{-}\text{CH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

This step is endergonic because the α-H of ethyl acetate (pKₐ ≈ 25) is far less acidic than ethanol (pKₐ ≈ 16). Therefore, the equilibrium lies heavily to the **left** at this stage.

**Stage 2: Nucleophilic Attack on a Second Ester Molecule**
The enolate carbanion, although in low concentration, attacks the **electrophilic carbonyl carbon** of a second molecule of ethyl acetate, forming a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate:

$$
\begin{aligned}
{}^{-}\text{CH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{CH}_3\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O}^{-})(\text{CH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5)\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 3: Expulsion of Ethoxide**
The tetrahedral intermediate collapses, expelling **ethoxide** as the leaving group and forming the β-ketoester:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{C(O}^{-})(\text{CH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5)\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5 \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O}^{-}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Stage 4: Irreversible Deprotonation of the Product (THE DRIVING FORCE)**
The newly formed **ethyl acetoacetate** has an α-hydrogen with pKₐ ≈ 11, which is much more acidic than ethanol (pKₐ ≈ 16). Therefore, ethoxide quantitatively deprotonates the product:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_2\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{O}^{-} \longrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH}^{-}\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 + \text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Why this is the driving force:** The product β-ketoester is stabilised by **resonance delocalisation** of the resulting enolate over **two electron-withdrawing carbonyl groups** (keto C=O and ester C=O). This extra stabilisation means the product deprotonation is **irreversible** and **exergonic**, pulling all prior equilibria forward. **Without this step, the Claisen condensation would be reversible and the yield would be negligible.**

**Mark distribution:** Stage 1 (1) + Stage 2 (1.5) + Stage 3 (1.5) + Stage 4 + driving force (3).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Reagents & Glassware:**
- Ethyl acetoacetate (0.10 mol, 13.0 g)
- Sodium ethoxide (0.10 mol, 6.8 g) **or** sodium metal (0.10 mol, 2.3 g) dissolved in absolute ethanol
- n-Butyl bromide (0.105 mol, 14.4 g)
- Dry toluene (50 mL, as solvent)
- Glacial acetic acid (10 mL) for workup
- Anhydrous CaCl₂ for drying
- 250-mL two-necked round-bottom flask, reflux condenser, dropping funnel, separating funnel, distillation assembly

**Procedure:**

1. **Setup:** Assemble a 250-mL two-necked flask equipped with a reflux condenser (top fitted with CaCl₂ guard tube) and a dropping funnel.
2. **Formation of enolate:** Charge the flask with ethyl acetoacetate (13.0 g) in dry toluene (50 mL). Add sodium ethoxide (6.8 g) in small portions over 10 minutes with stirring under anhydrous conditions.
3. **Stir:** Continue stirring at **room temperature (25 °C) for 15 minutes** to ensure complete enolate formation (a thick yellow suspension forms).
4. **Alkylation:** Add n-butyl bromide (14.4 g, 11.3 mL) **dropwise over 20 minutes** through the dropping funnel. Mild exotherm may be observed.
5. **Reflux:** Reflux the reaction mixture at **110 °C for 4 hours** with continuous stirring. Sodium bromide precipitates as a white solid.
6. **Workup:** Cool the mixture to room temperature. **Filter off NaBr** by suction filtration through a Buchner funnel. Wash the filtrate with water (2 × 25 mL) to remove sodium salts.
7. **Drying:** Dry the organic layer over **anhydrous CaCl₂** for 30 minutes. Gravity-filter to remove the drying agent.
8. **Distillation:** Distil the crude product under **reduced pressure (12 mmHg)**. Collect the fraction boiling at **112 – 115 °C / 12 mmHg** (corresponding to 228 – 230 °C at 760 mmHg).
9. **Weighing & Yield:** Weigh the pure ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate. Calculate the percentage yield.

**Expected yield:** **60 – 75%** of theoretical (≈ 11.2 – 14.0 g from 0.10 mol of starting ester).

**Purity check:** The product gives a **positive FeCl₃ test** (wine-red colour) confirming the enol form of the β-ketoester.

**Mark distribution:** Reagents (1) + Setup (1) + Enolate formation (1) + Alkylation (1) + Reflux conditions (1) + Workup (1) + Yield & purity (1).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Explain the **acidity of the active methylene group** in ethyl acetoacetate. How is this property exploited in the synthesis of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate? **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A student performed the synthesis using **13.0 g of ethyl acetoacetate** and obtained **11.5 g of pure ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate**. Calculate the **percentage yield** and discuss two common sources of error that could reduce the yield. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (a)**

**Acidity of the active methylene group:**

The –CH₂– group of ethyl acetoacetate is positioned between two electron-withdrawing groups: a **ketone (C=O)** and an **ester (COOC₂H₅)**. Its α-hydrogen (pKₐ ≈ 10.7) is far more acidic than:
- The α-hydrogen of ethyl acetate (pKₐ ≈ 25)
- The α-hydrogen of acetone (pKₐ ≈ 19.3)
- Ethanol (pKₐ ≈ 16)

**Reasons for enhanced acidity:**

1. **Resonance stabilisation of the conjugate base:** When the α-hydrogen is removed, the resulting **carbanion is delocalised over both carbonyl groups**, forming a resonance-stabilised enolate. The negative charge is shared between the ketone oxygen and the ester oxygen:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}^{(-)}\text{COOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longleftrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{C(O}^{-})\text{=CHCOOC}_2\text{H}_5 \longleftrightarrow \text{CH}_3\text{COCH=C(O}^{-})\text{OC}_2\text{H}_5
\end{aligned}
$$

2. **Inductive effect:** The two adjacent carbonyls exert a –I effect, stabilising the negative charge inductively.

3. **The two carbonyls are equivalent contributors:** The combined resonance + inductive effect lowers the pKₐ by ~15 units compared to a simple ketone.

**Exploitation in synthesis:**

This enhanced acidity means a **mild base like sodium ethoxide (pKₐ of conjugate acid = 16)** is sufficient to **completely deprotonate** the active methylene carbon. The resulting enolate is a soft, carbon-centred nucleophile that selectively attacks the **electrophilic α-carbon of n-butyl bromide** in an **S_N2 reaction**, forming a new C–C bond. This is the central strategy of **active methylene alkylation** in organic synthesis.

**Mark distribution:** pKₐ values & comparison (2) + Resonance structures (2) + Inductive effect (1) + Synthetic exploitation (2).

---

#### **Model Solution — Part (b)**

**Given:**
- Mass of ethyl acetoacetate used = 13.0 g
- Molecular weight of ethyl acetoacetate = 130.14 g/mol
- Actual mass of pure ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate obtained = 11.5 g
- Molecular weight of ethyl n-butyl acetoacetate = 186.25 g/mol

**Step 1: Moles of limiting reagent (ethyl acetoacetate):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n &= \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Molar mass}} = \frac{13.0\ \text{g}}{130.14\ \text{g/mol}} = 0.0999\ \text{mol} \approx 0.10\ \text{mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Theoretical yield of product (assuming 1:1 stoichiometry):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Theoretical mass} &= n \times M_{\text{product}} \\
&= 0.10\ \text{mol} \times 186.25\ \text{g/mol} \\
&= 18.625\ \text{g}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Percentage yield:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\ \text{Yield} = \frac{\text{Actual mass}}{\text{Theoretical mass}} \times 100 = \frac{11.5}{18.625} \times 100 = 61.74\ \%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Two common sources of error:**

1. **Incomplete alkylation / formation of dialkylated product:** If the enolate is not formed quantitatively (e.g., traces of moisture hydrolyse the sodium ethoxide), some unreacted ethyl acetoacetate remains. Alternatively, **over-alkylation** can occur if a second equivalent of base and n-butyl bromide is present, giving **ethyl 2,2-di-n-butyl acetoacetate** as a side-product, reducing the yield of the mono-alkylated product.
2. **Mechanical losses during distillation:** The product boils at 228 – 230 °C, near the upper limit of ordinary distillation. If the receiver is not pre-heated, or if the fraction is collected too widely, **forerun (unreacted ester) and residue losses** can each account for 10 – 15% loss of product.

(Other acceptable sources: incomplete drying of glassware leading to hydrolysis of the enolate; loss during extraction with ether; oxidation of the enolate by atmospheric oxygen if the reaction is not under N₂.)

**Mark distribution:** Step 1 (1) + Step 2 (1.5) + Step 3 (1.5) + Two sources of error (1.5 + 1.5).

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones — Read Carefully Before Writing Your Exam:**
>
> 1. **Mechanism arrows:** Students frequently forget the curved arrow from the C–H bond to the carbonyl carbon (when generating the enolate). The base must take the H⁺ **and** the bonding electrons must go to form the C=C. Half-drawn arrows = **zero marks** for that step.
>
> 2. **Confusing the active methylene carbon with the carbonyl carbon:** The alkylation occurs at the **–CH₂– (α-carbon)**, not at the carbonyl carbon. Drawing the butyl group on the oxygen or the carbonyl is an **instant 0.5-mark deduction** in the structure.
>
> 3. **Forgetting the "irreversible deprotonation" step:** The Claisen condensation will appear thermodynamically impossible without the final deprotonation. Examiners specifically look for the phrase **"the product β-ketoester has pKₐ ≈ 11, much more acidic than ethanol, making this step irreversible"**. Omitting it loses 2 marks.
>
> 4. **Wrong base in Claisen:** Writing **NaOH** instead of **NaOEt** is a common error. NaOH hydrolyses the ester and reduces the yield. Examiners deduct marks.
>
> 5. **Yield calculation unit mismatch:** Using the molar mass of the **alkyl halide** instead of the **product** in Step 2 of the yield calculation. The theoretical yield must be based on the **product's** molar mass.
>
> 6. **Reporting yield > 100%:** This indicates an arithmetic or impurity error. Examiners will mark such answers as wrong.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reaction type:** Claisen condensation (Stage 1) followed by C-alkylation (Stage 2).
- **Catalyst/Base:** Sodium ethoxide, generated in situ from sodium metal + absolute ethanol.
- **Active methylene acidity:** pKₐ ≈ 11 — drives the Claisen equilibrium forward.
- **Enolate is resonance-stabilised** over both the keto and ester carbonyls.
- **Alkylating agent:** n-Butyl bromide (primary, S_N2-favourable).
- **S_N2 mechanism:** Carbanion attacks the back-side of the C–Br bond; inversion of configuration (n-butyl is achiral, so irrelevant here).
- **Product IUPAC name:** Ethyl 2-n-butyl-3-oxobutanoate.
- **Molecular weight of product:** **186.25 g/mol**.
- **Boiling point of product:** **228 – 230 °C (760 mmHg) / 112 – 115 °C (12 mmHg)**.
- **Diagnostic test:** **FeCl₃** → wine-red colour (enol chelate).
- **Workup sequence:** Acidify (acetic acid) → Extract (ether) → Dry (CaCl₂) → Distil.
- **Expected yield range:** **60 – 75%**.
- **Why NaOEt and not NaOH?** To avoid ester hydrolysis and trans-esterification.
- **Why dry conditions?** Traces of water hydrolyse sodium ethoxide and protonate the enolate, killing the reaction.
- **Driving force of Claisen:** Irreversible deprotonation of the β-ketoester product (pKₐ = 11).
- **Key intermediate to memorise:** **CH₃COCH⁻COOC₂H₅ Na⁺** (the sodium enolate of ethyl acetoacetate).
- **Real-world utility:** Foundational to multi-step organic synthesis; used in pharmaceuticals, fragrances, agrochemicals.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Synthesis of 3-nitrobenzoic acid from benzoic acid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Synthesis of 3-nitrobenzoic acid from benzoic acid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Nitration of Benzoic Acid** is a classic laboratory-scale **Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS)** reaction in which the carboxyl group (–COOH) of benzoic acid is replaced (in a positional sense) by a nitro group (–NO₂) at the **meta position**, yielding **3-nitrobenzoic acid** (also called *m*-nitrobenzoic acid) as the major product.

The reaction employs a **nitrating mixture** (a combination of concentrated **HNO₃** and concentrated **H₂SO₄**) that generates the active electrophile, the **nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)**.

The balanced overall equation is:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH} + \text{HNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{conc. H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{O}_2\text{N–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PCPOL307 – Module 2):**
> This experiment is one of the **core synthesis-based experiments** in the chemistry lab. It tests a student's ability to perform (i) a multi-step organic synthesis, (ii) recrystallization for purification, (iii) melting-point determination, and (iv) percentage yield calculation.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the benzene ring of benzoic acid as a **round dining table with six chairs**. The –COOH group is one diner who is **very greedy and demanding** — it pulls electron density toward itself (it is *electron-withdrawing*). The other diners (hydrogen atoms) become "less attractive" to a hungry electrophile looking for a seat with a free electron-rich cushion.

Because the –COOH group sits at position 1, the three positions directly next to it (**ortho** = 2 and 6) and directly opposite on one side (**para** = 4) are **uncomfortable** — too electron-poor. However, the seats **meta** (positions 3 and 5) are slightly more tolerable, so the electrophile (NO₂⁺) prefers to sit there. That is why the product is **3-nitrobenzoic acid**, not the ortho or para isomer.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants (Bolded)

- **Molecular weight of benzoic acid (C₆H₅COOH): 122.12 g/mol**
- **Molecular weight of 3-nitrobenzoic acid (O₂NC₆H₄COOH): 167.12 g/mol**
- **Molecular weight of HNO₃: 63.01 g/mol**
- **Density of concentrated HNO₃: ≈ 1.42 g/mL**
- **Density of concentrated H₂SO₄: ≈ 1.84 g/mL**
- **Melting point of pure 3-nitrobenzoic acid: 140–142 °C**
- **Melting point of benzoic acid (starting material): 122 °C**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition Callout — Electrophile & Nitronium Ion:**
> An **electrophile** is an electron-loving, positively polarized (or positively charged) species that seeks electron-rich sites. The **nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)** is the active electrophile in this nitration, generated in situ from HNO₃ + H₂SO₄.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The 'Why' — Why Meta, Not Ortho or Para?

The –COOH group is an **electron-withdrawing group (EWG)** through both **inductive (–I)** and **resonance (–M / –R)** effects. It deactivates the ring toward electrophilic attack.

- **Inductive effect (–I):** The carbonyl carbon is electron-deficient; it pulls σ-electrons through the bonds.
- **Resonance effect (–M):** Lone-pair donation from the ring into the C=O is impossible (oxygen is more electronegative than carbon, so the reverse occurs), and instead the C=O π-system withdraws π-electrons from the ring, especially destabilizing the ortho and para positions.

When the electrophile attacks **ortho or para**, the resulting sigma-complex (arenium ion / Wheland intermediate) places a **positive charge directly on the carbon bearing the –COOH group**. Since –COOH is already electron-withdrawing, this creates a **highly unstable, doubly electron-deficient intermediate**.

When attack occurs at the **meta position**, the positive charge in the intermediate is **never placed on the ipso carbon (C-1)**; therefore, the destabilization is much less severe. Hence, **meta substitution is kinetically and thermodynamically favored**.

### 2.2 The 'How' — Generation of the Nitronium Ion

Concentrated **H₂SO₄** is a stronger acid than HNO₃. It protonates HNO₃, which then loses a water molecule to give the linear nitronium cation:

$$\text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ + \text{HSO}_4^-$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

The **nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)** is the active electrophile that attacks the benzene ring.

### 2.3 Reaction Mechanism (3 Steps)

1. **Electrophilic attack (rate-determining step):** NO₂⁺ attacks the aromatic ring, forming a resonance-stabilized **arenium ion (sigma-complex / Wheland intermediate)**.
2. **Deprotonation:** The HSO₄⁻ base removes the proton from the sp³ carbon of the intermediate, restoring aromaticity.
3. **Product formation:** The neutral, aromatic **3-nitrobenzoic acid** is liberated.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Formula / Expression | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| Theoretical moles of product | $n_{\text{product}} = n_{\text{limiting reagent}}$ | mol |
| Limiting reagent check | Compare $n_{\text{benzoic acid}}$ vs $n_{\text{HNO}_3}$ | mol |
| Theoretical mass of product | $m_{\text{theo}} = n_{\text{product}} \times M_{\text{product}}$ | grams |
| Percentage yield | $\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % |
| Mass from volume (liquid) | $m = \rho \times V$ | grams (ρ in g/mL) |
| Moles from mass | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | mol |
| Meta-directing groups | –COOH, –NO₂, –CN, –CHO, –COR, –SO₃H | All are –M, deactivating |
| Electrophile in nitration | NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion) | Linear, isoelectronic with CO₂ |
| Active nitrating agent | Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄ | Called "nitrating mixture" |
| Recrystallization solvent | Hot water (or dilute HCl) | 3-nitrobenzoic acid is moderately soluble in hot water |
| Melting point (pure) | 140–142 °C | Sharp range ≤ 1 °C indicates purity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Purity:** A melting point range of more than **2 °C** or a depressed melting point indicates the presence of impurities. This is a high-yield KTU viva question.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Nitrated aromatic acids such as 3-nitrobenzoic acid are key intermediates in:

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** precursors to local anesthetics (e.g., procaine, benzocaine family).
- **Dye industry:** starting materials for azo-dye synthesis.
- **Polymer industry:** precursors to aromatic diamines (after reduction of –NO₂ to –NH₂) used in making aramid fibers.
- **Agrochemicals:** building blocks for herbicides and pesticides.

Understanding regioselectivity (ortho/para vs meta) is foundational for **process chemists** designing industrial syntheses where isomeric purity directly affects drug safety and dye color.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Detailed Mechanism of Nitration

**Step 1 — Generation of NO₂⁺** (already shown above).

**Step 2 — Electrophilic attack.** The π-electrons of the benzene ring attack the nitrogen of NO₂⁺, forming a new C–N σ-bond and creating a positively charged cyclohexadienyl cation (the Wheland intermediate). Three resonance structures are possible; for the *meta* attack on benzoic acid, none of these place the positive charge on the carbon bearing the –COOH group, which is why meta attack is preferred.

The Wheland intermediate for *meta* attack (showing one key resonance form) can be represented as:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH} + \text{NO}_2^+ \longrightarrow [\text{Arenium ion (sigma-complex)}]^+$$

**Step 3 — Loss of H⁺ (deprotonation).** The bisulfate ion (HSO₄⁻) abstracts the proton from the sp³ carbon:

$$[\text{Arenium ion}]^+ + \text{HSO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{O}_2\text{N–C}_6\text{H}_4\text{–COOH} + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

The aromatic sextet is restored, and the final product is **3-nitrobenzoic acid**.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Yield Calculation Derivation

**Standard Lab Conditions (KTU Typical):**
- Benzoic acid taken: **5.0 g**
- Concentrated HNO₃: **10 mL**
- Concentrated H₂SO₄: **10 mL**
- Actual mass of pure 3-nitrobenzoic acid obtained (after recrystallization): **4.2 g** (assumed value for illustration)

**Step 1: Calculate moles of benzoic acid (limiting reagent check).**

$$n_{\text{BA}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{BA}}}{M_{\text{BA}}} = \dfrac{5.0 \text{ g}}{122.12 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$n_{\text{BA}} = 0.04094 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 2: Calculate moles of HNO₃.**

Volume = 10 mL, density = 1.42 g/mL.

$$m_{\text{HNO}_3} = \rho \times V = 1.42 \text{ g/mL} \times 10 \text{ mL} = 14.2 \text{ g}$$

$$n_{\text{HNO}_3} = \dfrac{14.2 \text{ g}}{63.01 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.2254 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 3: Identify the limiting reagent.**

The stoichiometry is **1 : 1** (benzoic acid : HNO₃).

$$n_{\text{BA}} = 0.04094 \text{ mol} \ll n_{\text{HNO}_3} = 0.2254 \text{ mol}$$

Therefore, **benzoic acid is the limiting reagent**, and HNO₃ is in large excess (this is intentional — concentrated acid also acts as the solvent medium).

**Step 4: Theoretical moles of 3-nitrobenzoic acid.**

By 1 : 1 stoichiometry:

$$n_{\text{product, theo}} = n_{\text{BA}} = 0.04094 \text{ mol}$$

**Step 5: Theoretical mass of 3-nitrobenzoic acid.**

$$m_{\text{theo}} = n \times M = 0.04094 \text{ mol} \times 167.12 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$m_{\text{theo}} = 6.842 \text{ g}$$

**Step 6: Percentage yield.**

$$\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$$

$$\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{4.2 \text{ g}}{6.842 \text{ g}} \times 100 = 61.39\ \%$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Typical KTU Expected Yield Range:** For this experiment, a yield of **55–70 %** is considered excellent; below 40 % indicates procedural loss (incomplete transfer, premature crystallization, inefficient filtration).

### 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedural Derivation (Worked Walkthrough)

1. **Setup:** Place benzoic acid (5 g) in a clean 100 mL round-bottom flask (RBF) kept in an **ice bath**.
2. **Slow addition of nitrating mixture:** In a separate beaker, mix conc. HNO₃ (10 mL) and conc. H₂SO₄ (10 mL) **slowly with cooling** (this generates NO₂⁺; the mixture warms up). Add this nitrating mixture **dropwise** to the benzoic acid with continuous swirling and **ice-bath cooling** to keep T ≤ 10–15 °C.
3. **Reaction:** Stir at room temperature for **15–20 minutes**. The reaction is exothermic; do not allow temperature to exceed 20 °C, or else polynitration (dinitration) and oxidation will occur.
4. **Quenching:** Pour the reaction mixture slowly into **50–75 mL of ice-cold water** with stirring. The crude 3-nitrobenzoic acid precipitates as a pale-yellow solid.
5. **Filtration:** Vacuum-filter using a Büchner funnel; wash the solid with **cold water** (2 × 10 mL) to remove residual acids.
6. **Recrystallization:** Dissolve the crude product in the minimum amount of **boiling water**; filter hot if any insoluble impurity is visible; cool the filtrate in an ice bath. Pure 3-nitrobenzoic acid crystallizes as white to off-white needles.
7. **Drying:** Filter and air-dry (or oven-dry below 80 °C).
8. **Characterization:** Determine the **melting point** (expected: 140–142 °C) and **percentage yield**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why ice-cold water for quenching?** It rapidly dilutes the acid, stops further reaction, and minimizes hydrolysis/local heating that could decompose the product.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Procedural Block Diagram

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
*Why is the –COOH group in benzoic acid a meta-director in electrophilic aromatic substitution?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The –COOH group is a strong **electron-withdrawing group (EWG)** exhibiting both **–I (inductive)** and **–M (resonance)** effects. It deactivates the ring toward electrophilic attack. When an electrophile attacks at the *ortho* or *para* position, the resulting Wheland intermediate (sigma-complex) places a **positive charge on the carbon bearing the –COOH group**, which is highly destabilizing because –COOH itself withdraws electrons. In *meta* attack, the positive charge never resides on the ipso carbon, so the intermediate is comparatively more stable. Therefore, the *meta* isomer (3-nitrobenzoic acid) is the major product. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
*What is the role of concentrated H₂SO₄ in the nitration of benzoic acid?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Concentrated H₂SO₄ acts as a **protonating agent and dehydrating agent**. It protonates HNO₃ to form protonated nitric acid (H₂NO₃⁺), which subsequently loses a water molecule to generate the **nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)** — the active electrophile that attacks the aromatic ring. H₂SO₄ also absorbs the water produced during the reaction, driving the equilibrium forward. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]*

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe the laboratory procedure for the synthesis of 3-nitrobenzoic acid from benzoic acid. Include the reagents, conditions, and workup.

**(b) [7 marks]** Starting from 5.0 g of benzoic acid and using excess nitrating mixture, calculate the theoretical yield of 3-nitrobenzoic acid. If the actual yield is 4.2 g, what is the percentage yield?

**Model Answer:**

**(a) [7 marks]**
1. Weigh **5.0 g of benzoic acid** into a clean 100 mL round-bottom flask and place it in an **ice bath**. **(1 mark)**
2. In a separate beaker, prepare the **nitrating mixture** by carefully mixing **10 mL of concentrated HNO₃** and **10 mL of concentrated H₂SO₄** with cooling (this generates the nitronium ion NO₂⁺). **(1 mark)**
3. Add the nitrating mixture **dropwise** to the benzoic acid while swirling, keeping the temperature **below 15 °C** to avoid polynitration and oxidation. **(1 mark)**
4. After complete addition, stir the reaction mixture at **room temperature for 15–20 minutes**. **(1 mark)**
5. **Quench** the reaction by slowly pouring it into **50–75 mL of ice-cold water** with continuous stirring; the crude 3-nitrobenzoic acid precipitates. **(1 mark)**
6. Filter the solid by **vacuum filtration** (Büchner funnel); wash with **cold water** to remove residual acids. **(1 mark)**
7. **Recrystallize** the crude product from the minimum volume of **boiling water**, cool in ice, filter, and dry. Determine the **melting point** (expected 140–142 °C). **(1 mark)**

**(b) [7 marks]**

Step 1: Moles of benzoic acid.

$$n_{\text{BA}} = \dfrac{5.0 \text{ g}}{122.12 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.04094 \text{ mol}$$ **(2 marks)**

Step 2: Stoichiometry is 1 : 1, so moles of product (theoretical) = 0.04094 mol. **(1 mark)**

Step 3: Theoretical mass.

$$m_{\text{theo}} = 0.04094 \text{ mol} \times 167.12 \text{ g/mol} = 6.842 \text{ g}$$ **(2 marks)**

Step 4: Percentage yield.

$$\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{4.2}{6.842} \times 100 = 61.39\,\% \approx 61.4\,\%$$ **(2 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Alternative]*

**(a) [7 marks]** With the help of a stepwise mechanism, explain why nitration of benzoic acid gives predominantly the *meta* product. Identify the electrophile.

**(b) [7 marks]** A student obtained 3.5 g of crude 3-nitrobenzoic acid from 4.0 g of benzoic acid. After recrystallization, only 2.8 g of pure product was recovered. Calculate (i) the crude percentage yield and (ii) the recovery percentage after recrystallization. Comment on the purity using the melting point data: observed 138–141 °C.

**Model Answer:**

**(a) [7 marks]**

The electrophile is the **nitronium ion, NO₂⁺**, generated as: **(1 mark)**

$$\text{HNO}_3 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2^+ + 2\,\text{HSO}_4^- + \text{H}_3\text{O}^+$$

Mechanism: **(3 marks)**
1. NO₂⁺ attacks the π-cloud of the benzene ring → Wheland intermediate (arenium ion).
2. For *ortho/para* attack, the +ve charge in the intermediate falls on the C bearing –COOH, which is destabilizing because –COOH is electron-withdrawing.
3. For *meta* attack, the +ve charge is delocalized over the other carbons — never on the ipso carbon — so the intermediate is more stable.
4. Loss of H⁺ to HSO₄⁻ restores aromaticity, giving 3-nitrobenzoic acid.

Hence, the *meta* isomer predominates. **(3 marks total for reasoning)**

**(b) [7 marks]**

Step 1: Moles of benzoic acid.

$$n_{\text{BA}} = \dfrac{4.0 \text{ g}}{122.12 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.03276 \text{ mol}$$ **(1 mark)**

Step 2: Theoretical mass of product.

$$m_{\text{theo}} = 0.03276 \times 167.12 = 5.475 \text{ g}$$ **(1 mark)**

Step 3: Crude percentage yield.

$$\%\text{Yield}_{\text{crude}} = \dfrac{3.5}{5.475} \times 100 = 63.93\,\%$$ **(1.5 marks)**

Step 4: Recovery after recrystallization.

$$\%\text{Recovery} = \dfrac{2.8}{3.5} \times 100 = 80.00\,\%$$ **(1.5 marks)**

Step 5: Purity comment. **(2 marks)**
The observed melting point is **138–141 °C** (range = 3 °C), which is **below the literature value of 140–142 °C** and shows a **broad range > 2 °C**. This indicates the presence of impurities (likely residual benzoic acid or moisture). The product should be re-recrystallized for further purification.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Insights):**
> 1. **Forgetting the limiting-reagent check** in yield calculations → lose 1–2 marks. Always check moles of benzoic acid vs HNO₃.
> 2. **Confusing ortho/para vs meta directors.** The –COOH group is **meta-directing**, NOT ortho/para. Students often mix this up with –OH or –CH₃.
> 3. **Not mentioning the nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)** explicitly — KTU examiners allot at least 1 mark for identifying the electrophile.
> 4. **Ignoring temperature control.** Failing to mention that the reaction mixture must be kept cold (≤ 15 °C) during nitration results in dinitration/oxidation — penalize 1 mark.
> 5. **In the melting-point question**, not commenting on the **sharpness of the range** (≤ 1 °C sharp = pure; > 2 °C broad = impure). This is a 2-mark differentiator.
> 6. **Mixing up the molecular weight:** students sometimes take MW of benzoic acid as 167 instead of 122.12 — full calculation goes wrong.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Reaction type:** Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS) — Nitration.
- **Active electrophile:** Nitronium ion, **NO₂⁺**, generated from **conc. HNO₃ + conc. H₂SO₄**.
- **Major product:** **3-nitrobenzoic acid** (*m*-nitrobenzoic acid).
- **Reason for meta-selectivity:** –COOH is **–I, –M (deactivating, meta-directing)**; *ortho/para* attack destabilizes the Wheland intermediate by placing +ve charge on the ipso carbon.
- **Key reagents:** Benzoic acid (5 g), conc. HNO₃ (10 mL), conc. H₂SO₄ (10 mL), ice-cold water (for quench).
- **Key conditions:** Temperature ≤ 15 °C during addition; stir 15–20 min at RT; quench into ice-water.
- **Purification technique:** **Recrystallization from hot water**.
- **Characterization:** **Melting point = 140–142 °C** (sharp range ≤ 1 °C indicates purity).
- **MWs to memorize:** Benzoic acid = **122.12 g/mol**; 3-nitrobenzoic acid = **167.12 g/mol**; HNO₃ = **63.01 g/mol**.
- **Expected % yield:** ~55–70 %.
- **Limiting reagent:** Benzoic acid (HNO₃ used in large excess to also act as solvent medium).
- **Safety points:** Concentrated H₂SO₄ and HNO₃ are highly corrosive — always add acid to water (not water to acid) and use gloves, goggles, and fume hood.
- **Viva-ready facts:**
  - Why is H₂SO₄ called the "activating acid" of nitrating mixture? → Generates NO₂⁺ and absorbs water.
  - What is the role of an ice bath? → Controls exotherm, prevents dinitration and oxidation.
  - Why is the product called "3-nitrobenzoic acid" and not "m-nitrobenzoic acid" in IUPAC? → Lower locant rule; –COOH gets C-1, –NO₂ at C-3.
  - What happens if temperature rises above 25 °C? → Polynitration (3,5-dinitrobenzoic acid) and oxidation to give colored byproducts.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Nitration of aromatic hydrocarbons
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Nitration of aromatic hydrocarbons

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

**Nitration of aromatic hydrocarbons** is a classic **Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS)** reaction in which one or more hydrogen atoms on the aromatic ring (benzene, toluene, chlorobenzene, naphthalene, etc.) are replaced by a **nitro group (–NO₂)**. The reaction is catalyzed by a strong acid system — typically a mixture of concentrated **HNO₃** and **H₂SO₄** (known as the *nitrating mixture* or *mixed acid*) — which generates the active electrophile, the **nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)**.

The general stoichiometry for the mononitration of benzene is:

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{HNO}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{Conc. H}_2\text{SO}_4} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is designed to demonstrate the **regioselectivity** of EAS, the role of the **nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)** as the true electrophile, and to practice the calculation of **percentage yield, theoretical yield, and limiting reagent identification** — all of which are **high-weightage** in the ESE viva and record evaluation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

Imagine an aromatic ring as a **heavily guarded circular fortress** (the six π-electrons forming a stable aromatic cloud). The nitro group (NO₂⁺) is like a **determined intruder** carrying a positive charge — it desperately wants to enter the fortress. 

However, the fortress walls are **electron-rich** and repel the positively charged intruder. The intruder needs **"keys" (the acid catalysts)** to break in. The strong acid mixture (H₂SO₄) acts as a **master locksmith** that forges the key (NO₂⁺) from a benign molecule (HNO₃). Once inside, the intruder temporarily deactivates one guard (forming the **sigma-complex / arenium ion**), but the fortress **regenerates its guards** by kicking out a proton (H⁺) — this restores full aromaticity. The net result: **one guard (H) is permanently replaced by the intruder (NO₂)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Connect:** Nitration is the foundational industrial step for synthesizing **nitrobenzene → aniline → dyes, pharmaceuticals, polyurethane foams, and explosives (TNT, picric acid)**. A chemical engineer must master this reaction's control variables (temperature, acid ratio, mixing) to safely scale it from a 10 mL lab beaker to a 10,000 L industrial CSTR.

---

### 1.3 Key Physical & Chemical Constants

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Electrophile** | **NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion)** | The actual attacking species |
| **Nitrating mixture** | **Conc. HNO₃ + Conc. H₂SO₄ (1:2 by volume)** | Generates NO₂⁺ *in situ* |
| **Reaction temperature** | **50 – 60 °C** (benzene), **30 – 40 °C** (toluene) | Higher temps → polynitration / oxidation |
| **Density of nitrobenzene** | **1.203 g/mL at 20 °C** | Used in yield calculations |
| **Molar mass of nitrobenzene** | **123.06 g/mol** | Stoichiometry constant |
| **Molar mass of benzene** | **78.11 g/mol** | Stoichiometry constant |
| **Aromaticity rule** | **Hückel's 4n + 2 π-electrons (n = 1 for benzene)** | Why the ring is "stable" |
| **Activation energy (Eₐ)** | ~ **55 – 65 kJ/mol** | Lower with electron-donating groups |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Condition:** All yield and stoichiometric calculations in this experiment assume the reaction is carried out at **STP-equivalent lab conditions (25 °C, 1 atm)** for theoretical yield purposes. The actual reaction temperature is reported separately as an observation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The "Why & How" — Breaking Down the Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution Mechanism

The nitration of benzene proceeds through a well-defined **three-step ionic mechanism**. Each step has a specific energetic and electronic rationale.

#### **Step 1: Generation of the Electrophile (Nitronium Ion, NO₂⁺)**

Conc. H₂SO₄ is a **stronger acid** than HNO₃. It donates a proton to the nitric acid, which then loses a water molecule to generate the linear nitronium cation.

**Why this step?**
The NO₂⁺ ion is the **true electrophile** — pure HNO₃ alone is too weak an electrophile to attack the electron-rich aromatic ring. Concentrated H₂SO₄ acts as a **proton shuttle** and a **dehydrating agent**, pushing the equilibrium toward NO₂⁺ formation.

**How it works (protonation → dehydration):**

$$\text{HNO}_3 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \longrightarrow \text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ + \text{HSO}_4^-$$

$$\text{H}_2\text{NO}_3^+ \longrightarrow \text{NO}_2^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}$$

#### **Step 2: Electrophilic Attack — Formation of the Sigma Complex (Arenium Ion)**

The π-cloud of benzene attacks the **electron-deficient** nitrogen of NO₂⁺, forming a new C–N σ-bond. This disrupts aromaticity and produces a **positively charged, non-aromatic cyclohexadienyl cation** (the *Wheland intermediate* or *σ-complex*).

**Why this is the rate-determining step (RDS):**
The aromaticity is temporarily broken, which carries a high energetic penalty (~ 110 kJ/mol of resonance energy is lost). The transition state is **late and product-like**, making this the **slowest, most endothermic** step.

**How it works:**

$$\text{C}_6\text{H}_6 + \text{NO}_2^+ \longrightarrow \left[\text{C}_6\text{H}_6\!-\!\text{NO}_2\right]^+ \quad (\sigma\text{-complex})$$

#### **Step 3: Deprotonation — Restoration of Aromaticity**

A base (typically HSO₄⁻ or H₂O) abstracts the proton from the sp³ carbon bearing the –NO₂ group. The two electrons of the C–H bond flow back into the ring, **restoring aromaticity** and yielding the final nitrobenzene.

**Why is this fast?**
The driving force is the **regain of aromatic stabilization energy** (~ 150 kJ/mol released). This step is **strongly exothermic** and essentially irreversible.

**How it works:**

$$\left[\text{C}_6\text{H}_6\!-\!\text{NO}_2\right]^+ + \text{HSO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{NO}_2 + \text{H}_2\text{SO}_4$$

---

### 2.2 Regioselectivity & Directing Effects (Substituted Benzenes)

When the aromatic ring carries a substituent (e.g., –CH₃ in toluene, –Cl in chlorobenzene), the incoming NO₂⁺ is **directed** to specific positions:

| Substituent Type | Example | Directive Effect | Reactivity vs. Benzene | Reason |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Activating, ortho/para-directing** | –OH, –OCH₃, –CH₃, –NH₂ | ortho + para | **Faster** | Donate electrons via resonance / hyperconjugation |
| **Deactivating, ortho/para-directing** | –Cl, –Br, –F | ortho + para | **Slower** | Inductive withdrawal, but resonance donation to ring |
| **Deactivating, meta-directing** | –NO₂, –CN, –COOH, –SO₃H | meta | **Much slower** | Strong inductive + resonance withdrawal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Viva Favorite:** *"Why is toluene nitrated at a lower temperature (30–40 °C) than benzene (50–60 °C)?"* — Because the –CH₃ group is **electron-donating**, it **activates** the ring, making toluene *more reactive* than benzene. Lower temperature is sufficient and prevents **polynitration** (formation of 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene, i.e., TNT).

---

### 2.3 Side Reactions to Watch For (Examiner's Trap!)

1. **Polynitration** → formation of *m*-dinitrobenzene or TNT.
2. **Oxidation** of the ring by HNO₃ at high temperatures → formation of **picric acid** (yellow by-product).
3. **Sulfonation** by H₂SO₄ at temperatures > 100 °C → formation of **benzenesulfonic acid**.
4. **Phenolic oxidation** (if phenol is used) → tarry, dark-colored polymeric mass.

> [!WARNING]
> **Temperature control is critical.** Exceeding 60 °C for benzene or 50 °C for phenol leads to **violent runaway reactions and oxidation**, which is a top reason for poor yields and unsafe conditions in the KTU lab.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Quantity | Expression | Units | Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Theoretical Yield** | $n_{\text{limiting}} \times M_{\text{product}}$ | **grams (g)** | Maximum possible product from stoichiometry |
| **Actual Yield** | Measured mass of dried product | **grams (g)** | From lab experiment |
| **Percentage Yield** | $\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual}}{\text{Theoretical}} \times 100$ | **%** | KTU record mandatory entry |
| **Moles** | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | **mol** | Convert mass ↔ moles |
| **Volume to Mass (liquid)** | $m = \rho \times V$ | **g** (with $\rho$ in **g/mL**) | For liquid reactants like HNO₃ |
| **Limiting Reagent Rule** | Smaller value of $\dfrac{n}{\nu}$ (where $\nu$ = stoichiometric coefficient) wins | dimensionless | Identifies LR |
| **Regioselectivity Ratio (toluene)** | **ortho : para ≈ 60 : 40** | % | Expected experimental observation |
| **Volume of mixed acid (typical)** | **20 mL (1:2 HNO₃:H₂SO₄)** | **mL** | Standard KTU lab protocol |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Table-Building Rule Reminder:** In the lab record, **never** use the vertical pipe symbol `|` inside table cells to denote separators other than markdown column dividers. Always use `\vert` in LaTeX or spell out "absolute value of" in text.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry Sector | End Product from Nitration |
|---|---|
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Paracetamol (acetaminophen), Nitrofurantoin |
| **Dyes & Pigments** | Aniline → azo dyes (e.g., Methyl Orange, Congo Red) |
| **Explosives** | TNT (2,4,6-trinitrotoluene), Picric acid, PETN precursors |
| **Polymer Industry** | MDI (methylene diphenyl diisocyanate) for polyurethanes |
| **Agrochemicals** | Nitrobenzene → herbicides and pesticides |
| **Perfume Industry** | Musk ketone, Musk xylene (synthetic nitromusks) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Chemical Engineering Note:** Industrial nitration is typically performed in a **Continuous Stirred Tank Reactor (CSTR)** or **plug-flow reactor** with **adiabatic temperature control** and **in-line phase separation** due to the exothermic nature (ΔH ≈ –117 kJ/mol for benzene nitration).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example — Percentage Yield Calculation for Nitration of Benzene

> **Given Data (Standard KTU-style problem):**
> - Volume of benzene used: **5.0 mL**
> - Density of benzene: $\rho_{\text{benzene}} = 0.876 \text{ g/mL}$
> - Volume of conc. HNO₃: **5.0 mL** (excess)
> - Density of HNO₃: $\rho_{\text{HNO}_3} = 1.41 \text{ g/mL}$
> - Assay of HNO₃: **70 % (w/w)**
> - Molar mass of benzene: $M_{\text{benzene}} = 78.11 \text{ g/mol}$
> - Molar mass of HNO₃: $M_{\text{HNO}_3} = 63.01 \text{ g/mol}$
> - Molar mass of nitrobenzene: $M_{\text{nb}} = 123.06 \text{ g/mol}$
> - Actual mass of pure, dried nitrobenzene obtained: **5.8 g**

---

#### **Step 1: Convert Liquid Volumes to Masses**

For benzene:

$$m_{\text{benzene}} = \rho_{\text{benzene}} \times V_{\text{benzene}}$$

$$m_{\text{benzene}} = 0.876 \text{ g/mL} \times 5.0 \text{ mL} = 4.38 \text{ g}$$

For HNO₃ (before applying assay):

$$m_{\text{HNO}_3, \text{ gross}} = \rho_{\text{HNO}_3} \times V_{\text{HNO}_3} = 1.41 \text{ g/mL} \times 5.0 \text{ mL} = 7.05 \text{ g}$$

Applying the 70 % assay (purity correction):

$$m_{\text{HNO}_3, \text{ pure}} = 7.05 \text{ g} \times 0.70 = 4.935 \text{ g}$$

---

#### **Step 2: Convert Masses to Moles**

For benzene:

$$n_{\text{benzene}} = \dfrac{m_{\text{benzene}}}{M_{\text{benzene}}} = \dfrac{4.38 \text{ g}}{78.11 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$n_{\text{benzene}} = 0.05608 \text{ mol}$$

For HNO₃ (pure):

$$n_{\text{HNO}_3} = \dfrac{m_{\text{HNO}_3, \text{ pure}}}{M_{\text{HNO}_3}} = \dfrac{4.935 \text{ g}}{63.01 \text{ g/mol}}$$

$$n_{\text{HNO}_3} = 0.07832 \text{ mol}$$

---

#### **Step 3: Identify the Limiting Reagent**

From the balanced equation: $\nu_{\text{benzene}} = 1$ and $\nu_{\text{HNO}_3} = 1$.

$$\dfrac{n_{\text{benzene}}}{\nu_{\text{benzene}}} = \dfrac{0.05608}{1} = 0.05608$$

$$\dfrac{n_{\text{HNO}_3}}{\nu_{\text{HNO}_3}} = \dfrac{0.07832}{1} = 0.07832$$

**Comparison:** $0.05608 < 0.07832$

> **∴ Benzene is the LIMITING REAGENT (LR).** All yield calculations must be based on benzene.

---

#### **Step 4: Calculate Theoretical Yield of Nitrobenzene**

Stoichiometry: **1 mol benzene → 1 mol nitrobenzene**.

$$n_{\text{nb, theoretical}} = n_{\text{benzene, LR}} = 0.05608 \text{ mol}$$

Converting to mass:

$$m_{\text{nb, theoretical}} = n_{\text{nb, theoretical}} \times M_{\text{nb}}$$

$$m_{\text{nb, theoretical}} = 0.05608 \text{ mol} \times 123.06 \text{ g/mol}$$

$$m_{\text{nb, theoretical}} = 6.902 \text{ g}$$

---

#### **Step 5: Calculate Percentage Yield**

$$\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Yield}}{\text{Theoretical Yield}} \times 100$$

$$\% \text{Yield} = \dfrac{5.8 \text{ g}}{6.902 \text{ g}} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \text{Yield} = 84.03 \,\%}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Standard:** A good lab yield for benzene nitration is **70 – 85 %**. Yields below 60 % indicate loss during separation (steam distillation, washing with NaHCO₃, drying over anhydrous Na₂SO₄) or incomplete reaction.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example — Regioselectivity Calculation for Nitration of Toluene

> **Given:** 10.0 g of toluene is nitrated. The products obtained are: *o*-nitrotoluene = 9.8 g, *p*-nitrotoluene = 6.4 g, and *m*-nitrotoluene = 0.5 g.

**Step 1: Total mass of mononitrotoluene isomers**

$$m_{\text{total}} = 9.8 + 6.4 + 0.5 = 16.7 \text{ g}$$

**Step 2: Mass percentages of each isomer**

$$\% \text{ ortho} = \dfrac{9.8}{16.7} \times 100 = 58.68 \,\%$$

$$\% \text{ para} = \dfrac{6.4}{16.7} \times 100 = 38.32 \,\%$$

$$\% \text{ meta} = \dfrac{0.5}{16.7} \times 100 = 2.99 \,\%$$

**Step 3: Ortho : Para ratio**

$$\text{ortho : para} = 58.68 : 38.32 \approx \boxed{1.53 : 1} \quad (\text{or } \approx 60 : 40)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This experimentally verifies **Hückel's rule of directive effects** — the –CH₃ group in toluene is an *ortho/para director*, so the meta isomer should be **< 5 %** of the product mix. Any meta yield > 10 % indicates **poor temperature control** or **reagent contamination**.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Diagnostic Tests for Nitro Group in the Product

| Test | Observation | Inference | Marks (KTU Viva) |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Physical appearance** | Pale yellow oily liquid, almond-like odor | Confirms nitrobenzene | 1 |
| **Density test** | Sinks in water ($\rho = 1.203$ g/mL $> 1$) | Confirms nitro group (organic + heavy) | 1 |
| **Mulliken's test (reduction with Zn + NH₄Cl)** | Colorless solution turns yellow with Schiff's reagent | Confirms –NO₂ reduced to –NH₂ | 2 |
| **Benedict's test on reduced product** | Reddish precipitate (Cu₂O) | Confirms primary aromatic amine (–NH₂) | 1 |
| **Ferrous sulfate test** | Brown ring (Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ complex) | Confirms NO₂ group | 1 |
| **Beilstein test (Cu wire)** | Green flame | Confirms presence of aromatic ring (not specific to NO₂) | 0.5 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — Complete Reaction Mechanism Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 0: Reactants Benzene C6H6 + HNO3 + H2SO4"]
    B["Step 1A: Protonation HNO3 + H2SO4 yields H2NO3+ and HSO4-"]
    C["Step 1B: Dehydration H2NO3+ yields NO2+ and H2O"]
    D["Step 2: Electrophilic Attack Benzene pi cloud attacks NO2+ forming sigma complex arenium ion"]
    E["Step 3: Deprotonation HSO4- removes H+ from sp3 carbon"]
    F["Final Product: Nitrobenzene C6H5NO2 + H2SO4 regenerated"]
    G["Side Reaction: Polynitration if T greater than 60 C"]
    H["Side Reaction: Oxidation to picric acid if strong heating"]
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D
    D --> E
    E --> F
    F -.excess HNO3.-> G
    F -.T too high.-> H
```

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Experimental Procedure Block Flow (Lab-Adapted Schematic)

