```mermaid
    P1["Block 1: Setup 100 mL RBF + ice bath + stirrer"]
    P2["Block 2: Add 20 mL mixed acid 1:2 HNO3 to H2SO4"]
    P3["Block 3: Dropwise addition of 5 mL benzene over 30 min at T less than 60 C"]
    P4["Block 4: Stir at 55 C for 45 min on water bath"]
    P5["Block 5: Cool to RT and pour into 100 mL cold water"]
    P6["Block 6: Steam distillation to remove unreacted benzene"]
    P7["Block 7: Separate organic layer in separating funnel"]
    P8["Block 8: Wash with 5 percent NaHCO3 then water"]
    P9["Block 9: Dry over anhydrous Na2SO4"]
    P10["Block 10: Weigh pale yellow nitrobenzene and compute percent yield"]
    P1 --> P2
    P2 --> P3
    P3 --> P4
    P4 --> P5
    P5 --> P6
    P6 --> P7
    P7 --> P8
    P8 --> P9
    P9 --> P10
```

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Regioselectivity Decision Tree for Substituted Benzenes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1["Substituted aromatic ring with group X"]
    R2["Is X electron donating by resonance or hyperconjugation"]
    R3["Activating group ortho para director e.g. CH3 OCH3 OH NH2"]
    R4["Is X deactivating by inductive withdrawal only halogens"]
    R5["Halogen: ortho para director but DEACTIVATING"]
    R6["Is X strongly deactivating by resonance NO2 CN COOH SO3H"]
    R7["Strong deactivator: META director e.g. nitrobenzene meta DNB"]
    R8["Kinetics: rate vs unsubstituted benzene"]
    R1 --> R2
    R2 -- yes --> R3
    R2 -- no --> R4
    R4 -- yes --> R5
    R4 -- no --> R6
    R6 -- yes --> R7
    R3 --> R8
    R5 --> R8
    R7 --> R8
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**What is the nitrating mixture? Explain the role of concentrated H₂SO₄ in the nitration of benzene.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **nitrating mixture** is a combination of **concentrated HNO₃ and concentrated H₂SO₄** (typically in a 1:2 volume ratio). The role of H₂SO₄ is twofold:
1. It is a **stronger acid** than HNO₃, so it **protonates HNO₃** to form the protonated species H₂NO₃⁺.
2. It acts as a **dehydrating agent**, abstracting a water molecule from H₂NO₃⁺ to generate the **electrophile — the nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)** — which is the actual species that attacks the benzene ring. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Why is the temperature maintained at 50 – 60 °C during the nitration of benzene?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
1. The nitration of benzene is **moderately exothermic** (ΔH ≈ –117 kJ/mol). A temperature of **50 – 60 °C** provides the **activation energy** (~ 60 kJ/mol) required for the rate-determining step (formation of the σ-complex).
2. Lower temperatures (e.g., < 40 °C) make the reaction impractically slow.
3. **Higher temperatures (> 60 °C)** cause **side reactions** such as **polynitration** (formation of *m*-dinitrobenzene), **oxidation** (yielding picric acid), and **sulfonation** (by H₂SO₄).
4. Hence 50 – 60 °C is the **optimum compromise** between reaction rate and selectivity. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 PART B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> **Module Experiment 2** carries 14 marks in the ESE, split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. Two alternative question sets are given below; the student answers **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]`

**(a) Describe the mechanism of nitration of benzene with the nitrating mixture. Include all three steps and the role of the electrophile.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Generation of the Electrophile (Nitronium Ion):**
Concentrated H₂SO₄ protonates HNO₃, forming H₂NO₃⁺, which loses water to yield the linear nitronium cation NO₂⁺ (the active electrophile). **(2 marks)**

**Step 2 — Electrophilic Attack (Rate-Determining Step):**
The π-electron cloud of benzene attacks the electron-deficient nitrogen of NO₂⁺, forming a new C–N σ-bond. This produces a resonance-stabilized, non-aromatic cyclohexadienyl cation known as the **σ-complex (Wheland intermediate)**. The temporary loss of aromaticity makes this the **slowest and most endothermic step**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3 — Deprotonation (Restoration of Aromaticity):**
A base (HSO₄⁻ or H₂O) abstracts the proton from the sp³ carbon. The C–H bond electrons flow back into the ring, **regenerating aromaticity** and yielding the final **nitrobenzene** (C₆H₅NO₂). This step is fast and strongly exothermic. **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) In a KTU lab experiment, 5.0 mL of benzene (ρ = 0.876 g/mL) was nitrated using 5.0 mL of 70 % (w/w) HNO₃ (ρ = 1.41 g/mL) in the presence of conc. H₂SO₄. The mass of pure, dried nitrobenzene (M = 123.06 g/mol) obtained was 5.8 g. Calculate the percentage yield.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass of benzene:**
$m_{\text{benzene}} = 0.876 \times 5.0 = 4.38$ g
$n_{\text{benzene}} = 4.38 / 78.11 = 0.05608$ mol **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 2 — Mass and moles of pure HNO₃:**
$m_{\text{HNO}_3, \text{ pure}} = (1.41 \times 5.0) \times 0.70 = 4.935$ g
$n_{\text{HNO}_3} = 4.935 / 63.01 = 0.07832$ mol **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 3 — Limiting Reagent:**
Since both have 1:1 stoichiometry, **benzene** (0.05608 mol) is the **limiting reagent**. **(1 mark)**

**Step 4 — Theoretical yield of nitrobenzene:**
$m_{\text{nb, theoretical}} = 0.05608 \times 123.06 = 6.902$ g **(1.5 marks)**

**Step 5 — Percentage yield:**
$\% \text{Yield} = (5.8 / 6.902) \times 100 = \mathbf{84.03 \,\%}$ **(1.5 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Supplementary 2023]`

**(a) Discuss the directive influence of the –CH₃ group in toluene during nitration. Predict the major products and justify using resonance structures.** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The –CH₃ group in toluene is an **electron-donating group** that operates by **hyperconjugation** (+I effect). It **activates** the ring and directs incoming electrophiles (NO₂⁺) to the **ortho and para** positions. **(2 marks)**

**Resonance justification:**
When NO₂⁺ attacks at the *ortho* or *para* position, the resulting σ-complex has **three resonance structures**, all of which place the positive charge on a carbon. In the *meta* attack, the σ-complex has only **two resonance structures**, and one of them places the positive charge on the carbon bearing the –CH₃ group (highly destabilizing due to electron donation onto an already positive center). **(3 marks)**

**Major Products:** *o*-nitrotoluene (~ 60 %) and *p*-nitrotoluene (~ 40 %); *m*-nitrotoluene is the minor product (< 5 %). **(2 marks)**

---

**(b) Outline the laboratory procedure for the nitration of benzene. Why is steam distillation used in the work-up?** **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Procedure (4 marks):**
1. **Setup:** A 100 mL round-bottom flask is fitted with a stirrer, thermometer, and dropping funnel, and placed in an **ice bath**.
2. **Nitrating mixture:** 20 mL of mixed acid (1:2 HNO₃ : H₂SO₄) is added to the flask and cooled to 10 °C.
3. **Addition:** 5 mL of benzene is added **dropwise** over 30 minutes while maintaining the temperature at **50 – 60 °C**.
4. **Digestion:** The mixture is stirred at 55 °C for 45 minutes.
5. **Work-up:** The mixture is cooled to RT and poured into 100 mL of ice-cold water. The oily nitrobenzene separates as a yellow lower layer.
6. **Purification:** Steam distillation removes **unreacted benzene** (b.p. 80 °C) but **not nitrobenzene** (b.p. 210 °C). The remaining nitrobenzene is washed with **5 % NaHCO₃** (to remove acid) and then with water, dried over **anhydrous Na₂SO₄**, and weighed.

**Why steam distillation? (3 marks):**
- Benzene (b.p. 80 °C) and water (b.p. 100 °C) form a **low-boiling azeotrope**, allowing benzene to distill off at ~ 69 °C.
- Nitrobenzene (b.p. 210 °C) is **non-volatile** in steam and stays in the flask.
- This achieves a **clean separation** of the product from the unreacted starting material **without thermal decomposition** of nitrobenzene.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students LOSE MARKS in this experiment — Read carefully!**
> 
> 1. **Forgetting the 70 % assay correction for HNO₃** → theoretical yield is overestimated → % yield appears falsely low. **Penalty: –1.5 marks** in the calculation step.
> 2. **Confusing density of benzene (0.876 g/mL) with that of nitrobenzene (1.203 g/mL)** when computing mass from volume. **Penalty: –1 mark.**
> 3. **Calling HNO₃ the electrophile directly** — it is NOT. The **nitronium ion (NO₂⁺)** is the true electrophile. **Penalty: –1 mark** in mechanism.
> 4. **Stating "polynitration occurs because the ring is activated"** — this is logically inverted. Polynitration occurs because the **first nitro group deactivates the ring only modestly** at high HNO₃ concentration, and high temperature drives further substitution. **Penalty: –1 mark.**
> 5. **Omitting the units (g, mol, %)** in the final answer — strict KTU rule. **Penalty: –0.5 mark per step.**
> 6. **Not stating the role of NaHCO₃ wash** — it neutralizes residual H₂SO₄ and HNO₃. **Penalty: –0.5 mark** in work-up explanation.
> 7. **Reporting % yield > 100 %** without identifying the error — this is a **red flag for incomplete drying** (residual water in the product inflates the mass). **Penalty: –2 marks** plus mandatory re-experiment.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Nitration = Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS)**, not nucleophilic.
- [x] **True electrophile = NO₂⁺ (nitronium ion)**, generated *in situ* from HNO₃ + H₂SO₄.
- [x] **Three-step mechanism:** (1) Electrophile generation, (2) σ-complex formation (RDS, endothermic), (3) Deprotonation (exothermic, fast).
- [x] **H₂SO₄ plays TWO roles:** proton donor AND dehydrating agent.
- [x] **Temperature window:** 50–60 °C (benzene), 30–40 °C (toluene), 0–10 °C (phenol) — lower for more activated rings.
- [x] **Toluene → ortho (60 %) + para (40 %)**; *meta* < 5 % — verifies directing effect of –CH₃.
- [x] **Formula to memorize:** % Yield = (Actual / Theoretical) × 100, with theoretical yield = $n_{LR} \times M_{product}$.
- [x] **Always apply purity (assay) correction** for HNO₃ before mole calculation.
- [x] **Steam distillation** removes unreacted benzene; NaHCO₃ wash removes residual acid; Na₂SO₄ drying removes water.
- [x] **End products of nitration** feed into: aniline → dyes, pharmaceuticals, polyurethanes, explosives (TNT, picric acid).
- [x] **Examiner's traps:** misidentifying LR, omitting units, claiming HNO₃ is the electrophile, reporting % yield > 100 %.
- [x] **Standard KTU lab yield benchmark for benzene nitration:** 70–85 %.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Side chain oxidation of aromatic hydrocarbons
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Side chain oxidation of aromatic hydrocarbons

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Side chain oxidation of aromatic hydrocarbons** is a classic organic redox reaction in which an alkyl substituent (the side chain) attached to a benzene ring is selectively oxidized to a carboxylic acid group (–COOH), while the aromatic ring itself remains completely intact and unaltered. The most commonly studied laboratory example is the oxidation of **toluene (methylbenzene)** to **benzoic acid** using hot alkaline **potassium permanganate (KMnO₄)** as the oxidizing agent.

The reaction is a heterogeneous reaction because toluene (organic, immiscible with water) is oxidized by an aqueous alkaline solution of KMnO₄, and vigorous stirring under reflux is required to bring the two phases into effective contact.

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4, \text{OH}^-, \Delta} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH} $$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is a mandatory qualitative/quantitative synthesis under **Module Experiment 2** of PCPOL307. Students are expected to perform the synthesis, isolate the solid acid product, recrystallize it, and report its **melting point** (theoretical value for benzoic acid = **122 °C**) and **percentage yield**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the benzene ring as a sturdy brick house that is fire-resistant. The side chain (–CH₃) is like a wooden ladder leaning against the house. If a forest fire (the strong oxidizer KMnO₄) sweeps through, the ladder burns completely away — but the brick house (aromatic ring) survives untouched. The carbon of the ladder that was directly attached to the house gets converted into a permanent chemical "handle" — the **–COOH** group — useful for further chemistry.

In simple words:
- **The aromatic ring is the survivor.**
- **The side chain is the sacrificial fuel.**
- **The product is an aromatic acid (a useful "handle" for further reactions).**

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does the ring survive?** Because aromaticity imparts an extraordinary **resonance stabilization energy** of approximately **150 kJ/mol (≈ 36 kcal/mol)**. KMnO₄ under these conditions cannot break this aromatic sextet.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Oxidizer used** | Alkaline KMnO₄ (typically 0.5–1.0 M) |
| **Reaction temperature** | Reflux (~100 °C, oil bath at 110–120 °C optional) |
| **Reaction time** | 2–4 hours under reflux |
| **Melting point of benzoic acid** | **122 °C** (literature, pure) |
| **Molar mass of toluene** | **92.14 g/mol** |
| **Molar mass of benzoic acid** | **122.12 g/mol** |
| **Theoretical yield (per gram of toluene)** | ≈ 1.325 g of benzoic acid |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Stepwise Breakdown

The side chain oxidation proceeds through a well-defined mechanistic cascade. The following bullets capture each operational step:

- **Step 1 — Activation of benzylic C–H bond:**
  The methyl group (–CH₃) of toluene has **benzylic hydrogens**. Because the C–H bond adjacent to an aromatic ring is weaker (~88 kcal/mol) than a primary aliphatic C–H (~98 kcal/mol), it is preferentially attacked by the oxidant.

- **Step 2 — Sequential oxidation to alcohol → aldehyde → acid:**
  The methyl group is not oxidized in one jump. It is oxidized progressively: **toluene → benzyl alcohol → benzaldehyde → benzoic acid**. The aldehyde stage is normally bypassed because aldehydes are more easily oxidized than the parent hydrocarbon; they rarely accumulate.

- **Step 3 — Base-catalyzed hydrolysis of intermediate esters:**
  In alkaline medium, the aldehyde intermediate undergoes **Cannizzaro-like disproportionation** and the acid formed immediately reacts with OH⁻ to form water-soluble **potassium benzoate (C₆H₅COO⁻K⁺)**.

- **Step 4 — Acidification to liberate the free acid:**
  After the reaction, the alkaline filtrate is cooled and acidified with dilute **HCl or H₂SO₄** (added drop-wise under stirring). At pH < 2, potassium benzoate is protonated to give insoluble benzoic acid, which precipitates as a white solid.

- **Step 5 — Purification by recrystallization:**
  The crude solid is dissolved in a minimum volume of hot water (boiling point method), filtered hot to remove insoluble impurities, and allowed to crystallize slowly on cooling. Needle-shaped white crystals of pure benzoic acid are obtained.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind the Selectivity

The aromatic ring is left untouched because **aromatic π-electrons are delocalized in a 6-electron aromatic sextet (Hückel's rule, 4n + 2 with n = 1)**. Mild oxidants like KMnO₄ cannot disrupt this aromaticity under aqueous alkaline conditions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU point:** Any side chain, regardless of its length (ethyl, propyl, isopropyl, tert-butyl), is degraded down to the **–COOH** group attached directly to the ring. This is because oxidation continues until a carbon directly bonded to the aromatic ring is fully oxidized.

Examples:

$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH}_2\text{CH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–COOH} $$
$$ \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH(CH}_3)_2 \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–COOH} $$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering / Laboratory Utility |
|---|---|---|
| **Stoichiometric oxidant balance** | $2\,\text{MnO}_4^- + \text{H}_2\text{O} + 3e^- \rightarrow 2\,\text{MnO}_2 + 2\,\text{OH}^-$ | Used to calculate minimum amount of KMnO₄ needed |
| **Side chain to acid conversion** | Ar–R → Ar–COOH (R is any alkyl) | Industrial synthesis of benzoic acid, phthalic acid, terephthalic acid |
| **Hückel's Aromaticity Rule** | $4n+2$ π-electrons (n = 1, 2, 3...) | Explains why ring is not oxidized |
| **Percentage yield formula** | $\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual Yield}}{\text{Theoretical Yield}} \times 100$ | Mandatory for lab record evaluation |
| **Theoretical yield (g)** | $\dfrac{m_{\text{toluene}}}{M_{\text{toluene}}} \times M_{\text{benzoic acid}}$ | Used to grade quantitative experiments |
| **Melting point of benzoic acid** | **122 °C** | Indicator of product purity |
| **Recrystallization solvent** | Hot water (polar protic) | Removes ionic and organic impurities |

> [!WARNING]
> **Do NOT use absolute values like |x| in markdown tables.** The vertical pipe breaks the table syntax. In LaTeX, use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert`.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Balanced Chemical Equation

The full balanced oxidation–reduction equation for the conversion of toluene to benzoic acid by alkaline permanganate must be derived in three stages.

### Step 1 — Identify oxidation states

In **toluene (C₆H₅CH₃)**, the benzylic carbon of the –CH₃ group has an oxidation state of **–3**.

In **benzoic acid (C₆H₅COOH)**, the corresponding carbon of the –COOH group has an oxidation state of **+3**.

**Change in oxidation state per benzylic carbon:**

$$
\Delta(\text{oxidation state}) = (+3) - (-3) = +6 \text{ electrons lost per toluene molecule}
$$

### Step 2 — Permanganate half-reaction (reduction)

In alkaline medium, MnO₄⁻ is reduced from Mn(+7) to Mn(+4) as MnO₂:

$$
\text{MnO}_4^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3e^- \longrightarrow \text{MnO}_2 + 4\,\text{OH}^-
$$

Each permanganate ion accepts **3 electrons**.

### Step 3 — Balance the electrons

Since toluene loses 6 electrons and each MnO₄⁻ accepts 3 electrons, we need **2 permanganate ions per toluene molecule**.

**Overall balanced equation:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 + 2\,\text{MnO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^- + 2\,\text{MnO}_2 + \text{OH}^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

After acidification with HCl:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^- + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}
$$

### Step 4 — Verification of atom and charge balance

| Atom | Left side | Right side |
|---|---|---|
| C | 7 | 7 |
| H | 5 + 3 = 8 | 5 + 1 + 2 + 2 = 10 (acid side correct) |
| Mn | 2 | 2 |
| O | 8 | 2 + 4 + 1 + 2 = 9 (balanced with H⁺ on acidification) |
| Charge | –2 | –2 |

> [!NOTE]
> The above derivation is the **core reduction-balance derivation** expected in KTU viva-voce and Part B (14-mark) questions. Memorize the change in oxidation state (+6) and MnO₄⁻ uptake (3 electrons).

## 3.2 Derivation of Theoretical Yield

Suppose a student is given **2.0 mL of toluene** (density ≈ **0.867 g/mL**).

**Step A — Mass of toluene used:**

$$
m_{\text{toluene}} = V \times \rho = 2.0\,\text{mL} \times 0.867\,\text{g/mL} = 1.734\,\text{g}
$$

**Step B — Moles of toluene:**

$$
n_{\text{toluene}} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{1.734\,\text{g}}{92.14\,\text{g/mol}} = 0.01882\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step C — Moles of benzoic acid (1:1 stoichiometry):**

$$
n_{\text{benzoic acid}} = 0.01882\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step D — Theoretical mass of benzoic acid:**

$$
m_{\text{theoretical}} = n \times M = 0.01882\,\text{mol} \times 122.12\,\text{g/mol} = 2.299\,\text{g}
$$

**Step E — If actual yield obtained = 1.5 g, percentage yield is:**

$$
\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{1.5}{2.299} \times 100 = 65.24\,\%
$$

## 3.3 Complete Mechanism (For Advanced Understanding)

The mechanism proceeds via a free-radical pathway at the benzylic position. Although KTU expects only the summary, here is the complete pathway:

- **Initiation:** Permanganate abstracts a benzylic H·, generating a benzyl radical **C₆H₅–CH₂·**.
- **Propagation:** The benzyl radical reacts with oxygen/water to form benzyl alcohol **C₆H₅–CH₂OH**.
- **Further oxidation:** Benzyl alcohol → benzaldehyde → benzoic acid (each step removes 2 electrons).
- **Termination:** Carboxylate ion is stabilized as C₆H₅–COO⁻ in alkaline medium.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow of the Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Reagent Setup: Toluene + KMnO4 + NaOH] --> B[Round Bottom Flask Assembly]
    B --> C[Reflux for 2 to 4 hours at 100 degrees C]
    C --> D{Observation: Purple KMnO4 fades to brown MnO2}
    D -->|Color persists| E[Add more KMnO4 pellets and continue reflux]
    D -->|Color discharged| F[Cool and filter to remove MnO2]
    F --> G[Cool filtrate in ice bath]
    G --> H[Acidify with dilute HCl to pH less than 2]
    H --> I{White precipitate appears}
    I -->|Yes| J[Filter and wash crude benzoic acid]
    J --> K[Recrystallize from hot water]
    K --> L[Dry crystals in oven below 80 degrees C]
    L --> M[Weigh and record melting point]
    M --> N{MP near 122 degrees C}
    N -->|Yes| O[Pure Benzoic Acid - Product Confirmed]
    N -->|No| P[Repeat recrystallization]
```

## 4.2 Side Chain Oxidation Transformation Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Toluene C6H5CH3] -->|Step 1: Oxidation| B[Benzyl Alcohol C6H5CH2OH]
    B -->|Step 2: Oxidation| C[Benzaldehyde C6H5CHO]
    C -->|Step 3: Oxidation| D[Benzoic Acid C6H5COOH]
    D -->|Step 4: Neutralization with NaOH| E[Sodium Benzoate C6H5COONa]
    E -->|Step 5: Acidification with HCl| F[Pure Benzoic Acid Crystals]
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Reaction Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Reagents
        R1[Toluene - organic phase]
        R2[Alkaline KMnO4 - aqueous phase]
    end
    subgraph Reaction_Zone
        Z1[Reflux Condenser]
        Z2[Stirring and Heating Mantle]
    end
    subgraph Workup
        W1[Filtration to remove MnO2]
        W2[Acidification with HCl]
        W3[Recrystallization]
    end
    subgraph Product
        P1[Pure Benzoic Acid Crystals]
    end
    R1 --> Z1
    R2 --> Z1
    Z1 --> Z2
    Z2 --> W1
    W1 --> W2
    W2 --> W3
    W3 --> P1
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid flowcharts instead of physical molecular drawings?** A flowchart captures every operational stage of the lab procedure, which is the analytical requirement of a KTU lab record. Physical molecular structures (benzene rings with –CH₃ or –COOH) are better drawn by the student on the lab record itself using standard skeletal formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. Define side chain oxidation of aromatic hydrocarbons with a suitable example. Mention the role of alkaline KMnO₄. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
Side chain oxidation is the process in which an alkyl substituent attached to an aromatic ring is oxidized to a carboxylic acid (–COOH) group while the aromatic ring remains intact. For example, toluene (methylbenzene) is oxidized to benzoic acid by alkaline potassium permanganate. Alkaline KMnO₄ acts as a strong oxidizing agent that selectively attacks the benzylic C–H bonds, which are weaker than aliphatic C–H bonds due to resonance stabilization of the resulting benzyl radical. The aromatic ring is protected from oxidation by its resonance stabilization energy of approximately 150 kJ/mol.

### Question 2. Why is the aromatic ring not oxidized during side chain oxidation? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The aromatic ring is preserved because of its **aromatic stabilization energy** (≈ 150 kJ/mol) arising from the delocalized 6 π-electron system following **Hückel's rule (4n + 2 with n = 1)**. KMnO₄ in alkaline medium at 100 °C provides insufficient energy to disrupt this aromatic sextet. Hence, only the more reactive benzylic C–H bonds of the side chain are oxidized.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**(a)** Write the balanced chemical equation for the side chain oxidation of toluene to benzoic acid using alkaline KMnO₄. Derive the electron balance. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The unbalanced transformation is:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}
$$

**Oxidation half-reaction (benzylic carbon):** In toluene, the benzylic carbon has an oxidation state of –3. In benzoic acid, the corresponding carboxyl carbon has an oxidation state of +3. Hence each toluene molecule loses 6 electrons:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^- + 7\,\text{H}^+ + 6e^-
$$

**Reduction half-reaction (permanganate in alkaline medium):**

$$
\text{MnO}_4^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3e^- \longrightarrow \text{MnO}_2 + 4\,\text{OH}^-
$$

**Balancing the electrons:** Multiply the reduction half-reaction by 2 to consume 6 electrons:

$$
2\,\text{MnO}_4^- + 4\,\text{H}_2\text{O} + 6e^- \longrightarrow 2\,\text{MnO}_2 + 8\,\text{OH}^-
$$

**Adding both half-reactions:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 + 2\,\text{MnO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^- + 2\,\text{MnO}_2 + \text{OH}^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

This is the fully balanced equation in alkaline medium. On acidification:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^- + \text{H}^+ \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOH}
$$

**(b)** Starting from 2 mL of toluene (density = 0.867 g/mL), calculate the theoretical yield of benzoic acid. If the actual yield obtained is 1.45 g, calculate the percentage yield. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Mass of toluene:**

$$
m_{\text{toluene}} = 2\,\text{mL} \times 0.867\,\text{g/mL} = 1.734\,\text{g}
$$

**Step 2 — Moles of toluene:**

$$
n_{\text{toluene}} = \dfrac{1.734}{92.14} = 0.01882\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Moles of benzoic acid (1:1 stoichiometry):**

$$
n_{\text{benzoic acid}} = 0.01882\,\text{mol}
$$

**Step 4 — Theoretical mass of benzoic acid:**

$$
m_{\text{theoretical}} = 0.01882 \times 122.12 = 2.299\,\text{g}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage yield:**

$$
\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{1.45}{2.299} \times 100 = 63.07\,\%
$$

The theoretical yield is **2.30 g** and the percentage yield is approximately **63.1%**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**(a)** Describe the procedure for the side chain oxidation of toluene to benzoic acid in the laboratory. Mention the role of each reagent. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Apparatus Required:** Round-bottom flask (250 mL), reflux condenser, heating mantle, mechanical stirrer, Buchner funnel, suction pump, ice bath, watch glass.

**Reagents Required:** Toluene (2 mL), potassium permanganate (excess, ~6 g), sodium hydroxide pellets (~1 g), distilled water (100 mL), dilute hydrochloric acid, ice.

**Procedure:**

1. Place 2 mL of toluene in a 250 mL round-bottom flask. Add 100 mL of distilled water and 1 g of NaOH pellets.
2. Add 6 g of finely powdered KMnO₄ in small portions with shaking to prevent frothing.
3. Fit a reflux condenser and heat the mixture under reflux with continuous stirring for **2–4 hours** in a heating mantle. The purple color of KMnO₄ gradually fades to a brown suspension of MnO₂, indicating reaction progress.
4. If the purple color persists, add small additional portions of KMnO₄ and continue reflux.
5. Cool the reaction mixture and filter through a Buchner funnel to remove MnO₂. Wash the residue with hot water.
6. Cool the combined filtrate (which contains potassium benzoate) in an ice bath.
7. Slowly acidify with dilute HCl with constant stirring until pH < 2 (test with litmus paper). White crystalline benzoic acid precipitates.
8. Filter the product, wash with cold water, and dry.
9. Recrystallize from hot distilled water to obtain pure needle-shaped crystals of benzoic acid.
10. Determine the melting point (literature value **122 °C**).

**Role of Each Reagent:**

| Reagent | Role |
|---|---|
| Toluene | Substrate (the hydrocarbon to be oxidized) |
| KMnO₄ | Oxidizing agent (Mn⁷⁺ → Mn⁴⁺) |
| NaOH | Provides alkaline medium; converts benzoic acid to water-soluble benzoate |
| Dilute HCl | Liberates free benzoic acid from its salt |
| Hot water | Recrystallization solvent |

**(b)** Explain why side chain oxidation always produces the same acid regardless of the alkyl chain length. Illustrate with two examples. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Side chain oxidation proceeds until the carbon **directly bonded to the aromatic ring** is fully oxidized to a carboxylic acid (–COOH) group. The oxidant does not stop at intermediate stages (such as aldehyde or ketone) because these intermediates are themselves more readily oxidized than the parent alkyl group. Hence, regardless of whether the side chain is methyl (–CH₃), ethyl (–CH₂CH₃), or isopropyl (–CH(CH₃)₂), the final product is the same aromatic acid.

**Example 1 — Ethylbenzene to Benzoic Acid:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH}_2\text{CH}_3 \xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4, \text{OH}^-} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–COOH}
$$

The β-carbon (CH₃ end) is cleaved off, and the α-carbon (directly attached to the ring) is oxidized to –COOH.

**Example 2 — Cumene (Isopropylbenzene) to Benzoic Acid:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–CH(CH}_3)_2 \xrightarrow{\text{KMnO}_4, \text{OH}^-} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{–COOH}
$$

Both methyl groups of the isopropyl moiety are removed, leaving only the carboxyl carbon attached to the ring.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Students often write the **half-balanced** equation without MnO₂ on the product side. Always show the **complete balanced equation with MnO₂** in alkaline medium. Also, students forget that the aromatic ring must be drawn explicitly in the answer — drawing just "PhCH₃ → PhCOOH" without showing the ring costs 1–2 marks.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** Side chain oxidation converts an alkyl group on an aromatic ring into a –COOH group, while the ring stays intact.
- **Standard reagent:** Hot **alkaline KMnO₄** under reflux.
- **Ring survival:** Protected by **aromaticity** (Hückel 4n + 2 rule; resonance energy ≈ 150 kJ/mol).
- **Mechanistic sequence:** Toluene → Benzyl alcohol → Benzaldehyde → Benzoic acid.
- **Benzylic C–H bond:** Weaker (~88 kcal/mol) than primary aliphatic C–H (~98 kcal/mol), hence preferentially oxidized.
- **All alkyl chains give same acid:** Ethyl, propyl, isopropyl, tert-butyl all yield **benzoic acid**.
- **Workup steps:** Filter MnO₂ → acidify filtrate with HCl → filter benzoic acid → recrystallize from hot water.
- **Purity check:** Melting point must equal **122 °C**.
- **Yield formula:** Theoretical yield = (m_toluene / 92.14) × 122.12.
- **Industrial relevance:** This reaction is the prototype for industrial production of **benzoic acid, phthalic acid, and terephthalic acid** (the latter being a monomer of PET polyester).
- **Final balanced equation (must memorize):**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{CH}_3 + 2\,\text{MnO}_4^- \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COO}^- + 2\,\text{MnO}_2 + \text{OH}^- + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Benzoylation of phenols

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Benzoylation of Phenols** is a classic organic synthesis experiment based on the **Schotten–Baumann reaction**, wherein a phenolic hydroxyl group (–OH) is acylated with **benzoyl chloride (C₆H₅COCl)** in the presence of an **aqueous alkali (typically 5–10% NaOH)** to yield the corresponding **aromatic ester (phenyl benzoate)** along with NaCl and H₂O as by-products.

The general reaction is represented as:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCl} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH (aq.)}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOC}_6\text{H}_5 + \text{HCl}
$$

The liberated HCl is neutralized immediately by NaOH, driving the equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's Principle) and preventing the reverse hydrolysis of the ester.

> [!NOTE]
> The reaction belongs to the class of **Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution (NAS)** reactions, where the phenoxide ion (PhO⁻) generated *in situ* acts as the nucleophile and attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of benzoyl chloride.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real World Analogy

Imagine the phenol molecule as a **"lock"** with a reactive hydrogen key sitting in its –OH slot. Benzoyl chloride is like a **"key-maker"** — it has a carbonyl carbon that desperately wants to bond with a nucleophile. Sodium hydroxide plays the role of a **"facilitator/middleman"** that:

1. **Pulls the hydrogen off** the phenol (deprotonation) → makes the oxygen more electron-rich and aggressive (nucleophilic).
2. **Neutralizes any acid formed** (HCl) during the reaction → keeps the system clean.

The result? The phenol oxygen **snaps onto the carbonyl carbon** of benzoyl chloride, displacing the chloride leaving group, forming a beautiful, sweet-smelling aromatic ester — **phenyl benzoate**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this matter in engineering?** Esters are foundational to:
> - **Polymer industry** (polyesters, PET, polycarbonate plastics)
> - **Pharmaceuticals** (aspirin is the acetylated cousin of this reaction)
> - **Perfumery & flavoring** (benzoate esters are fruity/aromatic)
> - **Dye intermediates** and UV stabilizers

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Property | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| Molecular weight of phenol | **94.11 g/mol** | Limiting reagent calc. |
| Molecular weight of benzoyl chloride | **140.57 g/mol** | Reagent dosage |
| Molecular weight of phenyl benzoate (product) | **198.22 g/mol** | Theoretical yield calc. |
| Density of benzoyl chloride | **1.21 g/mL** | Volume-to-mass conversion |
| Melting point of pure phenyl benzoate | **69–70 °C** | Purity check |
| Typical NaOH concentration | **5–10% (w/v)** | Reaction medium |
| Reaction temperature | **Room temperature (25–30 °C)** | Exothermic control |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Breakdown of the Schotten–Baumann Mechanism

The mechanism proceeds through the **addition–elimination (tetrahedral intermediate)** pathway typical of acyl halides.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NaOH (aq.) and not just water or pyridine alone?**
> Because phenols are weakly acidic (pKa ≈ 10), a strong base is required to convert them into the more nucleophilic phenoxide ion (PhO⁻). The aqueous NaOH simultaneously quenches HCl, preventing ester hydrolysis.

### Step 1 — Deprotonation of Phenol

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- \text{Na}^+ + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

The phenoxide ion is a **stronger nucleophile** than neutral phenol due to its negative charge (greater electron density on oxygen).

### Step 2 — Nucleophilic Attack on Carbonyl Carbon

The lone pair on the phenoxide oxygen attacks the **electrophilic carbonyl carbon** of benzoyl chloride, forming a **tetrahedral intermediate**.

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{O}^- + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\overset{\displaystyle O}{\overset{\displaystyle \|}{C}}-\text{Cl} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\overset{\displaystyle O^-}{\overset{\displaystyle \mid}{C}}(\text{OC}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{Cl}
$$

### Step 3 — Elimination of Chloride (Leaving Group Departure)

The tetrahedral intermediate collapses, expelling Cl⁻ (a good leaving group) and reforming the C=O double bond.

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\overset{\displaystyle O^-}{\overset{\displaystyle \mid}{C}}(\text{OC}_6\text{H}_5)-\text{Cl} \longrightarrow \text{C}_6\text{H}_5-\overset{\displaystyle O}{\overset{\displaystyle \|}{C}}-\text{OC}_6\text{H}_5 + \text{Cl}^-
$$

### Step 4 — Neutralization of By-products

The expelled Cl⁻ combines with Na⁺; any residual HCl formed reacts with excess NaOH.

$$
\text{Cl}^- + \text{Na}^+ \longrightarrow \text{NaCl}
$$
$$
\text{HCl} + \text{NaOH} \longrightarrow \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are critical for the yield calculation part of your KTU lab record and viva.**

| Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Moles of limiting reagent | $n = \dfrac{m}{M}$ | mol | m = mass, M = molar mass |
| Theoretical yield (mass) | $m_{\text{theo}} = n_{\text{limiting}} \times M_{\text{product}}$ | g | Stoichiometry = 1:1 |
| Percentage yield | $\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100$ | % | Should be 60–85% |
| Volume from density | $V = \dfrac{m}{\rho}$ | mL | For liquid benzoyl chloride |
| Molarity (if required) | $M = \dfrac{n}{V_{(L)}}$ | mol/L | For NaOH solution |
| Rf value (TLC, optional) | $R_f = \dfrac{\text{distance travelled by spot}}{\text{distance travelled by solvent front}}$ | dimensionless | Identity check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Stoichiometry Reminder:** The reaction between phenol and benzoyl chloride is **1:1**. Therefore, the reagent present in **smaller molar quantity** limits the product. In typical KTU lab manuals, **phenol is the limiting reagent** because benzoyl chloride is taken in slight molar excess (~10%).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry / Field | Application of Benzoylation Chemistry |
|---|---|
| **Polymer Engineering** | Synthesis of aromatic polyesters (e.g., PET, PEN) — analogous chemistry |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Aspirin synthesis is acetylation; benzoylation yields benzoate prodrugs |
| **Cosmetics / Perfumery** | Phenyl benzoate has a mild floral/balsamic odor |
| **Dye Chemistry** | Aroyl groups act as protecting groups for phenols during multi-step syntheses |
| **Food Industry** | Benzoate esters (e.g., methylparaben) used as preservatives |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Standard KTU Lab Procedure (with Rationale at Each Step)

| Step | Action | Quantity | Rationale (Why) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Take phenol in a conical flask | **1.0 g (≈ 0.0106 mol)** | Limiting reagent for yield calc |
| 2 | Add 10% NaOH solution | **20 mL** | Dissolves phenol, generates phenoxide |
| 3 | Add benzoyl chloride in installments | **1.5 mL (≈ 1.82 g, 0.0129 mol)** | ~20% molar excess to ensure complete conversion |
| 4 | Cork and shake vigorously | **15–20 min** | Mixing; benzoyl chloride is immiscible with water — shaking emulsifies it |
| 5 | Observe white crystalline precipitate | — | Phenyl benzoate precipitates (insoluble in water) |
| 6 | Filter, wash with cold water | — | Removes NaCl, NaOH, unreacted phenol |
| 7 | Recrystallize from hot ethanol | — | Purification to sharp m.p. |
| 8 | Dry and weigh | — | For yield calculation |
| 9 | Determine melting point | — | Identity & purity check (literature: 69 °C) |

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of Percentage Yield

**Given:**
- Mass of phenol taken: m(phenol) = 1.0 g
- Molar mass of phenol: M(phenol) = 94.11 g/mol
- Molar mass of benzoyl chloride: M(BzCl) = 140.57 g/mol
- Density of benzoyl chloride: ρ = 1.21 g/mL
- Volume of benzoyl chloride: V = 1.5 mL
- Molar mass of phenyl benzoate: M(product) = 198.22 g/mol
- Actual mass of dry product obtained: m(actual) = 1.40 g *(example data)*

**Step 1 — Calculate moles of phenol (limiting reagent)**

$$
n(\text{phenol}) = \frac{m(\text{phenol})}{M(\text{phenol})} = \frac{1.0 \text{ g}}{94.11 \text{ g/mol}}
$$

$$
n(\text{phenol}) = 0.01063 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate mass and moles of benzoyl chloride**

$$
m(\text{BzCl}) = \rho \times V = 1.21 \text{ g/mL} \times 1.5 \text{ mL} = 1.815 \text{ g}
$$

$$
n(\text{BzCl}) = \frac{1.815 \text{ g}}{140.57 \text{ g/mol}} = 0.01291 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Identify the limiting reagent**

Since the stoichiometry is 1:1, and:

$$
n(\text{phenol}) = 0.01063 \text{ mol} \; < \; 0.01291 \text{ mol} = n(\text{BzCl})
$$

Therefore, **phenol is the limiting reagent** (smaller moles).

**Step 4 — Calculate theoretical yield of phenyl benzoate**

$$
m_{\text{theo}} = n(\text{limiting}) \times M(\text{product})
$$

$$
m_{\text{theo}} = 0.01063 \text{ mol} \times 198.22 \text{ g/mol}
$$

$$
m_{\text{theo}} = 2.107 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Calculate percentage yield**

$$
\% \text{ Yield} = \frac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theoretical}}} \times 100
$$

$$
\% \text{ Yield} = \frac{1.40 \text{ g}}{2.107 \text{ g}} \times 100 = 66.4\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A **good practical yield** for this KTU lab experiment ranges between **60% and 80%**. Yields below 50% suggest procedural errors (insufficient shaking, impure reagents, loss during recrystallization, or product hydrolysis from inadequate NaOH).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Pitfalls and Their Numerical Signatures

| Observed Issue | Likely Cause | Diagnostic Step |
|---|---|---|
| Low yield + oily residue | Insufficient shaking; benzoyl chloride droplets trapped | Increase shaking to 20 min, check cork seal |
| Very low m.p. (broad, < 60 °C) | Incomplete recrystallization; impurities | Re-recrystallize; use activated charcoal |
| High m.p. (> 75 °C) | Co-crystallization with unreacted BzCl or benzoic acid | Wash product with cold NaHCO₃ then water |
| Sticky / gelatinous product | Phenol not fully deprotonated; reaction in acidic medium | Verify NaOH concentration is fresh (≥ 10%) |
| No precipitate at all | Excess water; product remains dissolved; or reagent hydrolysis | Use ice-water after reaction to crash out product |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — Complete Reaction Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Weigh 1.0 g phenol] --> B[Add 20 mL of 10 percent NaOH solution]
    B --> C[Phenol dissolves to form sodium phenoxide]
    C --> D[Add 1.5 mL benzoyl chloride in 3 portions]
    D --> E[Cork flask and shake vigorously for 15 to 20 minutes]
    E --> F[White crystalline phenyl benzoate precipitates]
    F --> G[Add crushed ice to complete precipitation]
    G --> H[Filter using Buchner funnel and suction pump]
    H --> I[Wash residue with cold distilled water]
    I --> J[Recrystallize from hot ethanol]
    J --> K[Filter and air dry the pure crystals]
    K --> L[Weigh product and record mass]
    L --> M[Determine melting point using capillary tube method]
    M --> N{Pure product?}
    N -->|Yes| O[Record yield: compare with theoretical 2.107 g]
    N -->|No| P[Re-recrystallize and repeat drying]
    P --> M
    O --> Q[End: Calculate percentage yield and submit report]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanism Overview

```mermaid

        I1[Phenoxide Ion C6H5O-]

    end

```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution Steps

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: NaOH deprotonates phenol] --> S2[Forms phenoxide ion and water]
    S2 --> S3[Step 2: Phenoxide attacks carbonyl carbon of benzoyl chloride]
    S3 --> S4[Step 3: Tetrahedral intermediate forms with negative oxygen]
    S4 --> S5[Step 4: Chloride ion leaves, reforming C double bond O]
    S5 --> S6[Step 5: Phenyl benzoate formed, NaOH neutralizes residual HCl]
    S6 --> S7[Step 6: Product precipitates as white solid]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block-Level Architecture Note:** The above Mermaid diagrams represent the **functional flow** of both the laboratory procedure and the underlying mechanism. For KTU records, students are expected to draw the **curved-arrow electron-pushing mechanism** by hand in the observation section — this carries significant marks in the practical exam.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**
**What is the Schotten–Baumann reaction? Give the equation for the benzoylation of phenol.**

**Model Answer:**
The Schotten–Baumann reaction is the acylation of an alcohol or phenol using an acyl halide (or anhydride) in the presence of an aqueous alkali (typically NaOH) as a base to neutralize the liberated HCl. The aqueous medium and base prevent the reverse hydrolysis of the ester product.

For benzoylation of phenol:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCl} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH (aq.)}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOC}_6\text{H}_5 + \text{HCl}
$$

The HCl formed is immediately neutralized by excess NaOH to NaCl + H₂O, driving the reaction to completion. **[3 marks]**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
**Why is aqueous NaOH used instead of pyridine in the Schotten–Baumann reaction?**

**Model Answer:**
1. NaOH serves a **dual role** — it deprotonates phenol to form the more nucleophilic phenoxide ion *and* neutralizes the HCl by-product, shifting the equilibrium forward (Le Chatelier's principle).
2. The aqueous medium allows easy separation of the water-insoluble ester product (phenyl benzoate) by simple filtration.
3. Pyridine, although a useful base, is more expensive, toxic, and offers no advantage for a simple phenolic substrate. The aqueous NaOH work-up is cheaper, greener, and industrially scalable. **[3 marks]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Do NOT** confuse benzoylation with benzylation (the latter adds –CH₂C₆H₅, not –COC₆H₅).
> - Always show the **mechanism with curved arrows** — KTU examiners award 4–5 marks specifically for the mechanism.
> - **Stoichiometric calculation mistakes** (using wrong molar mass, swapping limiting reagent) cost 2–3 marks — double-check before writing.
> - In the **product drawing**, explicitly label the **ester linkage (–C(=O)–O–)**.
> - The role of NaOH is **twofold** (deprotonation + HCl scavenging) — students who mention only one lose 1 mark.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[(a) — 7 Marks]** With a suitable mechanism, explain the benzoylation of phenol. Why is the reaction carried out in an aqueous alkaline medium?

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Introduction [1 mark]:**
Benzoylation of phenol is a Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution (NAS) reaction in which the –OH group of phenol is acylated by benzoyl chloride (C₆H₅COCl) in the presence of aqueous NaOH to yield phenyl benzoate (C₆H₅COOC₆H₅). It is a classical example of the **Schotten–Baumann reaction**.

**(ii) Balanced Equation [1 mark]:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{OH} + \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COCl} \xrightarrow{\text{NaOH (aq.)}} \text{C}_6\text{H}_5\text{COOC}_6\text{H}_5 + \text{NaCl} + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**(iii) Mechanism [4 marks]:**

**Step 1 — Deprotonation:**
NaOH removes the acidic phenolic proton to generate the resonance-stabilized phenoxide ion (PhO⁻), which is a far stronger nucleophile than neutral phenol.

$$
\text{PhOH} + \text{OH}^- \rightarrow \text{PhO}^- + \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

**Step 2 — Nucleophilic addition:**
The phenoxide oxygen's lone pair attacks the electrophilic carbonyl carbon of benzoyl chloride, forming a tetrahedral alkoxide intermediate.

**Step 3 — Elimination:**
The intermediate collapses; Cl⁻ departs as a stable leaving group and the C=O double bond is reformed, yielding phenyl benzoate.

**Step 4 — Acid scavenging:**
The expelled Cl⁻ and any HCl generated are neutralized by excess NaOH to NaCl + H₂O.

**(iv) Role of Aqueous Alkali [1 mark]:**
- Phenol is only weakly acidic; NaOH converts it quantitatively to phenoxide, increasing nucleophilicity.
- The aqueous medium allows the water-insoluble ester to precipitate cleanly.
- NaOH scavenges HCl, preventing reverse hydrolysis of the ester (Le Chatelier).

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

**[(b) — 7 Marks]** In a benzoylation experiment, 1.0 g of phenol was treated with 1.5 mL of benzoyl chloride (density 1.21 g/mL) in 20 mL of 10% NaOH. After recrystallization, 1.45 g of pure phenyl benzoate was obtained. Calculate the percentage yield and identify the limiting reagent.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Moles of phenol [1 mark]:**

$$
n(\text{PhOH}) = \frac{1.0}{94.11} = 0.01063 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass and moles of benzoyl chloride [1 mark]:**

$$
m(\text{BzCl}) = 1.21 \times 1.5 = 1.815 \text{ g}
$$

$$
n(\text{BzCl}) = \frac{1.815}{140.57} = 0.01291 \text{ mol}
$$

**Step 3 — Limiting reagent [1 mark]:**
Since stoichiometry is 1:1 and n(PhOH) = 0.01063 mol < n(BzCl) = 0.01291 mol, **phenol is the limiting reagent**.

**Step 4 — Theoretical yield [2 marks]:**

$$
m_{\text{theo}} = 0.01063 \times 198.22 = 2.107 \text{ g}
$$

**Step 5 — Percentage yield [2 marks]:**

$$
\% \text{ Yield} = \frac{1.45}{2.107} \times 100 = 68.8\%
$$

The percentage yield is **68.8%**, which is within the acceptable range (60–80%) for this experiment. **[Total: 7 marks]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[(a) — 7 Marks]** Describe the laboratory procedure for the benzoylation of phenol. What safety precautions must be observed?

**Model Answer:**

**Procedure [5 marks]:**

1. Weigh **1.0 g of phenol** in a clean 100 mL conical flask.
2. Add **20 mL of 10% aqueous NaOH** and swirl to dissolve the phenol completely (solution becomes clear).
3. Measure **1.5 mL of benzoyl chloride** in a dry measuring cylinder (it fumes in moist air and hydrolyses).
4. Add benzoyl chloride to the flask in **3 portions of 0.5 mL each**, corking the flask tightly after each addition.
5. **Shake the flask vigorously** for 15–20 minutes, releasing the pressure occasionally (CO₂ may build up if traces of carbonate are present).
6. A **white crystalline mass** of phenyl benzoate separates.
7. Add **20 mL of ice-cold water** to ensure complete precipitation.
8. Filter through a **Buchner funnel** under suction; wash the residue with cold water (3 × 10 mL) to remove NaCl and NaOH.
9. **Recrystallize** the crude product from a minimum volume of hot ethanol.
10. Filter the recrystallized product, dry it, weigh it, and determine its **melting point** (literature: 69 °C).

**Safety Precautions [2 marks]:**
- Benzoyl chloride is a **lachrymator** (causes tears) and corrosive — handle inside a **fume hood**.
- Phenol is **toxic and corrosive** to skin — wear gloves; do not inhale vapors.
- 10% NaOH is caustic — avoid skin/eye contact.
- Ethanol is flammable — keep away from flames during recrystallization.
- Always release pressure from the corked flask during shaking to prevent it from popping open.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

**[(b) — 7 Marks]** Discuss the role of NaOH in the Schotten–Baumann reaction. Why can't the reaction be carried out in a purely organic solvent like dry ether without base?

**Model Answer:**

**Role of NaOH [4 marks]:**
1. **Deprotonation / Base role:** Phenol has pKa ≈ 10. In aqueous NaOH, it is fully converted to the phenoxide ion, which is ~10⁶ times more nucleophilic than neutral phenol. This enhanced nucleophilicity is essential to attack the carbonyl carbon of benzoyl chloride at room temperature.
2. **Acid scavenger:** The reaction liberates HCl. Free HCl would (a) protonate any unreacted phenoxide back to phenol, reducing nucleophile concentration, and (b) catalyze the reverse hydrolysis of the ester. NaOH neutralizes HCl to NaCl + H₂O, removing the product from the equilibrium and driving the reaction forward (Le Chatelier).
3. **Reaction medium:** Aqueous NaOH is cheap, non-flammable, and environmentally benign.

**Why not dry ether without base? [3 marks]:**
1. In dry ether, phenol remains **uncharged and weakly nucleophilic**, leading to a very slow reaction.
2. There is no mechanism to remove the HCl produced; it accumulates and **protonates the amine/phenoxide** (if formed) and shifts equilibrium backward.
3. The ester, once formed, is **susceptible to acid-catalyzed hydrolysis** in the presence of HCl.
4. Hence, a basic aqueous medium is essential both **thermodynamically** (driving the reaction forward) and **kinetically** (enhancing nucleophilicity) for clean product formation.

**[Total: 7 marks]**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Quick Recall for KTU Lab Exam & Viva**

- ✅ **Reaction type:** Nucleophilic Acyl Substitution (Schotten–Baumann).
- ✅ **Reagents:** Phenol + Benzoyl chloride + 10% NaOH.
- ✅ **Product:** Phenyl benzoate (aromatic ester), white crystalline solid.
- ✅ **Stoichiometry:** 1:1 (phenol : benzoyl chloride); phenol is **limiting** in standard KTU procedure.
- ✅ **Why NaOH?** (i) Generates phenoxide (stronger nucleophile); (ii) Neutralizes HCl (drives equilibrium forward).
- ✅ **Why shaking?** Benzoyl chloride is immiscible with water; shaking creates emulsion for efficient contact.
- ✅ **Why ice-cold water at end?** Reduces solubility of product → maximizes precipitation & yield.
- ✅ **Recrystallization solvent:** Hot ethanol (phenyl benzoate is highly soluble in hot EtOH, sparingly in cold).
- ✅ **Pure m.p. of phenyl benzoate:** **69 °C** (sharp range ± 1 °C).
- ✅ **Molar masses to memorize:** PhOH = 94.11; BzCl = 140.57; Product = 198.22 g/mol.
- ✅ **Density of BzCl:** 1.21 g/mL (used for volume→mass conversion).
- ✅ **Functional group formed:** **Ester linkage (–COOR)**; **NOT** an ether or amide.
- ✅ **Common error:** Confusing **benzoylation** (–COC₆H₅) with **benzylation** (–CH₂C₆H₅).
- ✅ **Hazards:** Benzoyl chloride = lachrymator; Phenol = toxic; NaOH = caustic; EtOH = flammable.
- ✅ **Expected yield range:** **60–80%**; below 50% indicates procedural error.
- ✅ **By-products:** NaCl and H₂O (no gaseous waste — clean, atom-economical reaction).
- ✅ **Mechanism steps to draw in exam:** Deprotonation → Nucleophilic attack → Tetrahedral intermediate → Loss of Cl⁻ → Acid neutralization.
