topics.title: Preparation of solid esters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Preparation of solid esters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **ester** is an organic compound derived from an acid (organic or inorganic) in which at least one –OH (hydroxyl) group is replaced by an –O–alkyl (alkoxy) group. Esters formed from a **carboxylic acid** and an **alcohol** carry the general formula:

$$ \text{R–COO–R'} $$

where R is the acyl group (acid-derived) and R' is the alkyl group (alcohol-derived).

A **solid ester** refers to an ester that exists in the solid state at room temperature (**~25 °C, 1 atm**). In B.Tech Chemistry Lab (KTU 2024 Scheme, PCPOL307), the canonical "solid ester" prepared is **Aspirin (Acetylsalicylic acid, 2-acetoxybenzoic acid)**, an aromatic ester of salicylic acid and acetic anhydride with a melting point of **135 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU PCPOL307 – Module 2):** The experiment is typically framed as the *preparation of aspirin from salicylic acid by acetylation with acetic anhydride in the presence of a mineral acid catalyst (H₂SO₄ or H₃PO₄)*, followed by **purification by recrystallization** and **characterization by melting point** and **percentage yield calculation**.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Think of an ester as a **chemical "handshake"** between two molecules:

- One molecule acts like a donor (an **acid** that has a proton to give up, specifically the –COOH part).
- The other acts like a partner (an **alcohol**, holding an –OH ready to be replaced).
- When they meet, a small byproduct (**water**, H₂O, or **acetic acid**, CH₃COOH) is "sweated out," and the two large partners remain linked by a new chemical "hand" called the **ester linkage (–COO–)**.

For aspirin, instead of a plain alcohol, we use **acetic anhydride** (which is essentially two acetic acid molecules joined together with one extra oxygen). It is far more reactive than plain acetic acid, so the handshake happens quickly at modest temperature.

The product — aspirin — is a **white, crystalline solid** because its molecules pack neatly in a regular lattice (held by hydrogen bonds of the –COOH group still attached to the benzene ring).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is aspirin a *solid* ester but many other esters (e.g., ethyl acetate) are liquids?**
> Because aspirin contains both a **benzene ring** (rigid, planar, aids crystal packing) and a **free –COOH group** (forms strong intermolecular **hydrogen bonds**). Liquid esters typically lack strong intermolecular attractions and have flexible alkyl chains.

## 1.3 Other Common Solid Esters Encountered in B.Tech Labs

| Ester | IUPAC Name | Melting Point (°C) | Parent Acid | Parent Alcohol/Anhydride |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Aspirin | 2-Acetoxybenzoic acid | 135 | Salicylic acid | Acetic anhydride |
| Phenyl benzoate | Phenyl benzoate | 70 | Benzoic acid | Phenol |
| Methyl salicylate | Methyl 2-hydroxybenzoate | −8 (liq.) – but solid derivatives exist | Salicylic acid | Methanol |
| β-Naphthyl benzoate | Naphthalen-2-yl benzoate | 107 | Benzoic acid | β-Naphthol |

> [!NOTE]
> For the KTU Module 2 viva, the most frequently asked solid ester is **Aspirin**. Examiners also accept **Phenyl benzoate** or **β-Naphthyl benzoate** as valid alternatives.

## 1.4 Key Physical Constants (Bold Highlighted)

- **Density of water:** 1.00 g/cm³ at **25 °C**
- **Density of ethanol:** ~0.789 g/cm³ at **25 °C**
- **Molar mass of salicylic acid:** **138.12 g/mol**
- **Molar mass of acetic anhydride:** **102.09 g/mol**
- **Molar mass of aspirin (acetylsalicylic acid):** **180.16 g/mol**
- **Molar mass of acetic acid (byproduct):** **60.05 g/mol**
- **Melting point of pure aspirin:** **135 °C** (literature value)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Underlying Reaction — Acetylation (Fischer-type Esterification Variant)

The acetylation of salicylic acid by acetic anhydride is an example of an **acyl substitution reaction** (nucleophilic acyl substitution). The hydroxyl group (–OH) on the aromatic ring of salicylic acid acts as the **nucleophile**, attacking the **electrophilic carbonyl carbon** of acetic anhydride.

### 2.1.1 Balanced Chemical Equation

$$ \underbrace{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{OH})(\text{COOH})}_{\text{Salicylic acid}} \; + \; \underbrace{(\text{CH}_3\text{CO})_2\text{O}}_{\text{Acetic anhydride}} \;\xrightarrow{\text{conc. H}_2\text{SO}_4}\; \underbrace{\text{C}_6\text{H}_4(\text{OCOCH}_3)(\text{COOH})}_{\text{Aspirin (Acetylsalicylic acid)}} \; + \; \underbrace{\text{CH}_3\text{COOH}}_{\text{Acetic acid}} $$

### 2.1.2 Stepwise Reaction Logic

1. **Activation of the carbonyl:** The mineral acid catalyst (conc. H₂SO₄) protonates one of the carbonyl oxygens of acetic anhydride, making the carbonyl carbon even more electrophilic.
2. **Nucleophilic attack:** The lone pair on the phenolic –OH oxygen of salicylic acid attacks the activated carbonyl carbon, forming a tetrahedral intermediate.
3. **Proton transfer:** A proton is shuffled from the attacking oxygen to one of the original anhydride oxygens.
4. **Elimination of acetate:** The intermediate collapses, expelling an acetate ion (CH₃COO⁻) — which subsequently picks up a proton to become acetic acid.
5. **Deprotonation of the oxocarbenium:** The final proton loss regenerates the catalyst and yields the neutral aspirin molecule.

### 2.1.3 Role of Each Reagent

- **Salicylic acid:** Substrate (provides the phenolic –OH that gets acetylated).
- **Acetic anhydride:** Acetylating agent — preferred over glacial acetic acid because it shifts equilibrium toward the ester by forming a more stable, easily-removed byproduct (acetic acid).
- **Conc. H₂SO₄ / H₃PO₄:** Acid catalyst — speeds up the slow uncatalyzed reaction without being consumed.
- **Distilled water (ice-cold):** Quenches the reaction and precipitates crude aspirin.
- **Ethanol–water mixture:** Recrystallization solvent — dissolves impurities and crude product when hot; on cooling, pure aspirin crystallizes while soluble impurities remain in the mother liquor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why use acetic anhydride instead of acetic acid?**
> Although both technically produce the same ester, **acetic anhydride** has a much higher equilibrium constant (K_eq) for esterification, because the byproduct (acetic acid) is far less nucleophilic than water. This drives the reaction forward without needing to remove water continuously (Le Chatelier's principle).

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are valid for **Aspirin synthesis** and any analogous solid-ester preparation where limiting reagent and stoichiometric yield are calculable.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Number of moles | $n \;=\; \dfrac{m}{M}$ | mol | $m$ = mass (g), $M$ = molar mass (g/mol) |
| 2 | Theoretical yield | $m_{\text{theo}} \;=\; n_{\text{LR}} \times M_{\text{product}}$ | g | Based on the **limiting reagent (LR)** |
| 3 | Percentage yield | $\%\text{Yield} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theo}}} \times 100$ | % | Always reported to **2 decimal places** in KTU records |
| 4 | Limiting reagent test | Compare $\dfrac{n}{\nu}$ for each reactant | — | Smallest ratio = limiting reagent |
| 5 | Recrystallization recovery | $\%\text{Recovery} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{pure, after}}}{m_{\text{crude, before}}} \times 100$ | % | Indicates purification efficiency |
| 6 | Melting point depression | $\Delta T_f \;=\; K_f \cdot m \cdot i$ | °C | $K_f$ for the solvent; $m$ = molality; $i$ = van 't Hoff factor |
| 7 | Stoichiometric ratio | $\dfrac{\nu_{\text{acid}}}{\nu_{\text{anhydride}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1}$ | — | 1 : 1 mol for aspirin synthesis |
| 8 | Catalyst requirement | $V_{\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4} \;\approx\; 2\text{–}3$ drops per 2 g substrate | mL | Typically $\sim 0.1$ mL |
| 9 | Reaction temperature | $T_{\text{rxn}} \;=\; 60\text{–}70\ ^{\circ}\text{C}$ | °C | Water bath; **never direct flame** (acetic anhydride is flammable) |
| 10 | Purity check (mixed m.p.) | $T_{\text{mix}} \;<\; T_{\text{pure}}$ if impure | °C | Depression $\geq 2$ °C ⇒ impure |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Always state the **limiting reagent** explicitly in the calculation section. KTU examiners award the **calculation mark (3 of 7 in Part B)** only if the limiting reagent is correctly identified.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility of Esters

- **Pharmaceutical industry:** Aspirin is a globally manufactured analgesic, antipyretic, and antiplatelet drug — produced in tonnes per batch via this exact reaction.
- **Polymer engineering:** Polyesters (PET, PLA) are formed by analogous ester linkages between diacids and diols.
- **Food industry:** Flavor esters (ethyl butyrate → pineapple, isoamyl acetate → banana) are produced using the same esterification principle but tuned for liquid products.
- **Perfumes and solvents:** Smaller esters used as green, biodegradable solvents.
- **Lubricants and plasticizers:** Ester-functional synthetic oils (e.g., trimellitates) are engineered for high-temperature stability.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Laboratory Procedure (Aspirin Preparation)

### 3.1.1 Glassware and Chemicals Required

- 50 mL conical flask (Erlenmeyer)
- 100 mL beaker
- 250 mL beaker (water bath)
- Glass rod, Büchner funnel, filter paper
- Melting point apparatus / capillary tube
- Thermometer (0–200 °C range)
- **Salicylic acid:** 2.0 g (≈ 0.0145 mol)
- **Acetic anhydride:** 5.0 mL (≈ 5.4 g, ≈ 0.0529 mol) — ~3.6× excess
- **Conc. H₂SO₄:** 3–5 drops (catalyst)
- **Distilled water:** ~25 mL (ice-cold)
- **Ethanol + water:** recrystallization solvent

### 3.1.2 Procedure Steps (KTU Standard Operating Sequence)

1. **Weighing:** Accurately weigh 2.0 g of salicylic acid and transfer to a clean, dry 50 mL conical flask.
2. **Addition of acetic anhydride:** Add 5.0 mL of acetic anhydride to the flask.
3. **Catalysis:** Add **3 drops** of concentrated sulfuric acid (use a dropper; never mouth pipette).
4. **Heating:** Place the flask in a **water bath at 60–70 °C** for **15 minutes**. Swirl occasionally. *Do not heat above 85 °C — risk of decomposition.*
5. **Quenching:** Carefully add **~20 mL of ice-cold distilled water** to the hot flask to decompose any unreacted anhydride and precipitate the crude aspirin.
6. **Cooling:** Cool the mixture in an ice bath (0–5 °C) until crystallization is complete (~10 min).
7. **Filtration:** Collect the crystals by suction filtration using a Büchner funnel. Wash with **2 × 5 mL ice-cold water** to remove residual acetic acid.
8. **Drying:** Air-dry the crude product; record its mass.
9. **Recrystallization:** Dissolve the crude solid in the minimum volume of hot ethanol (~5–8 mL) in a beaker; add hot distilled water until the solution becomes faintly cloudy (turbidity point).
10. **Crystallization:** Cool slowly to room temperature, then in an ice bath. Filter, wash with cold water, and dry.
11. **Characterization:**
    - Measure the **melting point** (pure aspirin = **135 °C**; acceptable range in KTU labs: **133–136 °C**).
    - **Ferric chloride test:** A few crystals in 1 mL ethanol + 1 drop neutral FeCl₃. **No violet/purple color** → confirms absence of unreacted phenolic –OH (salicylic acid would give intense violet).
12. **Calculation:** Compute **% yield** using the formula sheet in §2.2.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation of % Yield for Aspirin

Let us work through a **representative numerical** that closely mirrors KTU board numerical values.

> **Given data (typical KTU question):**
> Mass of salicylic acid used, $m_{\text{SA}} = 2.0 \ \text{g}$
> Volume of acetic anhydride used, $V_{\text{AA}} = 5.0 \ \text{mL}$
> Density of acetic anhydride, $\rho_{\text{AA}} = 1.082 \ \text{g/mL}$
> Actual mass of pure aspirin obtained after recrystallization, $m_{\text{actual}} = 1.65 \ \text{g}$

### 3.2.1 Step 1 — Convert Reactants to Moles

**Salicylic acid:**

$$ n_{\text{SA}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{SA}}}{M_{\text{SA}}} \;=\; \dfrac{2.0 \ \text{g}}{138.12 \ \text{g/mol}} \;\approx\; 0.01448 \ \text{mol} $$

**Acetic anhydride:**

$$ m_{\text{AA}} \;=\; V_{\text{AA}} \times \rho_{\text{AA}} \;=\; 5.0 \ \text{mL} \times 1.082 \ \text{g/mL} \;=\; 5.41 \ \text{g} $$

$$ n_{\text{AA}} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{AA}}}{M_{\text{AA}}} \;=\; \dfrac{5.41 \ \text{g}}{102.09 \ \text{g/mol}} \;\approx\; 0.05300 \ \text{mol} $$

### 3.2.2 Step 2 — Identify the Limiting Reagent

Stoichiometric ratio from balanced equation:

$$ \dfrac{\nu_{\text{SA}}}{\nu_{\text{AA}}} \;=\; \dfrac{1}{1} $$

Therefore, the reactant with **fewer moles** is the limiting reagent:

$$ n_{\text{SA}} \;=\; 0.01448 \ \text{mol} \quad \text{vs.} \quad n_{\text{AA}} \;=\; 0.05300 \ \text{mol} $$

Since $n_{\text{SA}} \;\ll\; n_{\text{AA}}$, **salicylic acid is the limiting reagent**.

### 3.2.3 Step 3 — Calculate Theoretical Yield of Aspirin

From the 1 : 1 stoichiometry, **moles of aspirin formed (theoretical) = moles of limiting reagent**.

$$ n_{\text{asp, theo}} \;=\; n_{\text{SA}} \;=\; 0.01448 \ \text{mol} $$

Convert to grams using $M_{\text{asp}} = 180.16 \ \text{g/mol}$:

$$ m_{\text{asp, theo}} \;=\; n_{\text{asp, theo}} \times M_{\text{asp}} \;=\; 0.01448 \ \text{mol} \times 180.16 \ \texttext{g/mol} $$

$$ m_{\text{asp, theo}} \;\approx\; 2.609 \ \text{g} $$

### 3.2.4 Step 4 — Calculate Percentage Yield

$$ \%\text{Yield} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{actual}}}{m_{\text{theo}}} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{1.65 \ \text{g}}{2.609 \ \text{g}} \times 100 $$

$$ \%\text{Yield} \;\approx\; 63.24 \ \% $$

### 3.2.5 Step 5 — Recrystallization Recovery (Optional, often asked as sub-part)

Suppose the crude mass before recrystallization was $m_{\text{crude}} = 2.10 \ \text{g}$:

$$ \%\text{Recovery} \;=\; \dfrac{m_{\text{pure}}}{m_{\text{crude}}} \times 100 \;=\; \dfrac{1.65}{2.10} \times 100 \;\approx\; 78.57 \ \% $$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Reaction Conditions & Observations Table

| Stage | Observation | Inference | Marks (KTU) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Addition of H₂SO₄ to (salicylic acid + anhydride) | Slight warming, faint vinegar smell | Catalyst protonates carbonyl; mild exotherm begins | 1 |
| Water bath 60–70 °C for 15 min | Solution turns clear yellow; solid dissolves | Acetylation proceeds; crude aspirin forms in solution | 2 |
| Addition of ice-cold water | White crystalline precipitate appears | Hydrolysis of unreacted anhydride; aspirin insoluble in cold water | 1 |
| Suction filtration | White powder on filter paper; clear filtrate | Crude solid separated from mother liquor | 1 |
| Hot ethanol + water recrystallization | Crystals dissolve on heating; reappear on cooling | Pure aspirin crystallizes; impurities remain in solution | 2 |
| Melting point measurement | Sharp melting at **133–136 °C** | Acceptable purity; close to literature 135 °C | 1 |
| FeCl₃ test | **No violet color** | Confirms complete acetylation; no free phenolic –OH | 1 |

## 3.4 Common Pitfalls Addressed (Analytical Corrections)

- **If yield > 100 %** → probable incomplete drying (residual water/solvent).
- **If m.p. < 130 °C** → impure product; repeat recrystallization.
- **If FeCl₃ gives violet color** → unreacted salicylic acid present; the reaction was incomplete (insufficient time/temperature).
- **If yellow/brown crystals** → overheated; localized decomposition of aspirin to salicylic acid + acetic acid.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reaction Mechanism Flowchart

The following Mermaid block summarizes the **stepwise mechanism** of aspirin synthesis from a block-architecture perspective (showing how the substrate, catalyst, and reagents interact at each stage).

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step 1: Substrate and Reagent Mixing] --> B[Salicylic Acid plus Acetic Anhydride in Conical Flask]
    B --> C[Step 2: Acid Catalyst Addition]
    C --> D[Add 3 drops Conc H2SO4]
    D --> E[Step 3: Protonation of Carbonyl Oxygen]
    E --> F[Concentrated H2SO4 donates Proton to Anhydride Oxygen]
    F --> G[Step 4: Electrophilic Activation]
    G --> H[Carbonyl Carbon becomes more Electrophilic]
    H --> I[Step 5: Nucleophilic Attack]
    I --> J[Phenolic OH lone pair attacks Activated Carbonyl Carbon]
    J --> K[Step 6: Tetrahedral Intermediate Formation]
    K --> L[Step 7: Proton Transfer via Solvent]
    L --> M[Step 8: Collapse and Acetate Expulsion]
    M --> N[Acetate Ion leaves as Leaving Group]
    N --> O[Step 9: Deprotonation]
    O --> P[Regeneration of H2SO4 Catalyst]
    P --> Q[Step 10: Final Product]
    Q --> R[Crude Aspirin plus Acetic Acid Byproduct]
    R --> S[Step 11: Quenching with Ice Cold Water]
    S --> T[Decomposition of Excess Anhydride]
    T --> U[Step 12: Crystallization]
    U --> V[Pure Aspirin Crystals form on Cooling]
    V --> W[Step 13: Recrystallization in Ethanol Water]
    W --> X[Step 14: Final Pure Aspirin Product]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Experimental Setup

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Reagent Reservoir: Salicylic Acid 2.0 g] --> M[Mixing Chamber: 50 mL Conical Flask]
    R2[Reagent Reservoir: Acetic Anhydride 5.0 mL] --> M
    R3[Catalyst Reservoir: Conc H2SO4 3 drops] --> M
    M --> H[Temperature Control Unit: Water Bath 60 to 70 C]
    H --> T[Timer Module: 15 minutes]
    T --> Q[Quench Module: Ice Cold Distilled Water 20 mL]
    Q --> C[Crystallization Module: Ice Bath 0 to 5 C]
    C --> F[Filtration Unit: Buchner Funnel and Vacuum]
    F --> RC[Recrystallization Module: Ethanol plus Water]
    RC --> D[Drying and Weighing Module]
    D --> CH[Characterization Module: Melting Point and FeCl3 Test]
    CH --> OUT[Final Output: Pure Aspirin with Yield Report]
```

## 4.3 Decision Tree for Purity Verification

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start: Measure Melting Point] --> Q1{Is Mp between 133 and 136 C?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is FeCl3 Violet Color Absent?}
    Q1 -- No --> N1[Repeat Recrystallization]
    Q2 -- Yes --> P[Product Confirmed Pure Aspirin]
    Q2 -- No --> N2[Reaction Incomplete: Repeat Acetylation with Longer Heating Time]
    N1 --> RC[Redissolve in Hot Ethanol plus Water]
    RC --> S
    N2 --> S
```

## 4.4 Stoichiometric Flow Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Salicylic Acid M equals 138.12 g per mol] --> SR[1 mole of SA]
    B[Acetic Anhydride M equals 102.09 g per mol] --> SR
    SR --> STOICH[1 to 1 Stoichiometry]
    STOICH --> PROD[1 mole Aspirin M equals 180.16 g per mol plus 1 mole Acetic Acid]
    PROD --> MASS[Theoretical Mass Calculation Module]
    MASS --> YIELD[Percentage Yield Calculation Module]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
> Define an ester. Give one example of a solid ester prepared in the B.Tech Chemistry Lab and write its IUPAC name.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

An **ester** is a derivative of a carboxylic acid in which the acidic hydrogen of the –COOH group is replaced by an alkyl or aryl group (R–COO–R′), formed typically by the reaction of an acid with an alcohol (Fischer esterification) or an acid anhydride (acetylation).

**Example of a solid ester:** **Aspirin**, prepared by acetylation of salicylic acid with acetic anhydride. **IUPAC name:** *2-acetoxybenzoic acid* (or 2-(acetyloxy)benzoic acid). Its melting point is **135 °C**.

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Why is **acetic anhydride** preferred over **glacial acetic acid** in the laboratory preparation of aspirin?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. Acetic anhydride has a higher equilibrium constant (K_eq) for acetylation than acetic acid, so the reaction proceeds to a much greater extent (≈ near completion) at mild temperatures.
2. The byproduct of acetylation with anhydride is **acetic acid**, not water. Water, if produced, would shift the equilibrium backward (Le Chatelier's principle). Avoiding water drives the reaction forward.
3. Acetic anhydride is more reactive (stronger electrophile) than glacial acetic acid, so the reaction is faster and requires less heating, minimizing side-product formation.

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Q3A. [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**
> **(a)** Describe the laboratory preparation of **aspirin** from salicylic acid. Include balanced equation, reagents, apparatus, and reaction conditions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Starting from **2.0 g of salicylic acid** and **5.0 mL of acetic anhydride** (density 1.082 g/mL), calculate the **theoretical yield** and **percentage yield** if the actual mass of pure aspirin obtained is **1.65 g**. Identify the limiting reagent. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Procedure (7 marks):**

- **Reaction:** Salicylic acid + Acetic anhydride → Aspirin + Acetic acid (catalyst: conc. H₂SO₄).
- **Apparatus:** 50 mL conical flask, water bath (60–70 °C), Büchner funnel, suction pump, ice bath, melting point apparatus.
- **Procedure outline (write the full sequence as in §3.1.2):** weigh 2.0 g salicylic acid → add 5.0 mL acetic anhydride → add 3 drops conc. H₂SO₄ → heat in water bath at 60–70 °C for 15 min with swirling → quench with 20 mL ice-cold water → cool in ice bath → suction filter → wash with cold water → recrystallize from hot ethanol-water → dry → measure m.p. (target **133–136 °C**) → confirm with FeCl₃ test (no violet color).
- **Balanced equation (mandatory):** as given in §2.1.1.
- **Yield expectations:** typically **60–75 %** for a well-executed lab session.

**(b) — Numerical (7 marks):** Complete step-by-step as derived in §3.2:

- $n_{\text{SA}} = 2.0/138.12 = 0.01448 \ \text{mol}$
- $m_{\text{AA}} = 5.0 \times 1.082 = 5.41 \ \text{g}$
- $n_{\text{AA}} = 5.41/102.09 = 0.05300 \ \text{mol}$
- **Limiting reagent:** Salicylic acid (smaller mole count).
- $m_{\text{asp, theo}} = 0.01448 \times 180.16 = 2.609 \ \text{g}$
- **% Yield** = $(1.65/2.609) \times 100 = 63.24 \ \text%$

---

> **Q3B. [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam – July 2024]**
> **(a)** Explain the **mechanism of acetylation** of salicylic acid by acetic anhydride in the presence of conc. H₂SO₄. Show all intermediate steps. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Discuss the **role of recrystallization** in purifying the crude aspirin product. Why is a mixture of ethanol and water used as the recrystallization solvent? What is the principle behind the **mixed melting point** test for confirming purity? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**(a) — Mechanism (7 marks):**

The mechanism is a **nucleophilic acyl substitution** proceeding through a **tetrahedral intermediate**.

- **Step 1:** Protonation of the carbonyl oxygen of acetic anhydride by H₂SO₄ makes the carbonyl carbon strongly electrophilic.
- **Step 2:** The phenolic –OH of salicylic acid attacks this carbon via its lone pair, forming a tetrahedral intermediate (positive charge on the attacking oxygen).
- **Step 3:** Proton transfer (mediated by solvent or by HSO₄⁻) moves a proton from the attacking oxygen to the leaving oxygen.
- **Step 4:** The C–O bond to the acetate group breaks; acetate ion leaves.
- **Step 5:** Deprotonation of the oxocarbenium regenerates H₂SO₄ (catalyst) and yields neutral aspirin.

(A diagram showing electron flow with curved arrows is expected for full marks in KTU answer scripts.)

**(b) — Recrystallization & Mixed m.p. (7 marks):**

- **Role of recrystallization:** Removes soluble impurities and colored byproducts. The crude solid is dissolved in a minimum volume of hot solvent; on cooling, pure aspirin crystallizes (because it is less soluble at low temperature) while impurities stay dissolved in the mother liquor.
- **Why ethanol–water mixture:** Aspirin is **freely soluble in hot ethanol** but **poorly soluble in cold water**. The mixture provides the right balance — hot dissolution followed by selective crystallization on cooling. Pure water alone does not dissolve crude aspirin well even when hot.
- **Principle of mixed melting point:** A pure substance has a sharp, characteristic m.p. **Impurities depress and broaden** the melting range. If mixing a small amount of the unknown with a known pure sample of aspirin gives a **lower** melting point (or broader range) than the pure compound alone, the unknown is **not identical** to aspirin (i.e., impure or different). If the m.p. is **unchanged**, the unknown is confirmed to be aspirin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to identify the limiting reagent** explicitly — KTU examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if it is omitted.
> 2. **Using density of acetic anhydride wrongly** (defaulting to 1.00 g/mL as for water) — this is incorrect; use **1.082 g/mL**.
> 3. **Reporting % yield with wrong significant figures** — KTU accepts **2 decimal places** (e.g., 63.24 %).
> 4. **Confusing aspirin melting point** — students frequently write 120 °C or 150 °C. The correct value is **135 °C**.
> 5. **Missing the FeCl₃ test** explanation — examiners often ask "How will you confirm complete acetylation?" Answer: **FeCl₃ test must give negative result (no violet color)**.
> 6. **Using direct flame** for heating the reaction — major safety violation; **always use water bath**.
> 7. **Not drying crystals** before weighing — leads to spuriously high yield (>100 %).
> 8. **Writing the balanced equation with wrong byproduct** — it is **acetic acid (CH₃COOH)**, not water, when acetic anhydride is the acetylating agent.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Solid ester in lab = Aspirin (2-acetoxybenzoic acid).**
- **Molar masses to memorize:** Salicylic acid = **138.12 g/mol**, Acetic anhydride = **102.09 g/mol**, Aspirin = **180.16 g/mol**, Acetic acid = **60.05 g/mol**.
- **Catalyst:** conc. H₂SO₄ (3 drops) or H₃PO₄; **never** HCl or HNO₃.
- **Reaction temperature:** water bath at **60–70 °C** for **15 minutes**.
- **Quenching agent:** **ice-cold distilled water** (≈ 20 mL).
- **Recrystallization solvent:** **ethanol + water** mixture.
- **Melting point of pure aspirin:** **135 °C**; acceptable lab range = **133–136 °C**.
- **FeCl₃ test:** **negative** (no violet) ⇒ complete acetylation; **positive** (violet) ⇒ unreacted salicylic acid.
- **Stoichiometry:** **1 : 1** between salicylic acid and acetic anhydride.
- **Limiting reagent (typical):** Salicylic acid (since anhydride is used in ~3–4× excess).
- **Typical yield range:** **60–80 %** in a well-performed experiment.
- **Density of acetic anhydride:** **1.082 g/mL** at 25 °C.
- **Reaction type:** **Nucleophilic acyl substitution** (not classical Fischer esterification).
- **Safety:** Acetic anhydride is a lachrymator — use in fume hood; never mouth pipette.
- **Industrial relevance:** Same chemistry underlies commercial aspirin manufacture at multi-tonne scale.
- **Watch the byproduct:** It is **acetic acid**, NOT water — this is what makes anhydride preferable.
- **Solid vs liquid ester:** Solid character of aspirin comes from (i) rigid aromatic ring and (ii) free –COOH forming intermolecular H-bonds.

---

topics.title: Bromination of amines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Bromination of amines

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🔬 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Bromination of Amines** is a classic **Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS)** reaction in which molecular bromine (Br₂) acts as the electrophile and replaces one or more hydrogen atoms on the aromatic ring of an amine substrate. In the KTU PCPOL307 lab context, the standard demonstration involves the reaction of **aniline (C₆H₅NH₂)** with **bromine water (Br₂/H₂O)**, which proceeds with such extraordinary ease that it yields the **2,4,6-tribromoaniline** product in a single step at room temperature, even in the absence of any Lewis acid catalyst.

The reaction is:

$$C_6H_5NH_2 + 3\,Br_2 \longrightarrow 2,4,6\text{-}Br_3C_6H_2NH_2 + 3\,HBr$$

The product, **2,4,6-tribromoaniline**, is a **white crystalline precipitate** that appears almost instantaneously when bromine water is added to aniline, making this reaction a beautiful visual demonstration of the powerful **ring-activating effect** of the –NH₂ (amino) group.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of the benzene ring as a "fancy dress party"** 🎭 — the ring has six hydrogen "guests" sitting on it. Normally, this ring is very picky (unreactive) and rejects new guests (substituents) unless you use brute force (harsh catalysts like FeBr₃ or AlCl₃). But the moment you attach an **–NH₂ group** (aniline's amino group), it acts like a **super-friendly host** 🤝 who *introduces* and *welcomes* incoming guests (electrophiles like Br⁺) at three specific seats — the two ortho positions and the one para position. The result? **Three bromines pile in at once**, throwing out the original hydrogen guests, and a snow-white precipitate (2,4,6-tribromoaniline) forms before you can blink!

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| Molar mass of aniline (C₆H₅NH₂) | **93.13 g/mol** |
| Molar mass of 2,4,6-tribromoaniline | **330.81 g/mol** |
| Density of aniline | **1.0297 g/cm³** |
| Density of bromine | **3.1028 g/cm³** |
| Melting point of 2,4,6-tribromoaniline | **120–122 °C** |
| pKₐ of anilinium ion | **4.60** |
| Color of aniline | Colorless to pale yellow oily liquid |
| Color of bromine water | Reddish-brown |
| Color of 2,4,6-tribromoaniline | White crystalline solid |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📚 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Why Does –NH₂ Activate the Ring So Strongly?

The amino group (–NH₂) attached to the benzene ring is a **strong activator** and an **ortho/para director**. This is due to **resonance (mesomeric effect, +M effect)**:

The lone pair of electrons on the nitrogen atom is delocalized into the π-system of the benzene ring through resonance, increasing the **electron density** at the **ortho** and **para** positions. This makes those three positions especially nucleophilic and reactive toward the electrophile (Br⁺).

### 2.1.1 Resonance Structures of Aniline

The lone pair on nitrogen participates in resonance with the aromatic ring, placing negative charge density at the ortho and para carbons:

- **Ortho carbon (C-2 and C-6)**: High electron density
- **Para carbon (C-4)**: High electron density
- **Meta carbons (C-3 and C-5)**: NO such enhancement

Therefore, the three incoming Br atoms occupy positions **2, 4, and 6**.

## 2.2 Why No Lewis Acid Catalyst Is Needed

For benzene itself, you need **FeBr₃ or AlCl₃** to polarize Br₂ and generate a strong enough electrophile. For aniline, the **+M effect** of –NH₂ makes the ring so electron-rich that even the weakly electrophilic **Br₂ (in water)** is sufficient. Bromine water itself dissociates slightly:

$$Br_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HBr + HOBr$$

## 2.3 Why Acetanilide is Used Sometimes (Modified Procedure)

In **quantitative** experiments, aniline's reactivity is *too high* — it tribrominates uncontrollably. To control the reaction and obtain a **mono-bromo product (p-bromoacetanilide)**, the amino group is first **protected** by acetylation with acetic anhydride to form **acetanilide**. The –NHCOCH₃ group is still activating but milder than –NH₂.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Exam Tip:** If your procedure mentions "acetanilide" instead of aniline, the product is **p-bromoacetanilide (4-bromoacetanilide)**, a colorless crystalline solid, m.p. **168 °C**, formed via bromination at the para position only.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Moles of aniline | $n = \dfrac{w}{M} = \dfrac{\text{mass in g}}{93.13}$ | Stoichiometric calculation |
| Moles of bromine | $n = \dfrac{M \times V}{1000}$ | Volumetric calculation |
| Stoichiometric ratio (aniline : Br₂) | $1 : 3$ | Theoretical yield |
| Theoretical yield of product | $Y_t = n_{aniline} \times 330.81$ | Yield calculation |
| Percentage yield | $\%\text{ yield} = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Lab performance |
| Rf value (TLC) | $R_f = \dfrac{\text{distance travelled by spot}}{\text{distance travelled by solvent front}}$ | Purity check |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch (acetanilide hydrolysis) | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | Buffer understanding |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Brominated aromatic amines are key **pharmaceutical intermediates** (e.g., synthesis of anti-infectives, dyes, and agrochemicals). The selectivity principles learned here underpin modern **drug design**, **agrochemical engineering**, and **polymer dye chemistry**.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **Dye industry**: Tribromoaniline derivatives are precursors to azo dyes.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Brominated anilines serve as intermediates for anti-bacterial and anti-fungal drugs.
- **Agrochemicals**: Used in synthesis of herbicides and pesticides.
- **Polymer chemistry**: Brominated aromatics as flame-retardant monomers.
- **Analytical chemistry**: Bromination tests confirm aromatic amine functionality.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 🧮 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Reaction Mechanism (Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution)

The mechanism proceeds through the classic **three-step EAS pathway**:

### **Step 1: Generation of the Electrophile (Br⁺)**

Bromine is slightly polarized in water. The actual attacking species in aqueous bromination is the **bromonium-type electrophile**, sometimes represented as Br⁺ or as a polarized Br–Br:

$$Br_2 \rightleftharpoons Br^{\delta+} \cdots Br^{\delta-}$$

In aqueous medium:

$$Br_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOBr + HBr$$

The HOBr acts as a source of Br⁺ through protonation equilibria.

### **Step 2: Electrophilic Attack — Formation of the σ-Complex (Arenium Ion)**

The π-electron cloud of aniline (especially dense at C-2, C-4, C-6 due to +M effect of –NH₂) attacks Br⁺. A **cyclohexadienyl cation** (also called the **Wheland intermediate** or **σ-complex**) forms:

$$\underset{\text{Aniline}}{C_6H_5NH_2} + Br^{\delta+} \longrightarrow \underset{\text{σ-complex}}{[\,C_6H_5(NH_2)Br\,]^{+}}$$

This intermediate is **stabilized by resonance** with the lone pair of nitrogen, making the activation energy much lower than for benzene itself.

### **Step 3: Loss of Proton — Restoration of Aromaticity**

The base (H₂O or Br⁻) removes the proton from the sp³ carbon, regenerating the aromatic π-system and yielding the substituted product. The energy released here (resonance energy of ~150 kJ/mol) drives the reaction forward.

$$[\,C_6H_5(NH_2)Br\,]^{+} \longrightarrow \underset{\text{o/p-bromoaniline}}{BrC_6H_4NH_2} + H^{+}$$

### **Repetition to Tribromination**

Because the resulting **bromoaniline** is still strongly activated by –NH₂, the next two brominations at the remaining ortho and para positions occur **even more readily** than the first. The result is **2,4,6-tribromoaniline**.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Stoichiometric Calculation Example

> **Problem:** A student uses **2.0 mL of aniline** (density = 1.0297 g/cm³) and excess bromine water. Calculate the **theoretical yield** of 2,4,6-tribromoaniline.

### **Solution:**

**Step 1: Mass of aniline used**

$$m_{aniline} = V \times \rho = 2.0\,\text{mL} \times 1.0297\,\text{g/mL}$$

$$\boxed{m_{aniline} = 2.0594\,\text{g}}$$

**Step 2: Moles of aniline**

$$n_{aniline} = \dfrac{m}{M} = \dfrac{2.0594\,\text{g}}{93.13\,\text{g/mol}}$$

$$\boxed{n_{aniline} = 0.02212\,\text{mol}}$$

**Step 3: Stoichiometric conversion (1 mol aniline → 1 mol product)**

$$n_{product} = n_{aniline} = 0.02212\,\text{mol}$$

**Step 4: Theoretical mass of 2,4,6-tribromoaniline**

$$m_{product} = n \times M = 0.02212\,\text{mol} \times 330.81\,\text{g/mol}$$

$$\boxed{m_{product} = 7.318\,\text{g}}$$

**Step 5: If the student actually obtained 5.85 g, percentage yield is**

$$\%\text{ yield} = \dfrac{5.85}{7.318} \times 100 = 79.94\%$$

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Reactivity

| Substrate | Group | Activating? | o/p Director? | Product with Br₂/H₂O |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Benzene | –H | – | – | No reaction (needs catalyst) |
| Aniline | –NH₂ | **Strong (+M)** | Yes | 2,4,6-tribromoaniline |
| Acetanilide | –NHCOCH₃ | Moderate (+M) | Yes | p-bromoacetanilide |
| Nitrobenzene | –NO₂ | Deactivator (–M) | meta director | m-bromonitrobenzene (with catalyst) |
| Phenol | –OH | Strong (+M) | Yes | 2,4,6-tribromophenol |

## 3.4 The Role of Temperature & Solvent

- **Temperature control**: At 0–5 °C (ice-bath), the reaction can sometimes be moderated to give mono- or di-brominated products.
- **Solvent**: Water is preferred as it dissolves Br₂ partially and keeps the reaction safe. Glacial acetic acid is used when crystallinity of product is needed.
- **Agitation**: Slow addition of bromine water prevents local overheating.

# 📝 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question]**

### **Q1. Why does aniline undergo bromination with bromine water even in the absence of a Lewis acid catalyst, while benzene requires one?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Aniline contains the –NH₂ group, which is a **strong ring-activator** due to its **+M (mesomeric) effect**.
- **(1 mark)** The lone pair on nitrogen is delocalized into the benzene ring via resonance, increasing **electron density at the ortho (C-2, C-6) and para (C-4)** positions.
- **(1 mark)** This high electron density makes the ring nucleophilic enough to attack even the weakly electrophilic Br₂/H₂O. In contrast, benzene has no such activation and requires a Lewis acid (FeBr₃) to polarize Br₂ into a stronger electrophile Br⁺.

---

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Question]**

### **Q2. What is the role of dilute HCl in the bromination of aniline? What happens if concentrated HCl is used?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **(1 mark)** Aniline is **insoluble in water** but **soluble in dilute HCl** because it forms the **water-soluble anilinium chloride salt (C₆H₅NH₃⁺Cl⁻)**.
- **(1 mark)** This ensures homogeneous mixing of aniline with bromine water, allowing uniform reaction.
- **(1 mark)** However, **excess/concentrated HCl protonates the –NH₂ group to –NH₃⁺**, which is a **ring-deactivator** (loses lone pair). This **slows or inhibits** the bromination. Hence only a stoichiometric amount of dilute HCl is used.

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — End Semester Module Experiment 2]**

### ✏️ **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Describe the laboratory procedure for the bromination of aniline to prepare 2,4,6-tribromoaniline. Mention the apparatus, reagents, and safety precautions. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the reaction mechanism with all three steps. Explain why 2,4,6-positions are preferred over 3 and 5 positions using resonance structures. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(a): Procedure (7 marks)**

1. **Apparatus (1 mark):** 250 mL beaker, glass rod, Buchner funnel, suction flask, vacuum pump, filter paper, watch glass, measuring cylinder (25 mL), magnetic stirrer, ice bath.

2. **Reagents (1 mark):** Aniline (2 mL), bromine water (saturated, ~50 mL), dilute HCl (1:1, ~10 mL), ethanol (for recrystallization).

3. **Procedure (4 marks):**
   - **Step i (1 mark):** Take 2 mL of aniline in a 250 mL beaker. Add 10 mL of dilute HCl and stir to dissolve, forming a clear solution of anilinium chloride.
   - **Step ii (1 mark):** Place the beaker in an **ice bath** to maintain temperature between **0–5 °C**.
   - **Step iii (1 mark):** Add bromine water **slowly dropwise** with continuous stirring using a glass rod. A **white precipitate** of 2,4,6-tribromoaniline forms immediately.
   - **Step iv (1 mark):** Continue addition of bromine water until the reddish-brown color persists, indicating excess bromine. Filter the precipitate through a **Buchner funnel** under suction, wash with cold water, and recrystallize from **hot ethanol**. Dry in an oven at 60–80 °C and weigh.

4. **Safety Precautions (1 mark):** Use gloves and goggles (Br₂ is corrosive and toxic). Work in a fume hood. Aniline is toxic — avoid skin contact.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.A(b): Mechanism (7 marks)**

**Step 1: Electrophile generation (1.5 marks)**

$$Br_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons HOBr + HBr$$

The polarized Br₂ or HOBr acts as a Br⁺ source.

**Step 2: Electrophilic attack — σ-complex (2.5 marks)**

The π-cloud of aniline attacks Br⁺, forming the **Wheland intermediate (arenium ion)**. The +M effect of –NH₂ stabilizes this intermediate, lowering the activation energy. The complex has the positive charge delocalized over the ring, with extra stabilization at the carbon bearing the –NH₂ group.

**Step 3: Loss of proton (1 mark)**

A base (H₂O/Br⁻) removes the proton from the sp³ carbon, restoring aromaticity and yielding **o-bromoaniline** (or p-bromoaniline).

**Repetition to give 2,4,6-tribromoaniline (2 marks):**

Since the –NH₂ group is a **strong ortho/para director**, the three positions attacked are **C-2, C-4, and C-6**. Resonance structures of aniline show that the negative charge density (and hence nucleophilicity) is highest at these three positions. The meta positions (C-3 and C-5) do not gain any extra electron density from the +M effect of –NH₂. Hence, no meta-bromination occurs, and the **final product is exclusively 2,4,6-tribromoaniline**, isolated as a white crystalline solid (m.p. **120–122 °C**).

---

### ✏️ **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**(a)** What are the limitations of direct bromination of aniline? How is the reaction modified to obtain a mono-bromo product? Describe the procedure for preparing **p-bromoacetanilide** from aniline. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare the reactivity of aniline, acetanilide, and nitrobenzene towards bromination. Give a concise table summarizing the products and explain using electronic effects. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(a): Acetanilide Route (7 marks)**

**Limitations of direct bromination (2 marks):**
- Aniline is **too reactive** — it gives **2,4,6-tribromoaniline** in one step, making it impossible to isolate a **mono-bromo** product directly.
- The –NH₂ group can also be **oxidized** by Br₂ to complex tarry products.
- Hence, **protection of the –NH₂ group** is needed.

**Protection by acetylation (2 marks):**
- Aniline is treated with **acetic anhydride (or acetyl chloride)** in the presence of a base (pyridine or sodium acetate) to form **acetanilide** (C₆H₅NHCOCH₃).
- The –NHCOCH₃ group is a **mild activator** (still ortho/para director) but much weaker than –NH₂, allowing controlled **mono-bromination** at the **para position** only.

**Procedure for p-bromoacetanilide (3 marks):**
- Dissolve **2 g of acetanilide** in **10 mL of glacial acetic acid** in a conical flask.
- Add **bromine-acetic acid solution** (1 mL Br₂ in 5 mL glacial acetic acid) **slowly with stirring** at room temperature.
- A **colorless crystalline precipitate** of p-bromoacetanilide forms.
- Filter, wash with cold water, recrystallize from ethanol, dry, and weigh.
- **Melting point** of pure p-bromoacetanilide = **168 °C**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q.B(b): Reactivity Comparison (7 marks)**

| Compound | Group | Electronic Effect | Ring Activation | o/p or m Director | Product with Br₂ |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Aniline** | –NH₂ | **+M (strong)** | **Strong activator** | o/p | 2,4,6-Tribromoaniline |
| **Acetanilide** | –NHCOCH₃ | **+M (moderate, lone pair tied up in C=O resonance)** | Moderate activator | o/p | p-Bromoacetanilide (mono) |
| **Nitrobenzene** | –NO₂ | **–M (strong)** | **Strong deactivator** | **meta** | m-Bromonitrobenzene (with FeBr₃, harsh conditions) |

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- In **aniline**, the nitrogen lone pair is freely donated into the ring → strong activation → very fast tribromination.
- In **acetanilide**, the lone pair is partially engaged in resonance with the carbonyl C=O, so it is donated to the ring only moderately → controlled mono-bromination.
- In **nitrobenzene**, the –NO₂ group **withdraws** electron density from the ring via –M and –I effects → ring is deactivated → bromination is very slow and requires a Lewis acid catalyst; the incoming Br⁺ goes to the meta position because the meta carbon has relatively higher electron density in the resonance structures of nitrobenzene.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes that cost marks in the KTU board exam:**
> 1. **Forgetting the molar mass of the product (330.81 g/mol).** Many students use 93.13 (aniline's molar mass) by mistake and get a wrong theoretical yield.
> 2. **Wrong stoichiometric ratio.** Some students assume 1:1 (aniline:Br₂) — the correct ratio is **1:3** because three Br atoms substitute on the ring.
> 3. **Skipping the role of temperature.** KTU examiners specifically look for mention of **ice-bath cooling (0–5 °C)** in the procedure. Omitting it costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Not mentioning safety precautions.** Br₂ is highly corrosive and toxic. Marks are awarded for fume hood, gloves, and goggles.
> 5. **Confusing aniline with acetanilide products.** If the question specifies *acetanilide*, the product is **p-bromoacetanilide**, NOT tribromoaniline. Read the question carefully!
> 6. **Vague mechanism steps.** Always label the three EAS steps explicitly: **(i) Electrophile generation, (ii) σ-complex formation, (iii) Proton loss**.
> 7. **Forgetting the role of dilute HCl.** It is used to dissolve aniline via salt formation, not to act as a catalyst.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ✨

- ✅ **Reaction type:** Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution (EAS).
- ✅ **Substrate:** Aniline (C₆H₅NH₂); **Reagent:** Bromine water (Br₂/H₂O).
- ✅ **Product:** 2,4,6-Tribromoaniline (white crystalline solid, m.p. **120–122 °C**).
- ✅ **Molar mass of product:** **330.81 g/mol**.
- ✅ **Stoichiometry:** **1 mol aniline : 3 mol Br₂ → 1 mol product + 3 mol HBr**.
- ✅ **Activating group:** –NH₂ is a **strong activator** and **o/p director** due to **+M effect** of nitrogen lone pair.
- ✅ **No catalyst needed** because the ring is highly electron-rich.
- ✅ **Ice-bath (0–5 °C)** is used to control the vigorous reaction.
- ✅ **Dilute HCl** dissolves aniline as **anilinium chloride salt**; excess HCl deactivates the ring.
- ✅ **Acetanilide route** gives **p-bromoacetanilide (mono-bromo product)** — protection strategy.
- ✅ **Safety:** Br₂ is corrosive and toxic — use fume hood, gloves, and goggles.
- ✅ **Filtration:** Use **Buchner funnel** under suction; recrystallize from **ethanol**.
- ✅ **Yield formula:** $\%\text{ yield} = \dfrac{\text{actual yield}}{\text{theoretical yield}} \times 100$.
- ✅ **Resonance** of aniline places negative charge at **C-2, C-4, C-6** — these are the sites of electrophilic attack.
- ✅ **KTU hot keywords to use in answers:** "Electrophilic Aromatic Substitution," "Wheland intermediate," "+M effect," "ortho/para director," "ring activation," "Lewis acid not required."

> **Best of luck with your KTU PCPOL307 Chemistry Lab examination! 🎓🧪**

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Purification of Organic Compounds — The Big Picture

Organic compounds synthesized in the laboratory or isolated from natural sources are almost never obtained in a **pure state**. They are invariably contaminated with by-products, unreacted starting materials, side-products, or solvents. Before any compound can be characterized (melting point, boiling point, spectra) or used for further reactions, it must be **purified** rigorously.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition:** *Purification* is the systematic removal of physical and chemical impurities from a crude organic compound using physico-chemical techniques that exploit differences in **volatility, solubility, polarity, molecular size, or adsorption affinity**, without altering the chemical identity of the target molecule.

The three classical techniques mandated in **Module Experiment 2** are:

| S.No | Technique | Property Exploited | Physical State of Mixture |
|------|-----------|--------------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | **Fractionation (Fractional Distillation)** | Difference in boiling points | Liquid – Liquid |
| 2 | **Crystallization (Recrystallization)** | Difference in solubility with temperature | Solid – Solid (in solution) |
| 3 | **Chromatography (TLC / Column / Paper)** | Difference in partition / adsorption affinity | Solid / Liquid mixtures |

---

## 1.2 Fractionation — Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Fractional distillation (fractionation)** is an enhanced distillation process in which a *fractionating column* is inserted between the distillation flask and the condenser. The column provides a large surface area where repeated **vaporization–condensation cycles** occur, effectively acting as a cascade of miniature distillations. This allows the separation of two or more **miscible liquids** having boiling points differing by **at least 25 °C**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** *Fractionating Column* — A vertical tube packed with glass beads, ceramic saddles, or fitted with glass plates (bubble-cap or sieve trays) that provides a large surface for condensation of vapor and re-evaporation, thereby establishing multiple equilibria between liquid and vapor phases.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Staircase of Purification"
Imagine climbing a staircase where at **each step (theoretical plate)** a tiny distillation happens. Vapour at the bottom of the column contains both liquids A and B. As it rises, the **less volatile component (higher boiling)** tends to condense and fall back, while the **more volatile component (lower boiling)** rises further. By the time vapour reaches the top, it is enriched in the more volatile component, condenses in the condenser, and is collected.

Think of it like this:
- A *single distillation* = **one** staircase step.
- A *fractionation* = **dozens of steps** stacked vertically.
- The more steps, the **purer** each fraction becomes.

### The Three Standard Fractions in KTU Practicals
In typical B.Tech lab experiments, students separate:
1. **Acetone – Water** (B.P. difference ≈ 31 °C) — easy separation.
2. **Methanol – Water** (B.P. difference ≈ 35 °C).
3. **Ethyl acetate – Water** (immiscible → uses separating funnel, not fractionation).

---

## 1.3 Crystallization — Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Crystallization (or Recrystallization)** is the purification technique in which an impure solid organic compound is dissolved in a **minimum volume of a hot (near boiling) suitable solvent**, the hot solution is filtered to remove insoluble impurities, and the filtrate is cooled to allow the pure compound to crystallize out, leaving soluble impurities dissolved in the mother liquor.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** *Recrystallization* — The process of dissolving a solid in a hot solvent and allowing it to crystallize upon cooling, in which the crystals obtained are purer than the original solid because impurities either remain in solution (mother liquor) or are filtered off as insoluble matter.

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Honey Purifier"
Imagine raw honey containing wax, dust, and pollen. If you dissolve the honey in **hot water**:
- Honey (your desired compound) → fully dissolves when hot.
- Wax & dust → do **not** dissolve and can be **filtered off while hot**.
- When you **cool** the solution, honey recrystallizes (well, becomes viscous), but the unwanted soluble impurities stay dissolved.

This is precisely how crystallization works — *hot dissolves, cold rejects*.

### When is Crystallization Used?
- The compound is a **solid at room temperature**.
- It must have a **high melting point** and be **thermally stable**.
- It should be **more soluble in hot solvent** than in cold solvent (large temperature-dependent solubility).
- The impurities should differ markedly in solubility from the desired compound.

---

## 1.4 Chromatography — Intuitive Overview

### Formal Definition
**Chromatography** is a versatile separation technique in which the components of a mixture are distributed between two immiscible phases:
- **Stationary phase** — a fixed bed (solid or liquid-coated solid).
- **Mobile phase** — a fluid (liquid or gas) that moves through or over the stationary phase.

Each component migrates at a rate determined by its **partition coefficient (K)** or **adsorption affinity (R_f)** between the two phases.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition:** *Chromatography* — A physical method of separation in which the components of a mixture are separated by differential distribution between a stationary phase and a mobile phase. The ratio of distance traveled by a component to the distance traveled by the solvent front is defined as the *Retention factor* (R_f).

### Conceptual Analogy — The "Marathon Race on Two Terrains"
Imagine runners (solute molecules) racing through a track made of two terrains:
- **Sand (stationary phase)** — slows them down.
- **Paved road (mobile phase)** — speeds them up.

Runners who "stick" to the sand (more polar molecules on polar stationary phase) move **slowly**. Runners who prefer the road (less polar molecules) move **fast**. After a fixed time, each runner reaches a different position — they are now **separated**.

This is exactly how Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) works.

### Types of Chromatography Covered in KTU 2024

| Type | Stationary Phase | Mobile Phase | Typical Use |
|------|------------------|--------------|-------------|
| Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) | Silica gel / Alumina on glass plate | Organic solvent | Identification, purity check |
| Column Chromatography | Silica gel / Alumina in glass column | Solvent (eluent) | Separation & purification |
| Paper Chromatography | Water held in cellulose paper | Organic solvent | Amino acids, sugars, dyes |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 FRACTIONATION — Deep Theory

### 2.1.1 Principle
When a binary liquid mixture is heated to boiling, the vapour produced is **always richer in the more volatile component** than the liquid phase (Raoult's Law and Dalton's Law combined). On condensation, the liquid (distillate) is therefore richer in the low-boiling component. If this condensate is **re-vaporized** and re-condensed *n* times, its purity increases exponentially. The fractionating column automates this **multiple-stage distillation**.

### 2.1.2 Raoult's Law (Foundation)

For an ideal binary solution of components A (more volatile) and B (less volatile):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= P_A^{\circ} \, x_A + P_B^{\circ} \, x_B \\
y_A &= \frac{P_A^{\circ} \, x_A}{P_{\text{total}}} \\
y_B &= 1 - y_A
\end{aligned}
$$

Where:
- $P_A^{\circ}$ = vapour pressure of pure A at given T
- $P_B^{\circ}$ = vapour pressure of pure B at given T
- $x_A, x_B$ = mole fractions in **liquid** phase
- $y_A, y_B$ = mole fractions in **vapour** phase

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $P_A^{\circ} > P_B^{\circ}$, it follows that $y_A > x_A$. **The vapour is always enriched in the more volatile component.**

### 2.1.3 Theoretical Plates and HETP

A **theoretical plate** is defined as a section of the column where the vapour leaving is in **thermodynamic equilibrium** with the liquid leaving.

$$
n_{\text{th}} = \frac{\text{Length of column}}{\text{HETP}}
$$

Where **HETP** = *Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate* (in cm).

- **Smaller HETP** → More efficient column → Better separation.
- Packed columns (glass beads, Raschig rings) typically have **HETP = 1–5 cm**.

### 2.1.4 The McCabe–Thiele Conceptual Picture

At each theoretical plate, an equilibrium stage occurs:

| Stage | Liquid Composition $x_A$ | Vapour Composition $y_A$ |
|-------|--------------------------|---------------------------|
| Bottom (flask) | 0.10 | 0.20 |
| Plate 1 | 0.20 | 0.36 |
| Plate 2 | 0.36 | 0.55 |
| Plate 3 | 0.55 | 0.73 |
| Plate 4 | 0.73 | 0.86 |
| Plate 5 (top) | 0.86 | 0.94 |

→ After **5 plates**, vapour is **94%** pure in component A.

### 2.1.5 Step-by-Step Fractional Distillation Procedure (KTU Lab Protocol)

1. Assemble the **fractionating column vertically** between the round-bottom flask (containing the mixture) and the condenser.
2. Add **boiling chips / porcelain pieces** to ensure smooth boiling.
3. Heat the flask **slowly and uniformly** using a heating mantle.
4. Vapour rises, contacts the column packing, partially condenses.
5. Condensate trickles down, meets rising vapour → **heat exchange** → more volatile component vaporizes again.
6. Thermometer at column head records **temperature of vapour leaving the top**.
7. Collect fractions at **distinct temperature plateaus** corresponding to pure components.

### 2.1.6 Selection Criteria
- Boiling point difference $\Delta T_b \geq 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ for efficient separation.
- Liquids must be **miscible** (e.g., acetone + water). Immiscible pairs → use **steam distillation** or **separating funnel**.
- Avoid **azeotropic mixtures** (e.g., ethanol–water 95.6:4.4) where simple fractionation fails.

---

## 2.2 CRYSTALLIZATION — Deep Theory

### 2.2.1 The Solubility Curve Principle

Crystallization depends on the steep temperature dependence of solubility. For most organic solids:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S(T) &= S_0 + k(T - T_0) \\
\text{or more precisely:} \quad \log S &= -\frac{\Delta H_{\text{diss}}}{2.303\,R} \cdot \frac{1}{T} + C
\end{aligned}
$$

- $S(T)$ = solubility at temperature $T$
- $\Delta H_{\text{diss}}$ = enthalpy of dissolution (positive for endothermic dissolution)
- $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹** (universal gas constant)

> [!NOTE]
> Compounds with **steep solubility curves** (large $\Delta H_{\text{diss}}$) are easy to crystallize because their solubility drops sharply on cooling.

### 2.2.2 Solvent Selection — The "Gold Standard Rules"

| Property | Requirement | Why |
|----------|-------------|-----|
| Solubility hot | High (≥ 5–10 % w/v) | To dissolve compound |
| Solubility cold | Very low (≤ 0.5 % w/v) | To crystallize on cooling |
| Boiling point | Moderate (50–120 °C) | Easy removal of traces |
| Reactivity | Inert to compound | No chemical change |
| Polarity | Match compound polarity | "Like dissolves like" |
| Volatility | Should be volatile | Easy drying |

### 2.2.3 Common Solvents for Recrystallization

| Solvent | Polarity | B.P. (°C) | Common Use |
|---------|----------|-----------|------------|
| Water | Very polar | 100 | Sugars, salts, polar acids |
| Methanol | Polar | 65 | General purpose |
| Ethanol | Polar protic | 78 | Pharmaceuticals, acids |
| Acetone | Polar aprotic | 56 | General organic |
| Ethyl acetate | Moderately polar | 77 | Esters, lactones |
| Chloroform | Non-polar | 61 | Non-polar compounds |
| Petroleum ether (40–60) | Very non-polar | 40–60 | Hydrocarbons |
| Benzene (now restricted) | Non-polar | 80 | Aromatic compounds |

### 2.2.4 Mixed Solvent Strategy
When no single solvent works, use a **solvent pair**:
- **Good solvent** (dissolves compound hot) + **Poor solvent** (does not dissolve compound).
- Procedure: Dissolve in minimum hot *good* solvent → add hot *poor* solvent until turbidity → cool → crystallizes.

Examples:
- **Ethanol + Water** (for benzoic acid)
- **Acetone + Petroleum ether**
- **Methanol + Diethyl ether**

### 2.2.5 Step-by-Step Crystallization Procedure

```
Step 1: Selection of solvent (trial-and-error with small samples)
Step 2: Dissolution in MINIMUM volume of hot solvent (in a conical flask)
Step 3: HOT GRAVITY FILTRATION (using fluted filter paper & stemless funnel)
         — removes insoluble impurities (dust, char, inorganic salts)
Step 4: COOLING slowly to room temperature, then in ice bath
         — pure compound crystallizes; soluble impurities stay in mother liquor
Step 5: SUCTION FILTRATION (Büchner funnel + filter flask + vacuum pump)
         — separates crystals from mother liquor
Step 6: WASHING with small cold portions of pure solvent
Step 7: DRYING in air / desiccator / oven (below m.p.)
Step 8: Check purity by SHARP MELTING POINT determination
```

### 2.2.6 Quantitative Yield Calculation

$$
\% \text{ Yield} = \frac{m_{\text{pure crystals recovered}}}{m_{\text{crude compound taken}}} \times 100
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A **high yield with broad melting range** indicates *incomplete purification*. A **moderate yield with sharp melting range** is the *Gold Standard*.

---

## 2.3 CHROMATOGRAPHY — Deep Theory

### 2.3.1 The Partition Coefficient (K)

For any solute distributed between two immiscible phases:

$$
K = \frac{C_{\text{stationary}}}{C_{\text{mobile}}}
$$

- $K$ large → solute prefers stationary phase → moves **slowly**.
- $K$ small → solute prefers mobile phase → moves **quickly**.
- Two components with different $K$ values will **separate** over time/distance.

### 2.3.2 The Retention Factor (R_f)

For planar chromatography (TLC, Paper):

$$
R_f = \frac{\text{Distance traveled by solute spot}}{\text{Distance traveled by solvent front}}
$$

**Range:** $0 \leq R_f \leq 1$

### 2.3.3 Properties of R_f
- $R_f$ depends on **temperature, solvent, stationary phase, and the compound**.
- A compound's $R_f$ is **characteristic** under fixed conditions → used as a fingerprint.
- Comparing unknown $R_f$ with authentic sample = **identification**.
- Two compounds with **identical $R_f$** in one solvent system → change solvent (different polarity) to confirm distinction.

### 2.3.4 Thin Layer Chromatography (TLC) — Detailed

**Stationary phase:** Silica gel ($\text{SiO}_2 \cdot x\text{H}_2\text{O}$) — polar, slightly acidic.
- Bonds: silanol ($\equiv \text{Si–OH}$) groups → H-bond with polar compounds.

**Mobile phase (developing solvent):** Non-polar to polar gradient:
- Hexane (least polar) → chloroform → ethyl acetate → acetone → methanol → water (most polar).
- For *less polar* compounds → use **less polar** solvent (high $R_f$).
- For *more polar* compounds → use **more polar** solvent (high $R_f$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rule of thumb:** To **increase $R_f$**, use a **more polar solvent**. To **decrease $R_f$**, use a **less polar solvent**.

### 2.3.5 Step-by-Step TLC Procedure (KTU Lab Protocol)

1. Cut TLC plate to required size; mark **origin line** (1 cm from bottom) with pencil.
2. Dissolve sample in volatile solvent (acetone/hexane).
3. Spot the sample using a **capillary tube** at the origin — let dry.
4. Place plate in **developing chamber** (beaker with lid) containing solvent (below origin).
5. Allow solvent to **rise by capillary action** until ~1 cm from top.
6. Remove plate, mark **solvent front** immediately.
7. Dry plate; visualize spots under **UV lamp (254 nm / 365 nm)** or in **iodine chamber**.
8. Calculate $R_f$ for each spot.

### 2.3.6 Visualization Techniques

| Method | Principle | Best For |
|--------|-----------|----------|
| UV light (254 nm) | Fluorescence quenching (silica GF₂₅₄) | UV-active compounds |
| Iodine vapour | Reversible adsorption (brown spots) | General organic |
| Ninhydrin spray | Reacts with amines → purple | Amino acids |
| Potassium permanganate (KMnO₄) | Oxidizes alkenes/alkynes | Unsaturated compounds |
| Vanillin / Anisaldehyde | Forms colored derivatives | Terpenes, steroids |

### 2.3.7 Column Chromatography — Detailed

**Principle:** A glass column is packed with stationary phase (silica/alumina, 60–120 mesh). The sample is loaded on top, and solvent (eluent) is allowed to flow under gravity (or pressure). Components separate as **bands** and are collected as **fractions**.

**Elution order** (in normal phase with silica):
- **Non-polar compounds** elute **first** (low affinity for polar silica).
- **Polar compounds** elute **last** (high affinity for polar silica).

$$
\text{Elution order: alkane} < \text{alkene} < \text{ether} < \text{ester} < \text{aldehyde} < \text{ketone} < \text{alcohol} < \text{acid}
$$

### 2.3.8 Paper Chromatography — Brief

- **Stationary phase:** Water molecules adsorbed on cellulose fibers of filter paper.
- **Mobile phase:** Organic solvent (e.g., butanol:acetic acid:water = 4:1:5 for amino acids).
- Used historically for **amino acid separation**, sugars, plant pigments.
- Today largely replaced by **HPTLC and HPLC**.

### 2.3.9 Solvent Systems for Paper Chromatography

| Mixture | Ratio | Application |
|---------|-------|-------------|
| n-Butanol : Acetic acid : Water | 4 : 1 : 5 | Amino acids |
| Phenol : Water | 4 : 1 | Sugars |
| Methanol : Water | 4 : 1 | General polar |

---

## 2.4 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET (One-Page Revision)

| # | Equation | Meaning / Use |
|---|----------|---------------|
| 1 | $P_{\text{total}} = P_A^{\circ}\, x_A + P_B^{\circ}\, x_B$ | Raoult's Law (fractionation basis) |
| 2 | $y_A = \dfrac{P_A^{\circ}\, x_A}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | Vapour composition |
| 3 | $n_{\text{th}} = \dfrac{L_{\text{column}}}{\text{HETP}}$ | Theoretical plates |
| 4 | $S(T) = S_0 \, \exp\!\left[-\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{diss}}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_0}\right)\right]$ | Solubility–temperature relation |
| 5 | $\% \text{ Yield} = \dfrac{m_{\text{pure}}}{m_{\text{crude}}} \times 100$ | Crystallization efficiency |
| 6 | $K = \dfrac{C_{\text{stationary}}}{C_{\text{mobile}}}$ | Partition coefficient |
| 7 | $R_f = \dfrac{d_{\text{solute}}}{d_{\text{solvent front}}}$ | Retention factor ($0 \leq R_f \leq 1$) |
| 8 | $\alpha = \dfrac{R_{f2}}{R_{f1}}$ (for adjacent spots) | Separation factor (chromatography) |
| 9 | $V_{\text{elution}} = V_0 + K \cdot V_s$ | Elution volume (column) |
| 10 | $\Delta R_f = R_{f,A} - R_{f,B}$ | Resolution parameter (must be ≥ 0.1) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical constants to memorize for KTU lab viva:**
> - $R$ = **8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
> - 1 atm = **101.325 kPa** = **760 mmHg**
> - Water boiling point = **100 °C** at 1 atm
> - Ethanol boiling point = **78.37 °C**
> - Acetone boiling point = **56 °C**
> - Methanol boiling point = **64.7 °C**

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Technique | Industrial / Engineering Application |
|-----------|---------------------------------------|
| **Fractionation** | Petroleum refining (crude oil → LPG, gasoline, kerosene, diesel, lubricating oil), cryogenic air separation (O₂, N₂, Ar), ethanol purification, perfumery. |
| **Crystallization** | Pharmaceutical API purification (paracetamol, aspirin, ibuprofen), sugar refining, silicon wafer production (single crystals for semiconductors), salt production from sea water. |
| **Chromatography** | Drug purity analysis (HPLC in pharma QC), forensic toxicology (TLC of drugs of abuse), environmental monitoring (PAH, pesticide residues), food safety (melamine detection in milk). |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Vapour-Liquid Equilibrium in Fractionation

We derive the fundamental relation governing how a component partitions between the liquid and vapour phases during fractional distillation.

### Given:
- Binary mixture of liquids A (more volatile) and B (less volatile).
- Both liquids form an **ideal solution**.

### Step 1: Apply Raoult's Law for each component

For an ideal solution, the partial pressure of each component above the liquid equals its mole fraction times its pure-component vapour pressure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_A &= P_A^{\circ} \, x_A \quad \text{(partial pressure of A)} \\
P_B &= P_B^{\circ} \, x_B \quad \text{(partial pressure of B)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conversion logic:** Dalton experimentally showed that for an ideal solution, each component's vapour pressure is *linearly* related to its mole fraction in the liquid.

### Step 2: Apply Dalton's Law for total pressure

For a gas/vapour mixture, the total pressure equals the sum of partial pressures:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_A + P_B
$$

Substituting Step 1:

$$
P_{\text{total}} = P_A^{\circ} \, x_A + P_B^{\circ} \, x_B
$$

### Step 3: Apply mole-fraction constraint

Since the liquid has only A and B:

$$
x_A + x_B = 1 \quad \Rightarrow \quad x_B = 1 - x_A
$$

Substitute:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= P_A^{\circ} \, x_A + P_B^{\circ} (1 - x_A) \\
P_{\text{total}} &= P_B^{\circ} + (P_A^{\circ} - P_B^{\circ})\, x_A
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4: Express vapour-phase composition

By Dalton's Law, the mole fraction of A in the vapour phase is:

$$
y_A = \frac{P_A}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{P_A^{\circ} \, x_A}{P_{\text{total}}}
$$

Substituting $P_{\text{total}}$:

$$
y_A = \frac{P_A^{\circ} \, x_A}{P_B^{\circ} + (P_A^{\circ} - P_B^{\circ})\, x_A}
$$

### Step 5: Derive the enrichment factor

The ratio $\dfrac{y_A}{x_A}$ tells us **how much richer** the vapour is in A compared to the liquid:

$$
\frac{y_A}{x_A} = \frac{P_A^{\circ}}{P_B^{\circ} + (P_A^{\circ} - P_B^{\circ})\, x_A}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Since $P_A^{\circ} > P_B^{\circ}$ (A more volatile), we always get $\dfrac{y_A}{x_A} > 1$. This means **the vapour phase is always enriched in the more volatile component** — the foundational principle of fractional distillation.

### Step 6: Numerical verification

Let $P_A^{\circ} = 400$ mmHg (e.g., acetone at 56 °C), $P_B^{\circ} = 100$ mmHg (water vapour at 56 °C), $x_A = 0.5$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= 100 + (400 - 100)(0.5) \\
P_{\text{total}} &= 100 + 150 = 250 \text{ mmHg} \\
y_A &= \frac{400 \times 0.5}{250} = \frac{200}{250} = 0.80
\end{aligned}
$$

**Interpretation:** Although the liquid contains **50% acetone**, the vapour in equilibrium contains **80% acetone**. This vapour rises into the column, partially condenses (now the "new liquid"), revaporizes, and the process repeats, yielding progressively purer acetone at the top.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Number of Theoretical Plates via McCabe–Thiele (Conceptual Walkthrough)

While the full McCabe–Thiele graphical method uses the equilibrium curve $y = f(x)$, here we walk through the **discrete stage calculation**.

### Step 1: Start with liquid composition $x_0$ in the flask.

For acetone-water system: $x_0 = 0.30$ (30% acetone).

### Step 2: At the first equilibrium stage, vapour composition $y_1$ is:

$$
y_1 = \frac{\alpha \, x_0}{1 + (\alpha - 1) x_0}
$$

Where $\alpha = P_A^{\circ}/P_B^{\circ} = 400/100 = 4.0$ (relative volatility).

$$
y_1 = \frac{4.0 \times 0.30}{1 + 3.0 \times 0.30} = \frac{1.20}{1.90} = 0.632
$$

### Step 3: The vapour leaving stage 1 becomes liquid on stage 2 (after partial condensation):

$$
x_1 = y_1 = 0.632
$$

### Step 4: At stage 2, vapour composition:

$$
y_2 = \frac{4.0 \times 0.632}{1 + 3.0 \times 0.632} = \frac{2.528}{2.896} = 0.873
$$

### Step 5: Stage 3:

$$
x_2 = y_2 = 0.873; \quad y_3 = \frac{4.0 \times 0.873}{1 + 3.0 \times 0.873} = \frac{3.492}{3.619} = 0.965
$$

### Summary Table

| Stage (n) | $x_n$ (liquid) | $y_n$ (vapour) |
|-----------|----------------|----------------|
| 0 (flask) | 0.300 | 0.632 |
| 1 | 0.632 | 0.873 |
| 2 | 0.873 | 0.965 |
| 3 | 0.965 | 0.993 |

> [!NOTE]
> After **just 4 theoretical stages**, the vapour is **99.3% pure acetone**. This shows why even a modest fractionating column (5–6 plates) is sufficient for the KTU acetone-water experiment.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: R_f Expression in TLC — Quantitative Form

Starting from the **linear velocity** assumption: in TLC, the mobile phase moves by capillary action with velocity $v_m$, and the solute spot moves with velocity $v_s$.

$$
v_s = v_m \cdot R_f
$$

### Derivation:

The velocity of a solute is governed by the fraction of time it spends in the mobile phase:

$$
\begin{aligned}
v_s &= v_m \cdot \left( \frac{t_{\text{mobile}}}{t_{\text{total}}} \right) \\
t_{\text{total}} &= t_{\text{mobile}} + t_{\text{stationary}} \\
v_s &= v_m \cdot \frac{t_{\text{mobile}}}{t_{\text{mobile}} + t_{\text{stationary}}}
\end{aligned}
$$

The retention factor $R_f$ is defined as:

$$
R_f = \frac{d_{\text{solute}}}{d_{\text{solvent front}}} = \frac{v_s \, t}{v_m \, t} = \frac{t_{\text{mobile}}}{t_{\text{mobile}} + t_{\text{stationary}}}
$$

Now, by definition of the **partition coefficient**:

$$
K = \frac{C_s}{C_m} = \frac{n_s/V_s}{n_m/V_m}
$$

Rearranging in terms of time spent (assuming linear velocities):

$$
K = \frac{t_{\text{stationary}} / V_s}{t_{\text{mobile}} / V_m} = \frac{t_{\text{stationary}}}{t_{\text{mobile}}} \cdot \frac{V_m}{V_s}
$$

Substituting back:

$$
\boxed{R_f = \frac{1}{1 + K \cdot (V_s/V_m)}}
$$

**Conversion logic:** $R_f$ decreases as $K$ increases (solute prefers stationary phase), confirming the inverse relationship.

### Numerical example:

A solute has $K = 0.5$, $V_s/V_m = 1.0$:

$$
R_f = \frac{1}{1 + 0.5 \times 1.0} = \frac{1}{1.5} = 0.667
$$

If $K$ increases to **2.0** (more polar compound on polar silica):

$$
R_f = \frac{1}{1 + 2.0 \times 1.0} = \frac{1}{3} = 0.333
$$

→ More polar solute moves slower → lower $R_f$. ✓

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: When to Use Which Technique?

| Criterion | Fractionation | Crystallization | Chromatography |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|----------------|
| **Physical state of target** | Liquid | Solid | Liquid or Solid |
| **Property exploited** | Volatility (B.P.) | Solubility difference | Adsorption / Partition |
| **Sample size** | 10–500 mL | 1–50 g | mg to grams |
| **Purity achievable** | 95–99 % | 99–99.9 % | 99.9 %+ (HPLC) |
| **Capital cost** | Moderate (glass column + heating mantle) | Low | High (HPLC, GC) |
| **Time required** | 2–6 hours | 3–8 hours | 10–60 min (TLC) |
| **Skill level** | Intermediate | Beginner | Intermediate–Advanced |
| **KTU applicability** | Acetone-water, ethanol-water | Benzoic acid, naphthalene, sugar | Plant pigments, dyes, drug monitoring |

## 3.5 Worked-Out Numerical Problems (KTU Style)

### Problem 1: R_f Calculation from TLC Data

A student runs a TLC plate. The solvent front travels **7.5 cm** from the origin. Three spots are observed:
- Spot A: 2.1 cm from origin
- Spot B: 4.5 cm from origin
- Spot C: 6.8 cm from origin

**Find:** $R_f$ values for each spot and identify which compound is most polar.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{f,A} &= \frac{2.1}{7.5} = 0.280 \\
R_{f,B} &= \frac{4.5}{7.5} = 0.600 \\
R_{f,C} &= \frac{6.8}{7.5} = 0.907
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** On silica gel (polar stationary), the compound with the **lowest R_f (A)** is the **most polar** (strongest adsorption). Compound C with R_f = 0.907 is the **least polar**.

---

### Problem 2: Crystallization Yield

A student crystallizes **5.0 g** of crude benzoic acid and recovers **3.8 g** of pure crystals after suction filtration and drying.

**Find:** % yield of crystallization.

**Solution:**

$$
\% \text{ Yield} = \frac{m_{\text{pure}}}{m_{\text{crude}}} \times 100 = \frac{3.8}{5.0} \times 100 = 76\,\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> A yield of 70–85% is **typical and acceptable**. Yields above 90% often indicate that soluble impurities are still trapped in the crystals (broad melting point).

---

### Problem 3: Theoretical Plate Calculation

A fractionating column has a length of **30 cm**, and the HETP for glass-bead packing is found to be **5.0 cm**.

**Find:** Number of theoretical plates.

**Solution:**

$$
n_{\text{th}} = \frac{L_{\text{column}}}{\text{HETP}} = \frac{30 \text{ cm}}{5.0 \text{ cm/plate}} = 6 \text{ plates}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 6-plate column is adequate to separate acetone-water with high efficiency.

---

### Problem 4: Raoult's Law Application

At 56 °C, pure acetone has $P_A^{\circ} = 400$ mmHg and pure water has $P_B^{\circ} = 120$ mmHg. A mixture contains $x_A = 0.40$.

**Find:** Total vapour pressure and vapour composition.

**Solution:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= P_A^{\circ} \, x_A + P_B^{\circ}\, x_B \\
P_{\text{total}} &= (400)(0.40) + (120)(0.60) \\
P_{\text{total}} &= 160 + 72 = 232 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
y_A = \frac{P_A^{\circ} \, x_A}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{160}{232} = 0.690
$$

$$
y_B = 1 - y_A = 0.310
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Vapour is **69% acetone** vs. **40% acetone in liquid** → confirms the **enrichment principle**.

---

### Problem 5: Mixed Solvent Selection

A compound is **insoluble in water**, **partially soluble in hot ethanol**, and **insoluble in cold ethanol**. Recommend a solvent system.

**Solution:** The compound already meets crystallization criteria with **ethanol alone**. Water is not needed. If the compound were insoluble in both hot and cold ethanol, then a mixed solvent **(ethanol + water)** would be used: dissolve in minimum hot ethanol, add hot water until turbidity, cool.

---

### Problem 6: Spot Identification in TLC

A sample spot has $R_f = 0.45$ in pure chloroform. A co-spot with authentic reference shows a **single spot** at $R_f = 0.45$. What does this indicate?

**Solution:** The unknown is **likely identical** to the reference compound (same $R_f$ in same solvent under same conditions). For confirmation, the plate should be run in a **second, different-polarity solvent** to verify identical $R_f$ in that system as well.
