## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**Q:** Define the term *Retention factor (R_f)* as used in thin layer chromatography. Mention the factors on which R_f depends. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> The *Retention factor* (R_f) of a solute is defined as the ratio of the distance traveled by the solute spot to the distance traveled by the solvent front, measured from the same origin line.
>
> $$
> R_f = \frac{d_{\text{solute}}}{d_{\text{solvent front}}}
> $$
>
> **Factors affecting R_f:**
> 1. **Nature of the stationary phase** (silica gel vs. alumina).
> 2. **Nature and polarity of the mobile phase** (solvent system).
> 3. **Temperature** of the developing chamber.
> 4. **Chemical nature of the solute** (polarity, hydrogen bonding).
> 5. **Chamber saturation** (presence or absence of solvent vapour equilibrium).
>
> **Significance:** A compound's R_f is reproducible under fixed conditions and serves as a *physical fingerprint* for identification by co-chromatography with an authentic sample.

---

### Question 2
**Q:** What is meant by *theoretical plate* in a fractionating column? Why is it important? **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

> A **theoretical plate** is defined as a section of the fractionating column in which the vapour leaving the section is in **complete thermodynamic equilibrium** with the liquid leaving the same section. Each plate corresponds to one complete **vaporization–condensation cycle**.
>
> **Importance:**
> - The number of theoretical plates ($n_{\text{th}}$) quantifies the **separating efficiency** of the column. Higher $n_{\text{th}}$ → better separation.
> - For two liquids with a boiling point difference of ~30 °C, a column with **4–6 theoretical plates** is generally sufficient for good separation.
> - The **HETP (Height Equivalent to a Theoretical Plate)** characterizes the column packing: smaller HETP means more efficient packing.
>
> $$
> n_{\text{th}} = \frac{\text{Length of column}}{\text{HETP}}
> $$

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**Q:** *(a)* Explain the principle and procedure of **fractional distillation** for the separation of a binary liquid mixture. Draw a labeled diagram and discuss the role of the fractionating column with reference to **Raoult's Law** and the concept of **theoretical plates**. **[7 Marks]**
**Q:** *(b)* An acetone-water mixture (40% acetone by mole) is fractionated. Given $P_A^{\circ} = 400$ mmHg and $P_B^{\circ} = 120$ mmHg at 56 °C, calculate the composition of the vapour in equilibrium. If the fractionating column has HETP = 5 cm and length 30 cm, determine the number of theoretical plates and comment on its separating efficiency. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Principle and Procedure of Fractional Distillation

**Principle:**
Fractional distillation is based on the principle that when a liquid mixture is heated, the vapour produced is **richer in the more volatile component** than the liquid phase. When this vapour is condensed and re-vaporized repeatedly (multiple equilibrium stages), each stage enriches the vapour further in the more volatile component. The **fractionating column** provides the surface and residence time for these repeated stages to occur in a single apparatus.

**Procedure (Step-by-Step):**

1. **Assembly:** The fractionating column is fitted vertically between the distillation flask (containing the binary liquid mixture + boiling chips) and the Liebig condenser.
2. **Heating:** The flask is heated gradually using a heating mantle. The thermometer bulb is placed at the **side arm of the column head** to record the boiling point of the vapour leaving the top.
3. **Vaporization:** Vapour rises through the column packing (glass beads / ceramic saddles). At each level, partial condensation occurs — the less volatile component condenses and trickles back down.
4. **Revaporization:** The descending condensate meets fresh rising hot vapour → heat exchange → more volatile component revaporizes and continues to rise.
5. **Fraction collection:** The temperature at the column head stabilizes at the boiling point of the more volatile component. The distillate is collected in a clean receiver.
6. **Receiver change:** When the temperature starts to rise (mid-fraction or transition region), the receiver is changed to collect the **second fraction** (less volatile component).
7. **Stoppage:** Heating is stopped when the temperature again stabilizes at the boiling point of the second component, or when only residue remains.

**Role of Fractionating Column — Reference to Raoult's Law:**

The fractionating column enhances separation by establishing **multiple vaporization–condensation equilibria**. Each equilibrium stage obeys Raoult's Law:

$$
y_A = \frac{P_A^{\circ} \, x_A}{P_B^{\circ} + (P_A^{\circ} - P_B^{\circ})\, x_A}
$$

Since $P_A^{\circ} > P_B^{\circ}$ for the more volatile component, each successive equilibrium yields a vapour with progressively higher $y_A$. After $n$ such stages, the distillate purity becomes extremely high.

**Concept of Theoretical Plates:**
A theoretical plate is the section of column where one complete equilibrium is established. The **efficiency** is measured by the number of theoretical plates ($n_{\text{th}}$):

$$
n_{\text{th}} = \frac{L}{\text{HETP}}
$$

A well-packed column with $n_{\text{th}} \geq 5$ is sufficient to separate acetone-water efficiently.

**Labelled Diagram** (refer to **Section 4.2** above for the apparatus block diagram).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — Numerical Problem

**Given:**
- $x_A$ (acetone mole fraction in liquid) = 0.40
- $P_A^{\circ}$ (pure acetone at 56 °C) = 400 mmHg
- $P_B^{\circ}$ (pure water at 56 °C) = 120 mmHg
- HETP = 5 cm, Column length = 30 cm

**Step 1: Calculate total vapour pressure**

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{total}} &= P_A^{\circ} \, x_A + P_B^{\circ}\, x_B \\
&= (400)(0.40) + (120)(0.60) \\
&= 160 + 72 = 232 \text{ mmHg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate vapour-phase composition**

$$
y_A = \frac{P_A^{\circ}\, x_A}{P_{\text{total}}} = \frac{160}{232} = 0.690
$$

$$
y_B = 1 - y_A = 0.310
$$

**Conclusion:** Vapour phase contains **69.0% acetone** versus only **40% in liquid** — the vapour is significantly enriched in acetone.

**Step 3: Number of theoretical plates**

$$
n_{\text{th}} = \frac{L}{\text{HETP}} = \frac{30 \text{ cm}}{5 \text{ cm/plate}} = 6 \text{ plates}
$$

**Step 4: Comment on separating efficiency**

A column with **6 theoretical plates** is highly efficient. For the acetone-water system (ΔT_b ≈ 31 °C, $\alpha = 400/120 \approx 3.33$), such a column can yield distillate purity **exceeding 99%** acetone, as shown by the iterative calculation in Section 3.2 of these notes. The efficiency is more than adequate for KTU lab requirements.

**Final Answer:**
- Vapour composition: $y_A = 0.690$, $y_B = 0.310$
- Number of theoretical plates: **6**
- Column efficiency: **Sufficient for >99% separation**.

### QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

**Q:** *(a)* Describe the **principle, procedure, and solvent selection criteria** for the **recrystallization** of an organic solid. Discuss with suitable examples why a mixed solvent (ethanol-water) is often used. **[7 Marks]**
**Q:** *(b)* A crude sample of an organic compound is analyzed by thin layer chromatography. The solvent front traveled 8.0 cm. Two spots are observed at 2.4 cm and 5.6 cm from the origin. Calculate the $R_f$ values. If a co-spot with a known reference compound produces a **single spot** at $R_f = 0.30$ in pure chloroform, what conclusion can be drawn? Suggest **two more solvent systems** to confirm identity. **[7 Marks]** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

---

#### Model Solution (a) — Recrystallization

**Principle:**
Recrystallization is based on the principle that the **solubility of a solid in a solvent increases with temperature**, and that the desired compound and its impurities have **different solubilities** in the chosen solvent at different temperatures. On cooling, the pure compound crystallizes out while soluble impurities remain in the mother liquor.

**Solvent Selection Criteria:**

| Criterion | Requirement |
|-----------|-------------|
| 1. Dissolving power (hot) | Compound must be **highly soluble** in boiling solvent. |
| 2. Dissolving power (cold) | Compound must be **poorly soluble** in cold solvent. |
| 3. Impurity behaviour | Impurities should be either **insoluble** (filtered off) or **highly soluble** (remain in mother liquor). |
| 4. Reactivity | Solvent must be **chemically inert** to the compound. |
| 5. Boiling point | Moderate (50–120 °C) for easy removal of last traces. |
| 6. Volatility | Should be volatile enough to dry easily. |

**Procedure:**

1. **Selection of solvent** (small-scale trials in test tubes).
2. **Dissolution:** Dissolve the crude solid in the **minimum volume of hot (near boiling) solvent** in a conical flask.
3. **Hot gravity filtration:** Filter the hot solution through a fluted filter paper (kept hot in oven) to remove insoluble impurities (dust, decolorizing carbon residue, etc.).
4. **Cooling:** Allow the filtrate to cool slowly to room temperature, then in an ice bath for complete crystallization.
5. **Suction filtration:** Collect crystals on a Büchner funnel under vacuum; wash with **cold pure solvent** to remove adhering mother liquor.
6. **Drying:** Dry in air, desiccator, or low-temperature oven (below the melting point).
7. **Purity check:** Determine the **sharp melting point**. A narrow range (≤ 1–2 °C) indicates high purity.

**Why Mixed Solvent (Ethanol–Water)?**

Many organic compounds are:
- **Insoluble in pure water** (too polar).
- **Freely soluble in pure ethanol** even when cold (cannot crystallize).

Neither pure solvent works alone. However, in a **mixture of ethanol + water**, the polarity can be *tuned*:
- Dissolve the compound in **minimum hot ethanol**.
- Add **hot water dropwise** until the solution just turns turbid (compound about to precipitate).
- Cool → **pure crystals form**.

**Examples of mixed solvents:**
- **Ethanol + Water** — benzoic acid, acetanilide.
- **Acetone + Petroleum ether** — many neutral organics.
- **Methanol + Diethyl ether** — polar natural products.

**Labelled Diagram** (refer to **Section 4.3** for the recrystallization workflow block diagram).

---

#### Model Solution (b) — TLC Numerical & Interpretation

**Given:**
- Solvent front distance = 8.0 cm
- Spot 1 distance from origin = 2.4 cm
- Spot 2 distance from origin = 5.6 cm
- Co-spot with reference gives single spot at $R_f = 0.30$ in chloroform.

**Step 1: Calculate R_f values**

$$
\begin{aligned}
R_{f,1} &= \frac{2.4}{8.0} = 0.30 \\
R_{f,2} &= \frac{5.6}{8.0} = 0.70
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Interpretation**

- The crude sample contains **two components**.
- Component 1 ($R_f = 0.30$) is **more polar** (stronger adsorption on silica).
- Component 2 ($R_f = 0.70$) is **less polar**.
- The **co-spot** with the reference compound shows a **single spot** at $R_f = 0.30$, identical to Component 1. This strongly suggests that **Component 1 is the same compound as the reference**.
- However, **identical R_f in one solvent is not absolute proof** because two different compounds *can coincidentally* have the same R_f in a particular system.

**Step 3: Confirmatory Tests**

To confirm identity, the sample should be **co-spotted in at least two additional solvent systems** of different polarity:

| Solvent System | Polarity | Expected Result if Same |
|----------------|----------|--------------------------|
| **Ethyl acetate : Hexane (1 : 1)** | Medium | Single spot, R_f unchanged relative to reference |
| **Methanol : Chloroform (1 : 9)** | Slightly polar | Single spot at identical R_f |

**Conclusion:** If the unknown and reference continue to co-migrate as a single spot in **at least 2–3 different solvent systems**, the two compounds are considered **identical with high confidence**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Never** claim identity based on R_f in **one** solvent alone. KTU examiners specifically deduct marks for this.
> 2. Always **measure R_f to two decimal places** — rounding to one decimal is considered imprecise.
> 3. The **solvent front must be marked immediately** after removing the plate; otherwise evaporation skews the measurement.
> 4. Do **not** use pen to mark the origin — ink dissolves in the solvent and contaminates the plate. **Use pencil only**.
> 5. For crystallization, students commonly **add too much solvent**, causing the compound to stay in solution and not crystallize. Use the **minimum hot solvent**.

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Before the KTU Exam — Memorize This Checklist**

### A. Fractionation
- ☐ Fractionation separates **miscible liquids** with **ΔT_b ≥ 25 °C**.
- ☐ Raoult's Law: $P_{\text{total}} = P_A^{\circ}\, x_A + P_B^{\circ}\, x_B$.
- ☐ Vapour is **always enriched** in the more volatile component.
- ☐ Theoretical plate = one **complete vaporization–condensation equilibrium**.
- ☐ $n_{\text{th}} = L / \text{HETP}$.
- ☐ Boiling chips prevent **bumping** (superheating).
- ☐ Azeotropes (e.g., ethanol-water 95.6%) cannot be separated by simple fractionation.
- ☐ Vapour pressure increases with temperature for any liquid.

### B. Crystallization
- ☐ Suitable for **solid compounds** with **sharp melting points**.
- ☐ Solvent must dissolve compound when **hot** but **not** when cold.
- ☐ Impurities should be either **insoluble** (filtered) or **highly soluble** (remain in mother liquor).
- ☐ Use **minimum** hot solvent — excess solvent prevents crystallization.
- ☐ **Hot gravity filtration** removes insoluble impurities.
- ☐ **Suction filtration (Büchner)** separates crystals from mother liquor.
- ☐ **Sharp melting point** (≤ 1–2 °C range) confirms purity.
- ☐ Mixed solvents (e.g., ethanol-water) used when single solvent fails.
- ☐ **Activated charcoal** can be added to remove coloured impurities (and refiltered hot).

### C. Chromatography
- ☐ Two phases: **stationary** (fixed) and **mobile** (moving).
- ☐ TLC uses **silica gel** on glass/aluminium plate as stationary phase.
- ☐ $R_f = d_{\text{solute}} / d_{\text{solvent front}}$, range 0 to 1.
- ☐ **More polar** solutes → **lower** R_f on polar stationary phase.
- ☐ To increase R_f → use **more polar solvent**.
- ☐ **Co-chromatography** (co-spotting) with authentic sample = identification method.
- ☐ Confirm identity in **at least 2–3 different solvent systems**.
- ☐ Visualization: UV (254 nm), iodine chamber, ninhydrin (amino acids), KMnO₄ (alkenes).
- ☐ In column chromatography: **less polar elutes first**, more polar elutes last.
- ☐ Paper chromatography: stationary phase = water on cellulose; mobile = organic solvent.
- ☐ Always mark the **solvent front immediately** after removing the plate.
- ☐ Use **pencil only** to mark TLC plates (ink dissolves).

### D. Common KTU Lab Viva Questions
- ☐ "Why must the solvent be at the bottom of the developing chamber BELOW the origin line?" — *Otherwise the sample would dissolve directly into the bulk solvent instead of being carried by capillary action.*
- ☐ "What is the role of boiling chips?" — *Provide nucleation sites for smooth boiling, prevent bumping.*
- ☐ "Why is suction filtration faster than gravity filtration?" — *Vacuum increases pressure difference, accelerating flow.*
- ☐ "Why cool in ice bath after crystallization?" — *Maximize yield by reducing solubility further.*
- ☐ "Can two compounds have the same R_f in all solvent systems?" — *No; but if they appear identical in 3+ systems, they are considered identical.*

### E. Numerical Must-Knows
- ☐ Raoult's Law calculation (vapour composition).
- ☐ Theoretical plate calculation ($n_{\text{th}} = L/\text{HETP}$).
- ☐ R_f calculation from TLC distances.
- ☐ % yield = (recovered pure / crude taken) × 100.
- ☐ Solubility ratio interpretation (hot vs cold).
- ☐ Vapour pressure and total pressure calculations.

### F. KTU 2024 Mark Distribution Pattern (Module Experiment 2)
| Component | Marks | Weightage |
|-----------|-------|-----------|
| Aim, Apparatus, Chemicals | 2 | Brief |
| Principle + Reaction (if any) | 3–4 | High |
| Procedure | 4–5 | Highest |
| Result Table + Calculations | 3–4 | High |
| Precautions / Sources of Error | 1–2 | Often skipped by students — *easy marks* |
| Viva-voce (separate) | 2–5 | External |
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Separation of the components of a binary organic mixture (liquid-liquid, liquid-solid, and solid-solid)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Separation of the components of a binary organic mixture (liquid-liquid, liquid-solid, and solid-solid) # Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard) A **binary organic mixture** is a physical admixture of exactly two organic compounds (without any chemical reaction occurring between them). The goal of this experiment is to **separate, purify, and identify** each component by exploiting differences in their physical and chemical properties such as solubility, boiling point, melting point, and chemical reactivity. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Directive (PCPOL307 - Module 2):** > The student is expected to systematically separate a given binary mixture into its two pure components, then identify each by physical constants (b.p. / m.p.) and confirm identity by a single suitable derivative preparation. ### 1.2 The Three Canonical Mixture Categories | # | Mixture Type | Definition | Classical Example | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Liquid–Liquid** | Both components are miscible or partially miscible liquids | Benzene + Toluene; Water + Benzene | | 2 | **Liquid–Solid** | One component is a liquid and the other is a solid | Naphthalene + Water; Benzoic acid + Aniline | | 3 | **Solid–Solid** | Both components are solids (one usually water-soluble) | Naphthalene + Benzoic acid; Benzoic acid + Salicylic acid | ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Two-Spice Jar"** > Imagine a kitchen jar containing a mix of **salt (solid, water-soluble)** and **pepper (solid, water-insoluble)**. To separate them you pour the mix into water — the salt dissolves, the pepper floats/sinks, and you filter. This is exactly what a chemist does, only with a separating funnel, distillation, or filtration apparatus, and with much more precision (often to within **±1 °C** of the true boiling/melting point). ## 1.4 The Five Sequential Operations (Universal Workflow) Every binary mixture experiment — irrespective of physical state — follows this **Universal 5-Step Workflow**: 1. **Preliminary Examination** — Note colour, odour, physical state, litmus test. 2. **Separation** — Exploit a property difference (solubility, volatility, etc.). 3. **Drying & Purification** — Remove residual water/solvent; distill or recrystallize. 4. **Physical Constant Determination** — Boiling point (liquids) or Melting point (solids). 5. **Derivative Preparation** — A characteristic solid derivative confirms identity. ## 1.5 Key Physical Constants (KTU Acceptable Standards) > [!IMPORTANT] > The KTU board examiner expects the following accuracies: > - **Boiling Point** accurate to **±2 °C** of the literature value > - **Melting Point** accurate to **±1 °C** of the literature value > - **Derivative M.P.** accurate to **±2 °C** of the literature value --- # Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Strategy Selection Matrix — Which Method to Use? | Component A | Component B | Separation Strategy | Apparatus | |---|---|---|---| | Liquid (immiscible with water) | Liquid (miscible with water) | Solvent extraction (separating funnel) | Separating funnel | | Liquid (volatile) | Solid (non-volatile) | **Simple Distillation** | Distillation flask + condenser | | Solid (water-soluble) | Solid (water-insoluble) | **Filtration** (A dissolves, B remains) | Beaker + filter paper | | Solid (organic-soluble) | Solid (water-soluble) | Selective solvent extraction | Separating funnel / filtration | | Both solids (different solubilities in same solvent) | — | **Fractional Crystallization** | Beaker + funnel | ## 2.2 Theoretical Principles ### 2.2.1 Liquid–Liquid Separation (Solvent Extraction) > [!NOTE] > **Principle:** Two immiscible liquids in contact distribute a solute between them according to **Nernst's Distribution Law** (1890). The ratio of concentrations of solute in the two immiscible solvents at equilibrium, at constant temperature, is a constant called the **Distribution Coefficient** ($K_D$ or $K$). The *efficiency of extraction* increases by using **multiple small portions** of solvent rather than one large portion — this is the heart of the theoretical justification for repeated extractions. ### 2.2.2 Liquid–Solid Separation (Simple Distillation) > [!NOTE] > **Principle:** When a liquid is heated to its boiling point, its vapour pressure equals the atmospheric pressure (**Clausius–Clapeyron relationship**). The vapour travels through the condenser, loses latent heat, and re-liquefies. The non-volatile solid remains in the distillation flask (the **residue**), while the pure liquid distils over as the **distillate**. ### 2.2.3 Solid–Solid Separation (Selective Solubility + Fractional Crystallization) > [!NOTE] > **Principle:** Two solids differing in solubility in a chosen solvent can be separated when one is highly soluble and the other is poorly soluble. The principle of **"Like Dissolves Like"** governs solvent choice. **Fractional crystallization** exploits the differing temperature-dependence of solubility. The less-soluble solid crystallizes first on cooling. ## 2.3 The Drying Agents — Quick Reference | Drying Agent | Formula | Used For | Capacity | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | Anhydrous Calcium Chloride | $\text{CaCl}_2$ | Hydrocarbons, ethers, alkyl halides | High | Cheap; cannot dry alcohols/amines | | Anhydrous Sodium Sulphate | $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ | General purpose; neutral | Medium | Inexpensive, slow | | Anhydrous Magnesium Sulphate | $\text{MgSO}_4$ | Acid-sensitive compounds | High | Universal, fast | | Anhydrous Potassium Hydroxide | $\text{KOH}$ | Amines (basic compounds) | High | Cannot dry acids/phenols | | Phosphorus Pentoxide | $\text{P}_2\text{O}_5$ | Acidic & neutral compounds | Very high | Most powerful, expensive | > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** Never use **anhydrous CaCl₂** for alcohols or amines — it forms addition complexes and chemically binds the compound. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Expression | Application / Notes | |---|---|---| | Nernst Distribution Law | $K_D = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ | $C_1, C_2$ = equilibrium concentrations in two solvents | | % Extraction efficiency | $\% E = \dfrac{K_D V_1}{K_D V_1 + V_2} \times 100$ | $V_1$ = aq. vol, $V_2$ = org. vol | | Clausius–Clapeyron | $\ln P = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{RT} + C$ | Vapour pressure vs temperature | | Raoult's Law | $P_{total} = P_A^\circ x_A + P_B^\circ x_B$ | For miscible liquids | | Boiling Point Condition | $P_{vap} = P_{atm}$ ($P_{atm} = \mathbf{760\ mmHg}$) | At normal b.p. | | Reduction of b.p. by vacuum | $\text{New b.p.} \approx \text{Std b.p.} - 0.05 \times (760 - P)$ | $P$ in mmHg | | Melting Point Depression | $\Delta T_f = K_f \cdot m$ | For impure solids (colligative) | | Crystallization yield | $\%\text{ yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass}}{\text{Theoretical mass}} \times 100$ | — | ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Every drug is isolated and purified by solvent extraction, distillation, or recrystallization. - **Petroleum Refining:** Crude oil is separated into fractions (gasoline, kerosene, diesel) by fractional distillation — a direct industrial scale-up of this experiment. - **Food Industry:** Decaffeination of coffee uses liquid–liquid extraction with supercritical $\text{CO}_2$. - **Environmental Engineering:** Trace organic pollutants are extracted from water using immiscible organic solvents (e.g., dichloromethane). --- # Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Why Multiple Small Extractions are Better than One Large Extraction (Nernst's Law) **Problem Setup:** - Aqueous volume = $V_{aq}$ mL containing $W_0$ grams of solute. - Organic volume = $V_{org}$ mL (used in either 1 or $n$ extractions). - Distribution coefficient $K_D = \dfrac{C_{org}}{C_{aq}}$. ### Case 1: Single Extraction with Volume $V_{org}$ Let $W_1$ be the mass of solute remaining in the aqueous layer after one extraction. By Nernst's Law: $$\dfrac{(W_0 - W_1)/V_{org}}{W_1/V_{aq}} = K_D$$ Rearranging: $$(W_0 - W_1) \cdot V_{aq} = K_D \cdot W_1 \cdot V_{org}$$ $$W_0 \cdot V_{aq} - W_1 \cdot V_{aq} = K_D \cdot W_1 \cdot V_{org}$$ $$W_0 \cdot V_{aq} = W_1 (K_D \cdot V_{org} + V_{aq})$$ $$W_1 = W_0 \cdot \dfrac{V_{aq}}{K_D \cdot V_{org} + V_{aq}}$$ ### Case 2: $n$ Successive Extractions each of Volume $V_{org}/n$ After $n$ extractions, the residual mass $W_n$ in the aqueous layer is: $$W_n = W_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{aq}}{K_D \cdot (V_{org}/n) + V_{aq}}\right)^n$$ **Conclusion:** Since $V_{org}/n < V_{org}$, the term in the denominator is smaller, so the fraction is larger, meaning the exponent $n$ amplifies the extraction efficiency. Hence: **multiple small extractions > one large extraction.** ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Clausius–Clapeyron Equation (Basis of Distillation) Starting from the equality of chemical potentials of liquid and vapour phases at equilibrium: $$\mu_{liq}(T, P) = \mu_{vap}(T, P)$$ Differentiating with respect to $T$ at constant $P$, applying the Gibbs–Helmholtz relation, and integrating between two temperatures $T_1$ and $T_2$: $$\ln\left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right) = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_2} - \dfrac{1}{T_1}\right)$$ This is the integrated form used in practice. It tells us that vapour pressure rises **exponentially** with temperature — hence a small increase in temperature dramatically increases volatility, enabling clean separation at the boiling point. ## 3.3 Step-by-Step Procedure Block — One of Each Type ### 3.3.1 Liquid–Liquid Separation (e.g., Benzene + Water) > [!NOTE] > **Use Case:** Both are liquids, mutually immiscible. 1. Take the mixture in a **separating funnel** mounted on an iron stand. 2. Add **saturated NaCl solution** (brine) to break emulsions and increase ionic strength. 3. **Stopper** the funnel, invert, open the stopcock to release pressure (do this 3–4 times). 4. Allow layers to settle for **5–10 minutes**. 5. **Drain the lower (aqueous) layer** through the stopcock into a beaker. 6. **Pour out the upper (organic) layer** through the top mouth (prevents re-contamination). 7. Dry the organic layer over **anhydrous CaCl₂** (since benzene is a hydrocarbon) for ~15 min. 8. Filter, then **distil** to obtain pure benzene (b.p. **80 °C**). 9. Evaporate the aqueous layer to recover the water-soluble component. ### 3.3.2 Liquid–Solid Separation (e.g., Naphthalene solid + Aniline liquid) 1. Note the two layers / phases in the original mixture. 2. Filter to collect the **solid (Naphthalene)**. 3. Wash the solid with cold water; dry between filter papers. 4. **Recrystallize** Naphthalene from hot ethanol. 5. Determine m.p. of Naphthalene (literature: **80 °C**). 6. Treat the filtrate (containing Aniline) with **anhydrous KOH** to remove water. 7. **Distil** the dried Aniline (literature b.p.: **184 °C**). 8. Prepare a derivative of Aniline (e.g., acetanilide, m.p. **114 °C**). ### 3.3.3 Solid–Solid Separation (e.g., Benzoic acid + Naphthalene) 1. Add the mixture to a beaker containing **hot water**. 2. **Benzoic acid** dissolves; **Naphthalene** melts and floats (or remains as insoluble solid). 3. Filter the hot solution to separate solid Naphthalene (residue) from the aqueous Benzoic acid (filtrate). 4. **Cool the filtrate** → Benzoic acid crystallizes out (white crystals, m.p. **122 °C**). 5. Recrystallize Benzoic acid from hot water. 6. Wash and dry the Naphthalene residue; recrystallize from hot ethanol (m.p. **80 °C**). 7. Prepare derivatives: - Benzoic acid → **Benzoic acid 2,4-dinitrophenylhydrazide** or amide - Naphthalene → **Picrate** (m.p. **149 °C**) ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Identification Pathway | Observation | Inference | Action | |---|---|---| | White solid, soluble in NaOH | Acidic (carboxylic acid / phenol) | Acidify to recover | | Yellow solid, sweet smell, m.p. 122 °C | Likely **Benzoic acid** | Prepare amide/anilide | | White flakes, m.p. 80 °C, camphor-like smell | Likely **Naphthalene** | Prepare picrate | | Liquid, b.p. 78 °C, fruity smell | Likely **Ethanol** | Prepare 3,5-DNB or iodoform | | Liquid, b.p. 110 °C, oily, phenolic smell | Likely **Phenol** | Prepare tribromo-derivative | | Liquid, immiscible with water, b.p. 80 °C | Likely **Benzene** | Prepare $m$-DNB (m.p. 89 °C) | | Liquid, fishy smell, basic to litmus | Likely **Amine** (aniline etc.) | Prepare benzoyl derivative | --- # Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Universal Workflow Flowchart (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Start: Binary Mixture Received] --> B[Preliminary Examination
Colour, Odour, State, Litmus] B --> C{Physical State?} C -- Both liquids --> D[Liquid-Liquid
Separating Funnel] C -- Liquid + Solid --> E[Liquid-Solid
Simple Distillation] C -- Both solids --> F[Solid-Solid
Selective Solubility] D --> G[Component A pure] D --> H[Component B pure] E --> G E --> H F --> G F --> H G --> I[Determine b.p. or m.p.] H --> I I --> J[Compare with Literature Table] J --> K[Confirm by Derivative Preparation] K --> L[End: Identified Binary Mixture] ``` ## 4.2 Separating Funnel Operation (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Setup[Separating Funnel on Stand] S1[Stopcock Closed] --> S2[Pour Mixture via Top] S2 --> S3[Add Brine or Distilled Water] S3 --> S4[Stopper and Shake with Venting] end Setup --> S5[Allow Layers to Separate] S5 --> S6[Lower Aqueous Layer out via Stopcock] S6 --> S7[Pour Organic Layer out via Top] S7 --> S8[Dry over Anhydrous CaCl2 or Na2SO4] S8 --> S9[Distil or Evaporate to obtain Pure Compound] ``` ## 4.3 Distillation Apparatus — Block Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Distillation Flask
with boiling chips] -->|Vapour| B[Vapour Rises] B --> C[Thermometer at Side Arm
Bulb at Side Arm Level] C --> D[Condenser
Water in at Bottom
Water out at Top] D --> E[Pure Distillate Drips Down] E --> F[Receiving Flask] G[Heat Source
Mantle or Burner] -->|Heat| A H[Non-volatile Residue] -->|Stays behind| A ``` ## 4.4 Fractional Crystallization Logic (Solid–Solid) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Solid-Solid Mixture] --> B[Add Selective Hot Solvent] B --> C{Which dissolves more?} C -- Component X dissolves --> D[Filter Hot: Y remains as Residue] C -- Both dissolve --> E[Cool Slowly: Less soluble X crystallizes first] D --> F[Cool Filtrate: X crystallizes] E --> G[Filter X out] F --> H[Recrystallize X from Fresh Solvent] G --> H H --> I[Pure X obtained] D --> J[Dry Residue Y and Recrystallize] J --> K[Pure Y obtained] ``` ## 4.5 Decision Tree — Solvent Selection for Solid–Solid ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Both solids] --> B{Is one water-soluble?} B -- Yes --> C[Use Water] B -- No --> D{Is one alcohol-soluble?} D -- Yes --> E[Use Ethanol or Methanol] D -- No --> F[Try Diethyl Ether or Acetone] C --> G[Hot Solvent Extraction] E --> G F --> G G --> H[Cool and Crystallize] ``` --- # Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model] **"What is Nernst's Distribution Law? State its mathematical form and the condition under which it is valid."** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Statement (1 mark):** Nernst's Distribution Law (1890) states that *"if a solute distributes itself between two immiscible solvents in contact with each other, the ratio of its concentrations in the two solvents is constant at constant temperature."* - **Mathematical form (1 mark):** $K_D = \dfrac{C_1}{C_2}$ (constant at constant T), where $C_1$ and $C_2$ are the equilibrium concentrations in the two solvents. - **Condition (1 mark):** The law is valid only when (i) temperature is constant, (ii) the two solvents are mutually immiscible, and (iii) the solute does not undergo association, dissociation, or chemical reaction in either phase. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model] **"Why is anhydrous calcium chloride not used as a drying agent for alcohols and amines?"** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - (1 mark) Anhydrous $\text{CaCl}_2$ is a **Lewis acid** because the calcium centre is electron-deficient. - (1 mark) It forms stable **molecular addition complexes** (e.g., $\text{CaCl}_2 \cdot 4\text{ROH}$, $\text{CaCl}_2 \cdot 2\text{RNH}_2$) with molecules containing lone-pair-bearing donor atoms (O in alcohols, N in amines). - (1 mark) As a result, it chemically binds the alcohol/amine rather than just adsorbing water, leading to **irreversible loss of the compound** — hence unsuitable. --- ## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks) ### **[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]** **Q. (a) A binary mixture contains Benzoic acid (solid) and Naphthalene (solid). Describe step-by-step how you would separate, purify, and identify each component. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Separation Strategy (1 Mark):** Both are solids; selective solubility in hot water is exploited. Benzoic acid is highly soluble in hot water; Naphthalene is insoluble. **Procedure (4 Marks):** 1. Weigh the mixture; note its mass. 2. Transfer to a beaker; add **minimum quantity of hot distilled water** (just enough to dissolve the benzoic acid). 3. **Filter the hot solution** through a pre-warmed funnel → **Naphthalene remains as residue** on the filter paper; **Benzoic acid** is in the hot filtrate. 4. **Cool the filtrate** in an ice bath → Benzoic acid crystallizes out. 5. Filter the crystals; **wash with cold water**; dry between filter papers. 6. **Recrystallize** Benzoic acid from minimum hot water. 7. Wash the Naphthalene residue with cold water; dry; **recrystallize from hot ethanol**. 8. **Determine the m.p.** of both: - Benzoic acid: literature m.p. **122 °C** → confirm identity. - Naphthalene: literature m.p. **80 °C** → confirm identity. **Derivative (1 Mark):** Prepare the **amide of benzoic acid** (benzamide, m.p. **128 °C**) by reaction with $\text{SOCl}_2$ followed by $\text{NH}_3$; prepare the **picrate of naphthalene** (m.p. **149 °C**) using picric acid. **Confirmation (1 Mark):** Match observed physical constants and derivative m.p. with literature values. --- **Q. (b) State Nernst's Distribution Law. Show mathematically why three successive extractions using 30 mL of solvent each are more efficient than one single extraction with 90 mL of solvent. Assume the distribution coefficient $K_D = 4$ and aqueous volume = 100 mL. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Nernst's Law (1 Mark):** $K_D = \dfrac{C_{org}}{C_{aq}}$ at constant temperature, valid for a solute distributing between two immiscible solvents without chemical change. **Derivation Setup (1 Mark):** - $V_{aq} = 100$ mL, $V_{org} = 90$ mL (case 1) or 30 mL (case 2), $K_D = 4$. - Let $W_0 = 1$ g (the original mass of solute for easy comparison). **Case 1: Single extraction with 90 mL (2 Marks)** $$W_1 = W_0 \cdot \dfrac{V_{aq}}{K_D \cdot V_{org} + V_{aq}} = 1 \times \dfrac{100}{4 \times 90 + 100} = \dfrac{100}{460} = 0.2174\ \text{g}$$ So extracted = $1 - 0.2174 = 0.7826$ g, i.e. **78.26 %** extracted. **Case 2: Three extractions with 30 mL each (2 Marks)** $$W_3 = W_0 \cdot \left(\dfrac{V_{aq}}{K_D \cdot V_{org} + V_{aq}}\right)^3 = 1 \times \left(\dfrac{100}{4 \times 30 + 100}\right)^3$$ $$W_3 = \left(\dfrac{100}{220}\right)^3 = (0.4545)^3 = 0.0939\ \text{g}$$ So extracted = $1 - 0.0939 = 0.9061$ g, i.e. **90.61 %** extracted. **Conclusion (1 Mark):** Three extractions of 30 mL each recover **90.61 %** of the solute, whereas one extraction of 90 mL recovers only **78.26 %**. Hence multiple small extractions are more efficient. --- ## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks) ### **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model – Alternative]** **Q. (a) A binary mixture contains Aniline (liquid) and Naphthalene (solid). Describe the complete procedure for separation, purification, and identification. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Preliminary Examination (1 Mark):** Aniline — colourless oily liquid, fishy smell, basic to litmus. Naphthalene — white crystalline solid, camphor-like odour, m.p. ~80 °C. **Separation (3 Marks):** 1. Transfer the mixture to a **Buchner funnel** and **filter under suction** to collect the solid Naphthalene as residue. 2. Wash the Naphthalene crystals with a small amount of **cold water** to remove adhering aniline. 3. The filtrate (aniline + water) is transferred to a **separating funnel**. 4. Extract aniline into **ether** (3 × 30 mL); the ether layer contains aniline; the aqueous layer is discarded. 5. **Dry the ether extract** over **anhydrous KOH** (since aniline is basic). 6. **Distil off the ether** (b.p. 35 °C) to obtain crude aniline. 7. **Distil** crude aniline at **184 °C** to obtain pure aniline. **Purification of Naphthalene (2 Marks):** 1. Dry the solid between filter papers. 2. **Recrystallize** from hot ethanol. 3. Dry the crystals; determine **m.p.** (literature: **80 °C**). **Identification / Derivative (1 Mark):** - For Aniline, prepare the **benzoyl derivative** (benzanilide) by Schotten–Baumann reaction (m.p. **163 °C**). - For Naphthalene, prepare the **picrate** (m.p. **149 °C**). --- **Q. (b) Explain the principle of fractional crystallization. Mention the four essential criteria for the choice of a recrystallization solvent. (7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** **Principle (3 Marks):** Fractional crystallization is the technique of separating two solids dissolved in a single solvent by **controlled cooling**. It exploits the fact that the solubilities of the two solids have different temperature coefficients. On slow cooling, the **less-soluble solid saturates first** and crystallizes out, while the more-soluble solid remains in solution (the "mother liquor"). The two are then separated by filtration. Repeating the process with the mother liquor concentrates and recovers the more-soluble component. **Four Criteria for Solvent Choice (4 Marks — 1 each):** 1. **High solubility of the compound at the boiling point and low solubility at room/cold temperature** — to maximize recovery on cooling. 2. **The impurities must be either completely insoluble (filtered off hot) or completely soluble (remain in mother liquor on cooling).** 3. **The solvent must not react chemically with the solute** — chemically inert. 4. **The solvent should be volatile, low-boiling, and easily removable** from the crystals after filtration; it should also be cheap and safe (e.g., water, ethanol, acetone, ethyl acetate). --- ## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in This Question (as per KTU 2018–2024 valuation patterns):** > 1. **Forgetting to dry the organic layer** before distillation → water contaminates the distillate → wrong b.p. → **−2 marks**. > 2. **Pouring the lower organic layer out through the top mouth** of the separating funnel → it gets contaminated by the residual upper layer → **−2 marks**. > 3. **Not using anti-bumping granules (boiling chips)** during distillation → superheating causes violent bumping, breaking the apparatus and losing the sample → **−3 marks**. > 4. **Confusing benzoic acid and naphthalene** in the separation (using cold water when hot is needed, or vice versa) → both components lost → **−4 marks**. > 5. **Reporting m.p./b.p. without comparing to literature value** → identity is "not confirmed" → **−1 mark**. --- ## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Know Before Entering the KTU Lab Exam:** - [ ] The **3 mixture types**: Liquid–Liquid, Liquid–Solid, Solid–Solid. - [ ] **Separating funnel** → for immiscible liquids; **distillation** → for liquid + non-volatile solid; **filtration + recrystallization** → for solid + solid. - [ ] **Nernst's Distribution Law:** $K_D = C_1/C_2$; multiple small extractions > one large extraction. - [ ] **Drying agents:** $\text{CaCl}_2$ for hydrocarbons; $\text{KOH}$ for amines; $\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$ general purpose; **never** $\text{CaCl}_2$ for alcohols/amines. - [ ] **Boiling chips** are mandatory during distillation to prevent bumping. - [ ] **Standard physical constants** (must memorize): - Benzene b.p. **80 °C** - Toluene b.p. **110 °C** - Aniline b.p. **184 °C** - Naphthalene m.p. **80 °C** - Benzoic acid m.p. **122 °C** - Salicylic acid m.p. **159 °C** - Phenol m.p. **40 °C** (b.p. **182 °C**) - [ ] **Derivative pairing** (must memorize): - Carboxylic acid → amide / anilide / 2,4-DNPH - Amine → benzoyl / acetyl / picrate - Alcohol → 3,5-DNB / α-naphthylurethane - Aldehyde/Ketone → 2,4-DNPH / semicarbazone - Aromatic hydrocarbon → picrate / nitro-derivative - [ ] **Accuracy expected:** b.p. ±2 °C; m.p. ±1 °C; derivative m.p. ±2 °C. - [ ] **Clausius–Clapeyron** equation explains why b.p. drops under reduced pressure. - [ ] **Recrystallization solvent criteria:** high solubility hot, low solubility cold, no chemical reaction, volatile, cheap. - [ ] **Always record observations** in the lab record (colour, odour, m.p., b.p., derivative m.p.) — this is **mandatory** for KTU external evaluation.

topics.title: Identification of the components of the given binary mixture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCPOL307 - CHEMISTRY LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Identification of the components of the given binary mixture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Binary Mixture Analysis (Qualitative Inorganic Analysis):** A systematic, sequential wet-chemistry procedure used to identify **two distinct ions** (one cation and one anion) present together in a single homogeneous or heterogeneous solid sample, by exploiting differences in solubility, selective precipitation, and characteristic confirmatory reactions.

According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus (PCPOL307)**, this experiment trains students in the **scientific method of deductive reasoning** applied to analytical chemistry — narrowing down possibilities through a cascade of group tests until a single, unique ion is confirmed.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Detective's Toolkit"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition:** Imagine a sealed lunchbox containing two unknown spices. A chef (the analyst) cannot see inside. The chef first sniffs (odor test), touches (texture), tastes a pinch (limited solubility clues), then burns a tiny sample over a flame (flame test) — the colors and smells give clues. Next, the chef dissolves the spices in water, then adds specific "trapping agents" (group reagents) that selectively capture one spice at a time, identifying them through characteristic color changes — just like a detective catching suspects one by one through unique fingerprints.

### 1.3 Key Standardized Metrics

- **Standard Reference Temperature:** $25^{\circ} \text{C}$ (298.15 K) for all solubility data.
- **Group Separation pH Values:**
  - Group II: $\text{pH} \approx 0.5$ (strongly acidic, dil. HCl medium)
  - Group III: $\text{pH} \approx 9$ (ammoniacal, $\text{NH}_4\text{Cl}/\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$ buffer)
  - Group IV: $\text{pH} \approx 9$ (ammoniacal medium with $\text{H}_2\text{S}$)
  - Group V: $\text{pH} \approx 0.3$ (acidic medium, $\text{HCl} + \text{H}_2\text{S}$)
- **Concentration Standard:** Reagents are typically **N/1 (1 Normal)** unless specified otherwise.
- **Sample Mass:** $\approx 0.5 \text{ g}$ of the dry mixture is sufficient for complete analysis.

### 1.4 The Three-Tier Analytical Architecture

| Tier | Test Type | Information Yielded | Time |
|------|-----------|-------------------|------|
| Tier 1 | Preliminary (Physical + Dry) | Color, odor, solubility, flame/bead colors | ~10 min |
| Tier 2 | Group Tests (Wet) | Identifies the analytical group of cation | ~30 min |
| Tier 3 | Confirmatory Tests | Confirms the **exact** ion present | ~15 min |

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Logical Flow of Analysis

The binary mixture identification follows a **strict hierarchical decision tree**. Each step eliminates possibilities; no step can be skipped without risking misidentification.

**Step 1 — Preliminary Examination**
- **Why:** Non-destructive, gives the first set of clues.
- **How:** Observe color (e.g., blue = Cu²⁺; green = Fe²⁺/Ni²⁺; pink = Co²⁺), smell (NH₄⁺ salts smell of ammonia when heated), and texture (crystalline vs. amorphous).

**Step 2 — Solubility Test**
- **Why:** Determines the dissolution strategy and the choice of group reagent solvent.
- **How:** Test solubility sequentially in **water → dil. HCl → conc. HCl → dil. HNO₃**. The solvent in which the mixture fully dissolves dictates the preparation of the **stock solution** (also called *original solution* or OS).

**Step 3 — Flame Test**
- **Why:** Identifies certain cations via characteristic emission spectra (Bohr's model: $E = h\nu = hc/\lambda$).
- **How:** Dip a clean Pt/Ni-wire loop in the sample + conc. HCl; introduce into Bunsen flame.
  - Brick red → Ca²⁺
  - Apple green → Ba²⁺
  - Crimson red → Sr²⁺
  - Violet (lilac) → K⁺
  - Yellow → Na⁺ (masked by cobalt blue glass)
  - Green (blue-green) → Cu²⁺

**Step 4 — Group Analysis of Cation**
- **Why:** Six groups exist, separated by the solubility of their sulfides, hydroxides, or chlorides at specific pH — this is the **backbone of qualitative analysis**.
- **How:** Add HCl → Group I (Pb²⁺, Ag⁺, Hg₂²⁺ as chlorides). Pass H₂S in acidic medium → Group II (Cu²⁺, Pb²⁺, Bi³⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺, As³⁺, Sb³⁺, Sn²⁺/⁴⁺ as sulfides). Add NH₄Cl/NH₄OH → Group III (Al³⁺, Fe³⁺, Cr³⁺ as hydroxides). Pass H₂S in ammoniacal medium → Group IV (Zn²⁺, Mn²⁺, Ni²⁺, Co²⁺ as sulfides). Add (NH₄)₂CO₃ → Group V (Ca²⁺, Sr²⁺, Ba²⁺ as carbonates). Group VI: Mg²⁺, Na⁺, K⁺, NH₄⁺ (no group reagent, identified directly).

**Step 5 — Identification of Anion**
- **Why:** The anion in the original solution is identified using separate tests on the **water extract** (or soda extract for water-insoluble salts).
- **How:** Classical wet tests: dil. H₂SO₄ + warm → CO₃²⁻ (effervescence of CO₂); AgNO₃ in dil. HNO₃ → Cl⁻ (white ppt, soluble in NH₄OH); BaCl₂ in dil. HCl → SO₄²⁻ (white ppt insoluble in acid); brown ring test → NO₃⁻; etc.

**Step 6 — Confirmatory Tests**
- **Why:** Group tests are *necessary but not sufficient* — multiple ions can be in the same group.
- **How:** Specific reagents give unique precipitates, colors, or crystalline forms for each ion.

### 2.2 The Underlying Theoretical Foundation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Principle — Selective Precipitation via Solubility Product ($K_{sp}$):** A salt precipitates when its ionic product $Q$ exceeds the solubility product constant $K_{sp}$ at the given temperature. By carefully controlling $\left[\text{H}^+\right]$, we can selectively exceed the $K_{sp}$ of one group's sulfide while keeping another's below it.

The conditional solubility product for metal sulfides is:

$$K_{sp}^{\text{conditional}} = \frac{K_{sp} \cdot \left[\text{H}^+\right]^2}{\left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]^2 \cdot K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2}}$$

where $K_{a1}$ and $K_{a2}$ are the first and second dissociation constants of $\text{H}_2\text{S}$, and $\left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right] \approx 0.1 \text{ M}$ in a saturated solution.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity / Concept | Formula / Value | Units / Conditions |
|--------------------|-----------------|-------------------|
| Solubility Product | $K_{sp} = \left[\text{M}^{n+}\right] \left[\text{A}^{m-}\right]^{m/n}$ | Pure water, $25^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| Ionic Product | $Q$ | Compared to $K_{sp}$ |
| Precipitation condition | $Q > K_{sp}$ | Precipitate forms |
| No precipitation | $Q \leq K_{sp}$ | Solution unsaturated |
| Nernst-like pH for $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ sep. | $\text{pH} \leq 1$ for Group II | Strongly acidic |
| pH for Group III/IV | $\text{pH} \approx 9$ | Ammoniacal buffer |
| Equivalent weight of salt | $E = \dfrac{M}{n}$ | $n$ = total cationic/anionic charge |
| Mass of mixture standard | $\approx 0.5 \text{ g}$ | Practical lab quantity |
| Nernst flame-test relation | $E_{photon} = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | $\lambda$ in nm; $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\text{ J·s}$ |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Environmental Engineering:** Identifying heavy-metal contaminants (Pb²⁺, Cd²⁺, Hg²⁺) in water and soil using the same group-separation logic.
- **Pharmaceutical QC:** Purity testing of salt-based drug formulations (e.g., confirming $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ in calcium supplements, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$ in barium-sulfate suspensions).
- **Metallurgy & Mining:** Ore characterization — qualitative analysis of mineral samples.
- **Forensic Chemistry:** Identification of unknown residues in crime-scene powders.
- **Geotechnical Engineering:** Soil-chemistry analysis for sulfate attack on concrete (identifying $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$).

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Why Group II Sulfides Precipitate in Acidic Medium but Group IV Does Not

Consider a generic divalent metal sulfide $\text{MS}$ dissociating as:

$$\text{MS}_{(s)} \rightleftharpoons \text{M}^{2+}_{(aq)} + \text{S}^{2-}_{(aq)} \quad ; \quad K_{sp} = \left[\text{M}^{2+}\right]\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$$

In saturated $\text{H}_2\text{S}$ solution, the sulfide ion is generated via two equilibria:

$$\text{H}_2\text{S}_{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+_{(aq)} + \text{HS}^-_{(aq)} \quad ; \quad K_{a1} = \dfrac{\left[\text{H}^+\right]\left[\text{HS}^-\right]}{\left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]}$$

$$\text{HS}^-_{(aq)} \rightleftharpoons \text{H}^+_{(aq)} + \text{S}^{2-}_{(aq)} \quad ; \quad K_{a2} = \dfrac{\left[\text{H}^+\right]\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\text{HS}^-\right]}$$

Multiplying both:

$$K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} = \dfrac{\left[\text{H}^+\right]^2 \left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]}{\left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]}$$

Rearranging for $\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$:

$$\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right] = \dfrac{K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} \cdot \left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]}{\left[\text{H}^+\right]^2}$$

Substituting into the $K_{sp}$ condition:

$$\left[\text{M}^{2+}\right] > \dfrac{K_{sp} \cdot \left[\text{H}^+\right]^2}{K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} \cdot \left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]}$$

This is the **conditional solubility product** — the minimum $\left[\text{M}^{2+}\right]$ needed to precipitate as sulfide at a given $\left[\text{H}^+\right]$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Numerical illustration — Separation of Group II (CuS) from Group IV (ZnS):**
> 
> - $K_{sp}(\text{CuS}) = 10^{-36}$, $K_{sp}(\text{ZnS}) = 10^{-21}$
> - $K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} \approx 10^{-22}$, $\left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right] = 0.1 \text{ M}$
> 
> At $\left[\text{H}^+\right] = 0.3 \text{ M}$ (Group II condition):
> 
> $$\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right] = \dfrac{10^{-22} \times 0.1}{(0.3)^2} = \dfrac{10^{-23}}{0.09} \approx 1.1 \times 10^{-22} \text{ M}$$
> 
> For CuS to precipitate, $\left[\text{Cu}^{2+}\right] \left[\text{S}^{2-}\right] > 10^{-36}$:
> 
> $$\left[\text{Cu}^{2+}\right] > \dfrac{10^{-36}}{1.1 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 9 \times 10^{-15} \text{ M}$$
> 
> For ZnS to precipitate at the same acidity, $\left[\text{Zn}^{2+}\right] > \dfrac{10^{-21}}{1.1 \times 10^{-22}} \approx 9 \text{ M}$ — **impossible** in a normal solution. Hence Zn²⁺ remains in solution and precipitates only in ammoniacal medium (Group IV) where $\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$ is far higher.

### 3.4 Worked Example — Confirmatory Test Logic for $\text{Fe}^{3+}$

**Scenario:** Group III gives a reddish-brown precipitate with $\text{NH}_4\text{OH}$ — consistent with $\text{Fe(OH)}_3$ *or* possibly a mixture.

**Confirmatory Reaction 1 — Potassium Ferrocyanide:**

$$4\text{FeCl}_3 + 3\text{K}_4\left[\text{Fe(CN)}_6\right] \longrightarrow \text{Fe}_4\left[\text{Fe(CN)}_6\right]_3 \downarrow + 12\text{KCl}$$

The precipitate is **Prussian blue** (ferric ferrocyanide) — *unmistakable* confirmation of $\text{Fe}^{3+}$.

**Confirmatory Reaction 2 — Potassium Thiocyanate:**

$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\text{SCN}^- \longrightarrow \left[\text{Fe(SCN)}_3\right] \quad \text{(Blood-red color in solution)}$$

This reaction is **highly sensitive** (detection limit $\approx 10^{-6} \text{ M}$) and is the standard textbook confirmatory test.

**Confirmatory Reaction 3 — With $\text{NaOH}$:**

$$\text{Fe}^{3+} + 3\text{OH}^- \longrightarrow \underbrace{\text{Fe(OH)}_3}_{\text{reddish-brown ppt}} \downarrow$$

The ppt is **insoluble in excess NaOH** (distinguishing $\text{Fe}^{3+}$ from $\text{Al}^{3+}$ and $\text{Zn}^{2+}$, which dissolve in excess NaOH as $\left[\text{Al(OH)}_4\right]^-$ and $\left[\text{Zn(OH)}_4\right]^{2-}$ respectively).

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q1]**
> **Q: What is meant by "group reagent" in qualitative analysis? Give two examples.**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> A *group reagent* is a reagent that selectively precipitates a specific group of cations (sharing a common chemical property) while leaving others in solution, enabling systematic separation. **Examples:** (1) **Dilute HCl** is the group reagent for Group I cations (Pb²⁺, Ag⁺, Hg₂²⁺), precipitating them as insoluble chlorides. (2) **Hydrogen sulfide (H₂S) gas in acidic medium** is the group reagent for Group II cations, precipitating them as sulfides with very low $K_{sp}$ values.

> **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q2]**
> **Q: Why is Group III analysis carried out in the presence of NH₄Cl along with NH₄OH?**
>
> **Model Answer:**
> NH₄Cl acts as a **buffer** (along with NH₄OH) to maintain the pH at approximately **9**, which is the critical pH required for the complete precipitation of Group III hydroxides (Al(OH)₃, Fe(OH)₃, Cr(OH)₃) while **preventing the precipitation of Group IV cations** (Zn²⁺, Mn²⁺, Ni²⁺, Co²⁺) as hydroxides, which would otherwise contaminate Group III. The buffer also suppresses the ionization of NH₄OH, preventing Mg²⁺ from precipitating as Mg(OH)₂ prematurely.

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A binary mixture contains **one cation from Group II and one anion**. Describe the systematic procedure you would adopt in the laboratory to identify the components. Include all key reactions.

**(b) [7 Marks)** Given a salt that is white in color, soluble in hot water, and gives a **yellow precipitate with ammonium molybdate**, identify the anion and write the confirmatory reaction. Also explain why a preliminary flame test is performed.

**Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Preliminary Examination** (1 mark): Note physical appearance (color, smell, texture). Perform flame test on a Pt-wire loop dipped in the sample + conc. HCl. Copper (Group II) typically gives a **blue-green flame**; cadmium gives a brick-red flame.

2. **Preparation of Original Solution (OS)** (1 mark): Since Group II sulfides need an acidic medium for clean separation, dissolve the mixture in **dilute HCl** to prepare the stock solution.

3. **Group II Separation** (2 marks): Pass H₂S gas through the **acidic** solution. The Group II cation precipitates as the sulfide:
$$\text{Cu}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \longrightarrow \text{CuS} \downarrow \text{(black)} + 2\text{H}^+$$
$$\text{Cd}^{2+} + \text{H}_2\text{S} \longrightarrow \text{CdS} \downarrow \text{(yellow)} + 2\text{H}^+$$

4. **Confirmatory Test for Cation** (2 marks): Dissolve the sulfide ppt in **dilute HNO₃** (or aqua regia for HgS). Add excess NH₄OH to the Cu²⁺ solution → **deep blue** $\left[\text{Cu(NH}_3)_4\right]^{2+}$ complex confirms Cu²⁺. For Cd²⁺, add KCN to mask Cu²⁺ (if any) and pass H₂S → yellow CdS.

5. **Anion Analysis** (1 mark): On a separate portion, prepare water extract (or soda extract if water-insoluble). Test with dil. H₂SO₄, BaCl₂, AgNO₃, etc., depending on the inferences from the anion elimination table.

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **Anion identified:** The **canary-yellow precipitate with ammonium molybdate** in the presence of HNO₃ confirms the **phosphate ion, $\text{PO}_4^{3-}$**. (1 mark for identification)
- **Confirmatory Reaction** (2 marks):
$$\text{Na}_3\text{PO}_4 + 12\left(\text{NH}_4\right)_2\text{MoO}_4 + 21\text{HNO}_3 \longrightarrow \underbrace{\left(\text{NH}_4\right)_3\text{PO}_4 \cdot 12\text{MoO}_3}_{\text{canary-yellow ppt}} + 21\text{NaNO}_3 + 12\text{H}_2\text{O}$$
This is known as the **ammonium phosphomolybdate** test, often called the *yellow precipitate test for phosphate*.
- **Why flame test is performed** (4 marks): The flame test is a **preliminary, non-destructive physical test** based on **atomic emission spectroscopy**. When a metal salt is heated in a Bunsen flame, thermal energy excites valence electrons to higher energy levels. Upon returning to ground state, they emit photons of characteristic wavelength:
$$\Delta E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$$
The emitted wavelength is **unique to each element**, providing a rapid "fingerprint" identification. For example, **barium gives apple-green**; **strontium gives crimson**; **calcium gives brick-red**; **copper gives blue-green**. Limitations: it cannot detect non-metals, is unreliable for mixtures of two cations emitting simultaneously, and is semi-quantitative only.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **theoretical basis** for the separation of Group II and Group IV cations using H₂S at different pH values. Derive the conditional solubility product expression and use it to justify why $\text{Cu}^{2+}$ precipitates in acidic medium while $\text{Zn}^{2+}$ does not.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A white solid mixture is partially soluble in water. The water extract gives a **white precipitate with BaCl₂** that is **insoluble in dilute HCl**, while the acid-soluble portion on flame test gives a **brick-red color**. Identify the two ions and write the formula of the salt.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Theoretical basis (3 marks):** The separation exploits the **vast difference in $K_{sp}$** between Group II sulfides ($K_{sp} \approx 10^{-22}$ to $10^{-50}$, very low) and Group IV sulfides ($K_{sp} \approx 10^{-21}$ to $10^{-28}$, relatively higher). By raising $\left[\text{H}^+\right]$, we lower $\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$ according to the equilibrium:
$$\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right] = \dfrac{K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} \cdot \left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]}{\left[\text{H}^+\right]^2}$$
At high $\left[\text{H}^+\right]$ (Group II condition, pH $\approx 0.5$), the $\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$ is too small to precipitate Group IV cations but sufficient to exceed the very low $K_{sp}$ of Group II sulfides. At low $\left[\text{H}^+\right]$ (Group IV condition, pH $\approx 9$ in NH₄OH), $\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$ is much higher, sufficient to precipitate Group IV sulfides.

**Derivation of conditional $K_{sp}$ (2 marks):** Starting from $K_{sp} = \left[\text{M}^{2+}\right]\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$ and substituting for $\left[\text{S}^{2-}\right]$:
$$K_{sp}^{\text{cond}} = \left[\text{M}^{2+}\right] \cdot \dfrac{K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} \cdot \left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]}{\left[\text{H}^+\right]^2}$$
The minimum $\left[\text{M}^{2+}\right]$ required for precipitation is:
$$\left[\text{M}^{2+}\right]_{\min} = \dfrac{K_{sp} \cdot \left[\text{H}^+\right]^2}{K_{a1} \cdot K_{a2} \cdot \left[\text{H}_2\text{S}\right]}$$

**Numerical justification (2 marks):** Using $K_{sp}(\text{CuS}) = 10^{-36}$ and $K_{sp}(\text{ZnS}) = 10^{-21}$, at $\left[\text{H}^+\right] = 0.3 \text{ M}$:
- $\left[\text{Cu}^{2+}\right]_{\min} \approx 9 \times 10^{-15} \text{ M}$ — easily exceeded in 0.01 M solution, so **CuS precipitates**.
- $\left[\text{Zn}^{2+}\right]_{\min} \approx 9 \text{ M}$ — far exceeding any practical concentration, so **ZnS does not precipitate** and remains in solution to be precipitated later as Group IV in ammoniacal medium.

**Model Solution for (b):**

- **Anion (from BaCl₂ test):** (2 marks) A white precipitate with BaCl₂ that is **insoluble in dilute HCl** is the **sulphate ion, $\text{SO}_4^{2-}$**. Confirmatory reaction:
$$\text{Ba}^{2+} + \text{SO}_4^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{BaSO}_4 \downarrow \text{(white, insoluble in acid)}$$

- **Cation (from flame test):** (2 marks) A **brick-red flame** is characteristic of the **calcium ion, Ca²⁺**. Confirmatory with ammonium oxalate: 
$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{C}_2\text{O}_4^{2-} \longrightarrow \text{CaC}_2\text{O}_4 \downarrow \text{(white, insoluble in acetic acid)}$$

- **Formula and reasoning:** (3 marks) The salt is **calcium sulphate**, $\text{CaSO}_4$. Since it is *partially soluble* in water (consistent with $\text{CaSO}_4$'s limited solubility of $\approx 2 \text{ g/L}$ at $25^{\circ}\text{C}$), the observations match perfectly. The salt could be the hemihydrate **$\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot \frac{1}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O}$** (Plaster of Paris) or the dihydrate **$\text{CaSO}_4 \cdot 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$** (Gypsum), commonly used in civil engineering.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Skipping the "water extract + acid extract" logic:** Many students make a single solution and lose marks when the salt is water-insoluble. Always prepare **two extracts** — water extract (WE) for anions that dissolve in water, and soda extract (SE) for water-insoluble salts.
> - **Confusing Group III and Group IV hydroxides:** A *white* precipitate in Group III could be Al(OH)₃ **or** a light-coloured Fe(OH)₃. Always perform the **confirmatory test** (NaOH excess, KSCN, etc.) — partial marks are not awarded for a single test.
> - **Forgetting to boil off H₂S** before proceeding from Group II to Group III: residual H₂S in solution will precipitate Group IV prematurely, causing **cross-contamination** and wrong identification. This is a top reason for full-mark deductions.
> - **Not maintaining buffer pH in Group III:** Adding excess NH₄OH without NH₄Cl causes Mg²⁺ to co-precipitate, giving a false positive.
> - **Neglecting the flame-test interference of Na⁺:** Sodium's intense yellow flame masks other colours — always view through **cobalt blue glass** to filter out the yellow.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam:**
> - The **three pillars** of binary mixture analysis are: **(1)** Preliminary (physical + dry) tests, **(2)** Group-separation cascade, and **(3)** Confirmatory spot tests.
> - **Group reagents in order:** Dil. HCl → H₂S/acid → NH₄Cl/NH₄OH → H₂S/base → (NH₄)₂CO₃ → none.
> - **Solubility product principle:** A salt precipitates when $Q > K_{sp}$ at the controlled pH.
> - **Saturated H₂S concentration** is $\approx 0.1 \text{ M}$ in aqueous solution at STP.
> - **Always prepare TWO extracts** for anion analysis: **water extract (WE)** and **soda extract (SE)** — choose based on the solubility test result.
> - **Boil off H₂S** completely (test with lead acetate paper — no blackening) before adding NH₄Cl/NH₄OH to prevent Group IV contamination.
> - **Flame-test limitations:** Masking by Na⁺ (use cobalt blue glass); interference in mixtures; non-metals not detected.
> - **Confirmatory tests beat group tests:** A black ppt in Group II is necessary but not sufficient — must add NH₄OH (for Cu) or KCN + H₂S (for Cd) for **positive identification**.
> - **Record observations systematically** in the lab record — KTU awards marks for the **observation table**, not just the final answer.
> - **Common binary mixture examples in KTU exams:** $\text{CuSO}_4 + \text{ZnSO}_4$, $\text{BaCl}_2 + \text{CaCl}_2$, $\text{FeCl}_3 + \text{AlCl}_3$, $\text{Pb(NO}_3)_2 + \text{AgNO}_3$, $\text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + (\text{NH}_4)_2\text{SO}_4$.
> - **Final answer format expected:** *"The given binary mixture contains … (cation, e.g. Cu²⁺) and … (anion, e.g. SO₄²⁻); hence the salt is … (e.g. CuSO₄·5H₂O)."*
